topics.title: Research and explain three real-world IoT applications using the components available in the IoT Trainer kit. Set up the IoT Trainer kit and understand its modules.
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Research and explain three real-world IoT applications using the components available in the IoT Trainer kit. Set up the IoT Trainer kit and understand its modules.

# Module Experiment 2: IoT Trainer Kit – Setup, Modules & Three Real-World Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an IoT Trainer Kit?

An **IoT Trainer Kit** is a pre-engineered, modular hardware–software learning platform that bundles a microcontroller, wireless communication module (Wi-Fi/BLE), a curated set of sensors and actuators, and pre-installed firmware/libraries so that students can prototype end-to-end **Internet of Things (IoT)** applications without assembling a circuit from scratch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**  
> An IoT Trainer Kit is a self-contained development platform that integrates **sensing**, **processing**, **communication**, and **actuation** subsystems, allowing the learner to implement the complete IoT reference model (Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business layers) for academic experimentation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The IoT Kit as a Human Body"

Think of the kit as a tiny robot patient in a clinic:

- **Sensors** are the *five senses* (eyes, skin, ears) — they gather information from the world.
- **Microcontroller** is the *brain* — it decides what to do with the data.
- **Wi-Fi module** is the *mouth and telephone* — it speaks to the outside world (cloud/mobile).
- **Actuators** (LED, buzzer, relay, motor) are the *hands and legs* — they physically respond.
- **Cloud dashboard** (Blynk / ThingSpeak) is the *doctor's monitor* — it shows the readings from anywhere on Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Voltage of Trainer Kits: 3.3 V / 5 V (DC).**  
> Always check jumper/switch position before powering — wrong voltage can permanently damage ESP8266/ESP32 modules which are **NOT 5 V tolerant** on GPIO pins.

### 1.3 Standard Modules Found in a KTU IoT Trainer Kit

| # | Module Type | Typical Components |
|---|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Processing Unit** | ESP8266 (NodeMCU) / ESP32 / Arduino Uno + ESP-01 |
| 2 | **Environmental Sensors** | DHT11/DHT22, LM35, DS18B20, LDR, Soil Moisture, MQ-2/MQ-135 |
| 3 | **Motion/Distance Sensors** | PIR (HC-SR501), Ultrasonic (HC-SR04), IR Obstacle |
| 4 | **Biometric Sensor** | Pulse / Heart-rate sensor (analog) |
| 5 | **Actuators** | 5 mm LED, Buzzer, Relay (5 V/230 V), SG90 Servo, DC motor + driver |
| 6 | **Display** | 16×2 LCD (with I²C backpack) / 0.96" OLED |
| 7 | **User Input** | Tactile push buttons, potentiometer |
| 8 | **Cloud/Connectivity** | Blynk, ThingSpeak, Thinger.io, MQTT (Mosquitto), Firebase |
| 9 | **Miscellaneous** | Breadboard, USB cable, jumper wires, resistors (220 Ω, 10 kΩ) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Glossary:**  
> - **NodeMCU** = ESP8266 + USB-Serial + 3.3 V regulator on a single board.  
> - **DHT11** measures 0–50 °C temperature and 20–90 % RH humidity.  
> - **MQ-2** detects LPG, propane, methane, smoke.  
> - **HC-SR04** measures 2 cm–400 cm distance with ±3 mm accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Three Real-World IoT Applications

The **IoT Architecture Reference Model (ITU-T Y.2060)** used by KTU 2024 consists of five layers. Every application below is mapped to these layers so the student can defend the "IoT-ness" of the project during the viva.

### 2.1 The Three Selected Real-World Applications

#### 🏠 Application 1 — **Smart Home Environment Monitor & Auto-Control**
A wall-mounted NodeMCU reads **temperature (DHT11)**, **humidity (DHT11)**, and **ambient light (LDR)**. The data is pushed to **Blynk / ThingSpeak** every 15 seconds. If temperature exceeds **35 °C** OR light falls below a threshold at night, the system switches a **relay-controlled fan** ON and an **LED strip** ON respectively. A **16×2 LCD** shows the local reading; the **buzzer** fires when humidity < 30 % (fire risk).

**IoT Layer Mapping**

1. **Perception Layer:** DHT11 (Temp+RH), LDR (Light)
2. **Network Layer:** ESP8266 Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz)
3. **Middleware Layer:** Blynk cloud / ThingSpeak REST API
4. **Application Layer:** Mobile dashboard (Blynk) showing real-time graphs
5. **Business Layer:** Energy saving — AC runs only when needed

**Why this is "IoT" and not just "Embedded":** The *remote* accessibility via the Internet and the *automated actuation* based on cloud-pushed thresholds.

---

#### 🌱 Application 2 — **Smart Agriculture Irrigation System**
Soil moisture sensor + DHT11 are buried in the root zone of a crop field. The **ESP8266** posts readings to **ThingSpeak** every 60 s. When volumetric water content < **30 %** (sensor analog < threshold), a **5 V relay** energises a **solar water pump** (or 12 V DC motor pump) for irrigation. The farmer also gets a **Blynk push notification** and can override via mobile.

**IoT Layer Mapping**

1. **Perception:** Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor, DHT11
2. **Network:** Wi-Fi (ESP8266 Station mode)
3. **Middleware:** ThingSpeak + IFTTT webhook
4. **Application:** Mobile app visualisation + SMS alert
5. **Business:** Water saving up to **40 %**, precision farming

**Engineering principle:** Closed-loop feedback control — *Sense → Decide → Actuate → Re-sense.*

---

#### ❤️ Application 3 — **Remote Patient Health Monitoring**
A wearable wristband houses a **pulse/heart-rate sensor** + **DS18B20 skin-temperature** sensor. The readings are streamed over Wi-Fi (or BLE for ESP32) to a **Firebase real-time database** every 5 s. A doctor sees live vitals on a web dashboard. If BPM < 50 or > 120, a **buzzer** alerts the patient and an **email/SMS** is sent via **Telegram bot** to the caregiver.

**IoT Layer Mapping**

1. **Perception:** Pulse sensor (analog), DS18B20 (1-Wire digital)
2. **Network:** ESP32 (Wi-Fi + BLE dual mode)
3. **Middleware:** Firebase RTDB / MQTT broker
4. **Application:** Web dashboard, Telegram Bot API
5. **Business:** Reduced ICU visits, telemedicine compliance

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Common Trainer Kit Modules

| Module | Pin Type | Operating Voltage | Communication Protocol | Typical Use in Lab |
|--------|----------|-------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| ESP8266 NodeMCU | Digital + Analog (A0) | **3.3 V** | Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n | Main controller |
| DHT11 | Digital single-wire | 3 – 5.5 V | One-Wire (proprietary) | Temp & humidity |
| HC-SR04 | Digital (Trig/Echo) | 5 V | GPIO timing pulse | Distance 2–400 cm |
| MQ-2 | Analog + Digital | 5 V | ADC / threshold | Gas/smoke |
| LDR | Analog | 3.3 – 5 V | Voltage divider | Light intensity |
| Soil Moisture | Analog + Digital | 3.3 – 5 V | ADC | Soil water % |
| Relay Module | Digital control | 3.3 V logic, 250 V/10 A | GPIO | Switch AC appliances |
| 16×2 LCD (I²C) | I²C (SDA/SCL) | 5 V | I²C (addr 0x27) | Local display |
| Servo SG90 | PWM | 5 V (separate recommended) | 50 Hz PWM | Angle control |
| Pulse Sensor | Analog | 3.3 – 5 V | ADC | BPM (60–100 normal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "3.3 V vs 5 V" Pitfall:** ESP8266 GPIO pins are **NOT 5 V tolerant**. When interfacing a 5 V sensor (HC-SR04, MQ-2), use a **voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ)** on the output line to ESP8266. This is the single most common hardware failure in KTU labs.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Three Applications

| Application | Engineering Discipline | Industry Use |
|-------------|------------------------|--------------|
| Smart Home | Home automation, HVAC, Energy management | Google Nest, Amazon Alexa routines |
| Smart Agriculture | AgriTech, IoT, Environmental engg. | John Deere See & Spray, Netafim |
| Health Monitoring | Biomedical engg., Telemedicine | Apple Watch ECG, Philips remote patient monitoring |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Setup Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Unboxing & First-Power-Up (Exhaustive Procedure)

1. **Inspect the kit** — verify presence of NodeMCU, sensors, USB cable, breadboard, jumper wires, and the kit manual.  
2. **Install the Arduino IDE** (v1.8.19 or v2.x) from the official site.  
3. **Add ESP8266 Board URL:**  
   `File → Preferences → Additional Board Manager URLs → http://arduino.esp8266.com/stable/package_esp8266com_index.json`  
4. **Install Board:** Tools → Board → Boards Manager → search "esp8266" → Install.  
5. **Select Board:** Tools → Board → NodeMCU 1.0 (ESP-12E Module).  
6. **Select Port:** Tools → Port → COMx (Windows) or /dev/ttyUSB0 (Linux).  
7. **Install required libraries** (Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries):  
   - `DHT sensor library` by Adafruit  
   - `Adafruit Unified Sensor`  
   - `LiquidCrystal_I2C`  
   - `BlynkSimpleEsp8266`  
   - `ESP8266WiFi`  
   - `ThingSpeak`  
   - `Servo.h` (built-in)  
8. **Connect NodeMCU via USB** — the onboard **blue LED** should glow and Windows should detect "CP2102 / CH340" USB-UART bridge.  
9. **Test Blink:** Upload File → Examples → Basics → Blink, change LED_BUILTIN pin.  
10. **Wi-Fi test:** Use `WiFiScan` example to confirm the on-board antenna is functional.

### 3.2 Pin-Mapping Matrix (Standard Trainer Kit Convention)

| Trainer Kit Module | NodeMCU Pin (D-number) | GPIO Number | Notes |
|--------------------|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| DHT11 DATA | D2 | GPIO4 | Pull-up 10 kΩ to 3.3 V |
| LDR (voltage divider) | A0 | ADC0 | 0–3.3 V analog range |
| Soil Moisture AO | A0 (multiplexed) | ADC0 | Use different pin if combined |
| MQ-2 AO | D5 (A0 if free) | GPIO14 / ADC | Heater needs 5 V |
| HC-SR04 TRIG | D1 | GPIO5 | Output, safe 3.3 V |
| HC-SR04 ECHO | D2 via divider | GPIO4 | Use 1 kΩ+2 kΩ divider |
| Relay IN | D6 | GPIO12 | Active LOW usually |
| Buzzer | D8 | GPIO15 | Active HIGH |
| LED | D0 | GPIO16 | Use 220 Ω series |
| Servo Signal | D4 | GPIO2 | 5 V power rail separate |
| LCD I²C SDA | D2 (SDA) | GPIO4 | Share with I²C devices |
| LCD I²C SCL | D1 (SCL) | GPIO5 | Share with I²C devices |
| Push Button | D3 | GPIO0 | Pull-down 10 kΩ to GND |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GPIO numbers differ from D-numbers:** Arduino IDE uses a *board pin map*. D2 on the silkscreen = GPIO4 on the ESP8266 die. This confuses beginners — keep this matrix in your record file.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Reading DHT11 Using Single-Wire Protocol

The DHT11 uses a proprietary **single-wire bidirectional** protocol. The complete timing derivation for a successful read is:

$$
T_{total} = T_{host\_start} + T_{sensor\_response} + T_{data\_bits}
$$

**Step 1 — Host pull-down start signal:**

$$
T_{host\_start} \geq 18\ \text{ms} \quad (\text{MCU drives DATA LOW})
$$

**Step 2 — Host releases line; pull-up brings HIGH; sensor responds:**

$$
T_{sensor\_response} = 80\ \mu s\ \text{LOW} + 80\ \mu s\ \text{HIGH}
$$

**Step 3 — 40 data bits = 5 bytes** (Humidity_int, Humidity_dec, Temp_int, Temp_dec, Checksum). Each bit begins with **50 µs LOW**, then:

$$
T_{bit} = 50\ \mu s + T_{HIGH}
$$

$$
\text{Bit Value} = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } T_{HIGH} \approx 27\ \mu s \\ 1, & \text{if } T_{HIGH} \approx 70\ \mu s \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Checksum verification:**

$$
\text{Checksum} = (H_{int} + H_{dec} + T_{int} + T_{dec}) \mod 256
$$

If the received byte 5 equals the computed checksum, the reading is **valid**. This is the standard KTU viva question: *"How does the MCU know DHT11 data is correct?"* — answer: parity checksum.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Choosing Each Application's Sensor

| Decision Parameter | Smart Home (App-1) | Smart Agri (App-2) | Health (App-3) |
|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Sampling rate | 15 s | 60 s | 5 s |
| Power source | USB 5 V | Solar + 18650 Li-ion | Rechargeable Li-Po |
| Wireless protocol | Wi-Fi (ESP8266) | Wi-Fi (NodeMCU) | BLE + Wi-Fi (ESP32) |
| Critical threshold | T > 35 °C | Soil < 30 % | BPM ∉ [50, 120] |
| Actuator | Relay (Fan), LED | Relay (Pump) | Buzzer |
| Cloud platform | Blynk | ThingSpeak | Firebase |
| Latency tolerance | 30 s | 5 min | < 2 s (life-critical) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic IoT Trainer Kit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World: Temp, Humidity, Light, Gas, Distance, BPM] --> B[Sensor Layer: DHT11, LDR, MQ2, HCSR04, Pulse]
    B --> C[Processing Layer: NodeMCU ESP8266]
    C --> D[Actuator Layer: Relay, LED, Buzzer, Servo, DC Motor]
    C --> E[WiFi 802.11 b/g/n 2.4 GHz]
    E --> F[Cloud Middleware: Blynk or ThingSpeak or Firebase]
    F --> G[Application Layer: Mobile App, Web Dashboard, Telegram Bot]
    G --> H[User: Read, Threshold Tune, Override]
    H --> G
    G --> F
    F --> C
    C --> D
    D --> I[Physical Action: Fan ON, Pump ON, Alarm]
```

### 4.2 Smart Home — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[NodeMCU Boot] --> A2[Connect to WiFi SSID]
    A2 --> A3{Connection OK?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Blink LED 3 times, halt]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Read DHT11 Temp and RH]
    A5 --> A6[Read LDR via ADC]
    A6 --> A7[Push to Blynk Cloud every 15 s]
    A7 --> A8{Temp > 35 deg C?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Activate Relay: Fan ON]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Relay OFF]
    A9 --> A11[Show values on 16x2 LCD]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A5
```

### 4.3 Smart Agriculture — Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[NodeMCU wakes] --> B2[Read Soil Moisture AO]
    B2 --> B3[Read DHT11]
    B3 --> B4[Upload to ThingSpeak Field 1 and 2]
    B4 --> B5{Moisture less than 30 percent}
    B5 -- Yes --> B6[Relay HIGH: Pump ON for 10 s]
    B5 -- No --> B7[Pump OFF]
    B6 --> B8[Send Blynk Notification to farmer]
    B8 --> B9[Wait 60 s]
    B7 --> B9
    B9 --> B1
```

### 4.4 Health Monitoring — Real-Time Streaming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[ESP32 on wrist] --> C2[Pulse Sensor Analog]
    C1 --> C3[DS18B20 One Wire Temp]
    C2 --> C4[Compute BPM via 5 s window]
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5[Push to Firebase RTDB]
    C5 --> C6[Web Dashboard Live]
    C5 --> C7{BPM out of 50 to 120}
    C7 -- Yes --> C8[Buzzer ON + Telegram Alert]
    C7 -- No --> C9[Normal Continue]
    C8 --> C10[Caregiver receives SMS]
    C9 --> C1
```

### 4.5 Pin Connection Schematic (Trainer Kit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NodeMCU
        N1[NodeMCU ESP8266]
    end
    subgraph Sensors
        S1[DHT11 to D2]
        S2[LDR to A0]
        S3[Soil Moisture to D3]
        S4[MQ2 AO to A0 via MUX]
    end
    subgraph Actuators
        AC1[Relay to D6]
        AC2[Buzzer to D8]
        AC3[LED to D0]
        AC4[Servo to D4]
    end
    subgraph Display
        DPY1[LCD I2C SDA D2]
        DPY2[LCD I2C SCL D1]
    end
    N1 --> S1
    N1 --> S2
    N1 --> S3
    N1 --> S4
    N1 --> AC1
    N1 --> AC2
    N1 --> AC3
    N1 --> AC4
    N1 --> DPY1
    N1 --> DPY2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
List any four sensors and two actuators that are commonly part of an IoT Trainer kit.

**Model Answer:**  
*Four sensors:* DHT11 (temperature & humidity), HC-SR04 (ultrasonic distance), MQ-2 (gas/smoke), Soil moisture sensor.  
*Two actuators:* 5 V Relay module and DC gear motor / buzzer.  
*(Any four sensors + any two actuators = 3 marks: 1 + 2 distribution.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**  
Why is ESP8266 not 5 V tolerant on its GPIO pins, and how is this handled in the trainer kit?

**Model Answer:**  
The ESP8266 chip is fabricated in a 40 nm CMOS process with a **3.3 V** I/O ring; applying 5 V to any GPIO causes **latch-up** and permanent damage. In the trainer kit, 5 V sensor outputs (e.g., HC-SR04 Echo) are stepped down using a **resistor voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ)** before reaching the GPIO. *(2 marks for the reason + 1 mark for the divider solution.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an IoT Trainer kit with a neat block diagram. List the roles of the perception, network, middleware, and application layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain how a Smart Agriculture Irrigation System can be implemented using a soil moisture sensor, DHT11, NodeMCU, relay, and ThingSpeak. State at least two engineering benefits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**  
The IoT Trainer kit follows the **ITU-T Y.2060 reference model**.

- **Perception Layer** — sensors (DHT11, LDR, MQ-2, soil, ultrasonic, pulse) convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.  
- **Network/Transport Layer** — NodeMCU's ESP8266 uses **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n** at **2.4 GHz** with **TCP/IP + MQTT** to send data.  
- **Middleware Layer** — Blynk/ThingSpeak/Firebase provides storage, analytics, and threshold engines.  
- **Application Layer** — mobile/web dashboards let users visualise and act.  
- **Business Layer** — energy/water saving, predictive maintenance.

**Block diagram** — see Section 4.1 above (draw in record). *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 distribution.)*

**(b)**  
- The **soil moisture sensor** outputs an analog voltage (0–3.3 V) inversely proportional to moisture.  
- Threshold set at **30 % VWC** → analog value < **1.0 V** (calibrated).  
- **DHT11** gives air temperature & humidity for crop-climate context.  
- **NodeMCU** reads both sensors every 60 s, posts to **ThingSpeak** field 1 and 2.  
- When soil < 30 %, the code drives **GPIO D6 HIGH** → relay coil energised → **pump contactor closes** → water flows.  
- After 10 s, the relay turns off (anti-overflow).  
- **Blynk notification** "Pump ON" sent to the farmer's phone.  
- **Engineering benefits:** (i) water saving up to **40 %**, (ii) labour saving, (iii) data-driven precision farming. *(7 marks: 2 architecture + 3 flow + 2 benefits.)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Describe the procedure to set up the IoT Trainer kit — from unboxing to running the "Blink" example on the NodeMCU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain a **Remote Patient Health Monitoring System** using a pulse sensor, DS18B20, ESP32, Firebase, and a Telegram bot. Include the BPM threshold logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**  
1. Verify components in the kit.  
2. Install **Arduino IDE 1.8.19/2.x** and the **CP2102/CH340** USB driver.  
3. Add `http://arduino.esp8266.com/...` in *Preferences → Additional Board URLs*.  
4. Install `esp8266` from Boards Manager.  
5. Select **NodeMCU 1.0 (ESP-12E)** and the correct **COM port**.  
6. Open `File → Examples → Basics → Blink`; change pin to `LED_BUILTIN` (=D0/GPIO16 for NodeMCU).  
7. Click **Upload**; the on-board blue LED blinks at 1 s interval — confirms MCU + USB-UART + IDE toolchain are functional. *(7 marks: 1+1+1+1+1+1+1 step-wise.)*

**(b)**  
- The **pulse sensor** amplifies photoplethysmography (PPG) signal; BPM is computed by averaging inter-beat intervals over a **5-s sliding window**.  
- **DS18B20** gives skin temperature with **±0.5 °C** accuracy over 1-Wire.  
- **ESP32** reads both, posts to **Firebase RTDB** every **5 s**.  
- A static web dashboard fetches Firebase using `firebaseio.com` REST.  
- **Threshold logic** in code: `if (BPM < 50 || BPM > 120) → buzzerHIGH(); bot.sendMessage("Critical BPM: " + bpm);`  
- The **Telegram bot** uses `https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/sendMessage` HTTP GET.  
- Benefits: enables 24/7 remote monitoring, reduces hospital readmissions. *(7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**  
> 1. **Writing "Arduino" instead of "ESP8266/NodeMCU"** in the controller section — the examiner wants the Wi-Fi-enabled MCU name, not just "Arduino".  
> 2. **Forgetting the cloud platform name** (Blynk/ThingSpeak) — saying only "data is sent to the internet" loses 1–2 marks.  
> 3. **Drawing a block diagram without sensor names** — every box in the perception layer must specify the *actual sensor* (DHT11, not just "temperature sensor").  
> 4. **Confusing GPIO numbers with D-numbers** in the pin map — leads to wrong wiring in the practical exam.  
> 5. **Skipping the "why it is IoT" justification** — every application must mention the *Internet + remote monitoring + automated actuation* triple.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **IoT Trainer Kit = Perception + Processing + Network + Middleware + Application layers.**  
- **Standard MCU = NodeMCU (ESP8266, 3.3 V logic, Wi-Fi built-in).**  
- **Common sensors** = DHT11, LDR, MQ-2, HC-SR04, Soil Moisture, Pulse.  
- **Common actuators** = Relay, LED, Buzzer, Servo, DC motor.  
- **Voltage rule:** ESP8266 = 3.3 V only; use voltage divider for 5 V sensor outputs.  
- **DHT11 uses single-wire protocol**; 40-bit frame; 50 µs LOW + 27 µs/70 µs HIGH = 0/1.  
- **Three reference applications:**  
  1. **Smart Home** — DHT11 + LDR → Blynk → Relay/LED  
  2. **Smart Agriculture** — Soil + DHT11 → ThingSpeak → Pump  
  3. **Health Monitoring** — Pulse + DS18B20 → Firebase/Telegram → Buzzer  
- **Cloud platforms taught in KTU 2024:** Blynk, ThingSpeak, Thinger.io, Firebase, MQTT.  
- **Sampling intervals** differ by criticality — 5 s (health) > 15 s (home) > 60 s (agri).  
- **Checksum verification** is the only way DHT11 confirms data integrity.  
- **Block diagrams in record** must show **five-layer IoT architecture**, not just a circuit.  
- **Examiner's favourite viva question:** *"Why is your project an IoT system and not merely an embedded system?"*  
  **Answer template:** Because data is **transmitted over the Internet** to a **cloud server**, **monitored remotely** on a mobile dashboard, and **actuation is triggered** by thresholds set in the **cloud** — meeting all three criteria of the ITU-T Y.2060 IoT definition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use the temperature and humidity sensor module in the IoT Trainer kit to collect environmental data. Write a script to display the readings on the provided interface.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use the temperature and humidity sensor module in the IoT Trainer kit to collect environmental data. Write a script to display the readings on the provided interface.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Data Acquisition in IoT:** Environmental data acquisition using a **Temperature and Humidity Sensor Module (DHT11/DHT22)** refers to the process of sensing ambient atmospheric parameters through a **single-wire digital interface**, conditioning the raw signals, and transmitting the calibrated data to a microcontroller (typically **ESP32/NodeMCU**) for real-time visualization on a user interface (LCD, Serial Monitor, ThingSpeak, or Blynk Dashboard).

The **DHT11/DHT22** is a digital signal-output sensor that combines a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** for temperature measurement and a **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** for relative humidity (RH) measurement. It outputs a calibrated digital signal on a single data pin using a proprietary 1-wire protocol.

### KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

| **CO (Course Outcome)** | **CO Description** | **BT Level** |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Implement IoT data acquisition and actuation using appropriate sensors and actuators | **BT3 (Apply)** |
| **CO3** | Develop IoT applications using cloud platforms and communication protocols | **BT3 (Apply)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **BT Level (Bloom's Taxonomy):** This experiment maps to **BT3 (Apply)** as students must implement a working script and verify sensor output, and partially to **BT4 (Analyze)** while interpreting the digital signal timing.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🍎 The "Human Skin" Analogy
Imagine you walk outside and your **skin** tells you two things: how **hot** it is (temperature) and how **sticky/moist** the air feels (humidity). The **DHT sensor is the "electronic skin"** of your IoT project.

- **The Thermistor** acts like your body's nerve endings sensing heat — as temperature rises, its electrical resistance drops (that's why it's called "Negative Temperature Coefficient").
- **The Capacitive Humidity Sensor** works like a tiny sponge that absorbs moisture from the air — when the air is humid, the sponge's ability to store electrical charge (capacitance) changes.
- **The Built-in Microcontroller inside the DHT** is the "translator" — it reads these changes and converts them into a digital **binary code** that your ESP32/NodeMCU can understand via just **ONE wire**.

### Key Performance Metrics

| **Parameter** | **DHT11** | **DHT22 (AM2302)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature Range** | **0 °C to 50 °C** | **-40 °C to 80 °C** |
| **Temperature Accuracy** | **±2 °C** | **±0.5 °C** |
| **Humidity Range** | **20% to 90% RH** | **0% to 100% RH** |
| **Humidity Accuracy** | **±5% RH** | **±2–5% RH** |
| **Operating Voltage** | **3 V to 5.5 V** | **3 V to 5.5 V** |
| **Sampling Rate** | **1 Hz (1 reading/sec)** | **0.5 Hz (1 reading/2 sec)** |
| **Resolution** | **8-bit (Temp), 8-bit (RH)** | **16-bit (Temp), 16-bit (RH)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The **Relative Humidity (RH)** is the ratio (in percentage) of the actual water vapor in the air to the maximum water vapor the air can hold at that temperature. **0% RH = Bone Dry, 100% RH = Saturated Air (Fog).**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle

The DHT sensor operates through **three internal stages** during one measurement cycle:

### Step 1: MCU Request (Start Signal)
The host microcontroller (ESP32) pulls the data line **LOW for ≥18 ms**, then releases it. This wakes up the DHT's internal ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit).

### Step 2: DHT Response (Presence Pulse)
The DHT sensor acknowledges by pulling the line **LOW for 80 µs** and then **HIGH for 80 µs**. This handshake confirms the sensor is alive and ready.

### Step 3: Data Transmission (40 Bits)
The DHT transmits **40 bits (5 bytes)** of data:
- **Byte 1:** Humidity integer part (RH_int)
- **Byte 2:** Humidity decimal part (RH_dec)
- **Byte 3:** Temperature integer part (T_int)
- **Byte 4:** Temperature decimal part (T_dec)
- **Byte 5:** Checksum = (Byte1 + Byte2 + Byte3 + Byte4) & 0xFF

Each bit is encoded as a **pulse-width modulated signal**:
- **Bit '0'**: 50 µs LOW + 26–28 µs HIGH
- **Bit '1'**: 50 µs LOW + 70 µs HIGH

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Humidity (RH)** | $RH = \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100\%$ | Quantifies moisture content relative to saturation |
| **Dew Point (Magnus Formula)** | $\gamma(T, RH) = \dfrac{\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}}{a - \dfrac{\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}}{b + T}}$ | Predicts condensation temperature |
| **Heat Index (Rothfusz)** | $HI = -42.379 + 2.049 \cdot T + 10.143 \cdot RH - 0.225 \cdot T \cdot RH$ | "Feels like" temperature for human comfort |
| **Absolute Humidity (g/m³)** | $AH = 216.7 \cdot \dfrac{RH/100 \cdot 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.62T}{243.12+T}}}{273.15 + T}$ | Mass of water per unit volume |
| **Steinhart-Hart (NTC Thermistor)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot (\ln(R))^3$ | Internal conversion of resistance to temperature |
| **Checksum Verification** | $Checksum = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \bmod 256$ | Data integrity validation |
| **Saturated Vapor Pressure (hPa)** | $E(T) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{17.62T}{243.12+T}\right)}$ | Used in Magnus & AH formulas |

> **Constants:** $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$ °C (Magnus coefficients valid for $0 \le T \le 60$ °C, $RH > 0$)

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dew Point from RH and Temperature (Magnus Form)

The **Dew Point (γ)** is the temperature at which water vapor in the air becomes saturated and condenses into liquid. It is derived from the Clausius-Clapeyron relation, simplified via the Magnus approximation.

### Given
- $T$ = Ambient temperature (°C)
- $RH$ = Relative Humidity (%)
- Magnus coefficients: $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$ °C

### Derivation

The saturation vapor pressure at temperature $T$:

$$
E(T) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{a \cdot T}{b + T}\right)}
$$

The actual vapor pressure (partial pressure) is:

$$
P_{act} = \dfrac{RH}{100} \cdot E(T)
$$

Setting $P_{act}$ equal to the saturation pressure at dew point $\gamma$:

$$
6.112 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{a \cdot \gamma}{b + \gamma}\right)} = \dfrac{RH}{100} \cdot 6.112 \cdot e^{\left(\frac{a \cdot T}{b + T}\right)}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
\dfrac{a \cdot \gamma}{b + \gamma} = \ln\left(\dfrac{RH}{100}\right) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}
$$

Solving for $\gamma$ algebraically (cross-multiplication):

$$
a \cdot \gamma = (b + \gamma) \cdot \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{RH}{100}\right) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}\right]
$$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{b \cdot \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{RH}{100}\right) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}\right]}{a - \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{RH}{100}\right) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}\right]}
$$

> **Final Closed-Form Equation (Dew Point):**
> $$\gamma = \dfrac{b \cdot \alpha}{a - \alpha}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \ln\left(\dfrac{RH}{100}\right) + \dfrac{a \cdot T}{b + T}$$

### Numerical Example
**Given:** $T = 30$ °C, $RH = 70\%$

**Step 1:** Compute $\alpha$

$$
\alpha = \ln(0.70) + \dfrac{17.625 \times 30}{243.04 + 30}
$$

$$
\alpha = -0.3567 + \dfrac{528.75}{273.04} = -0.3567 + 1.9364 = 1.5797
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\gamma$

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{243.04 \times 1.5797}{17.625 - 1.5797} = \dfrac{383.97}{16.0453} \approx 23.93\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Interpretation:** When air at 30 °C and 70% RH is cooled to **≈ 23.93 °C**, dew will begin to form.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Data Frame Decoding

The DHT transmits **40 bits** in LSB-first order. Below is a sample binary stream with explicit decoding:

| **Byte #** | **Bit Pattern (MSB → LSB)** | **Decimal** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Byte 1** | `0000 0010` | 2 | RH_integer = 2 (tens digit not used → means **50.x%**) |
| **Byte 2** | `1001 0010` | 146 | RH_decimal = 146 → 0.146 (fractional) |
| **Byte 3** | `0000 0001` | 1 | T_integer = 1 (means **20.x °C**) |
| **Byte 4** | `0100 0010` | 66 | T_decimal = 0.66 |
| **Byte 5** | `1010 1010` | 170 | **Checksum Verification** |

**Checksum Check:**

$$
(2 + 146 + 1 + 66) \bmod 256 = 215 \bmod 256 = 215
$$

Since $215 \neq 170$, the reading is **CORRUPTED** and must be discarded (sensor libraries perform this check automatically).

**Final Decoded Values (if valid):**

$$
RH = 2 \times 10 + 0.146 \times 10 = 21.46\%
$$

$$
T = 1 \times 10 + 0.66 = 10.66\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** For DHT11, the integer byte is the **tens digit**, and the decimal byte is the **ones decimal place**. For DHT22, both bytes are direct binary values.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What is the purpose of the checksum byte in the DHT sensor data frame? Show how validation is performed.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The 5th byte (checksum) in the 40-bit DHT data frame ensures **data integrity**. The 8-bit sum of the first four bytes must equal the checksum byte for the reading to be valid.

**Validation Equation:**

$$
\text{Checksum} = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \bmod 256
$$

If the computed sum does not match the received checksum byte, the reading is **discarded** to avoid transmitting corrupted environmental data, which is critical in **medical or industrial IoT systems**.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Write a MicroPython script to read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 sensor connected to GPIO 4 of an ESP32, and display the values on a 16x2 I²C LCD every 2 seconds. Compute and display the Dew Point also.**

#### **Model Solution:**

```python
# MicroPython Script: DHT11 with I2C LCD on ESP32
# Library: dht and machine, I2C LCD using machine.I2C

from machine import Pin, I2C
from dht import DHT11
from time import sleep

# ---------- I2C LCD Class (PCF8574-based) ----------
class LCD_I2C:
    def __init__(self, addr=0x27, scl=22, sda=21, freq=100000):
        self.i2c = I2C(0, scl=Pin(scl), sda=Pin(sda), freq=freq)
        self.addr = addr
        self.LCD_ADDR = addr
        self.ENABLE = 0x04
        self.BACKLIGHT = 0x08
        self._init_display()

    def _lcd_write(self, data, mode=0):
        high = data & 0xF0
        low = (data << 4) & 0xF0
        for byte in (high | self.BACKLIGHT, low | self.BACKLIGHT,
                     high | self.BACKLIGHT | self.ENABLE, low | self.BACKLIGHT | self.ENABLE):
            self.i2c.writeto(self.LCD_ADDR, bytes([byte | self.BACKLIGHT]))
            sleep(0.001)

    def _init_display(self):
        sleep(0.05)
        self._lcd_write(0x33)
        self._lcd_write(0x32)
        self._lcd_write(0x28)  # 4-bit, 2-line, 5x8 font
        self._lcd_write(0x0C)  # Display ON, cursor OFF
        self._lcd_write(0x06)  # Entry mode
        self._lcd_write(0x01)  # Clear display
        sleep(0.002)

    def clear(self):
        self._lcd_write(0x01)
        sleep(0.002)

    def write(self, text, line=1):
        cmd = 0x80 if line == 1 else 0xC0
        self._lcd_write(cmd)
        for ch in text:
            self._lcd_write(ord(ch), mode=1)

# ---------- DHT11 Sensor Initialization ----------
dht_pin = Pin(4, Pin.IN, Pin.PULL_UP)
sensor = DHT11(dht_pin)

# ---------- LCD Initialization ----------
lcd = LCD_I2C()
lcd.write("IoT Env Monitor", 1)
lcd.write("Initializing...", 2)
sleep(2)

# ---------- Main Loop ----------
while True:
    try:
        sensor.measure()
        temp = sensor.temperature()    # °C
        hum  = sensor.humidity()       # % RH

        # Magnus Dew Point Calculation
        a, b = 17.625, 243.04
        alpha = (a * temp) / (b + temp) + __import__('math').log(hum / 100.0)
        dew   = (b * alpha) / (a - alpha)

        # Display on LCD
        lcd.clear()
        lcd.write("T:{:.1f}C  H:{}%".format(temp, int(hum)), 1)
        lcd.write("DewPt:{:.1f}C".format(dew), 2)

        # Print to Serial Monitor for debugging
        print("Temperature: {:.1f} °C".format(temp))
        print("Humidity:    {}%".format(hum))
        print("Dew Point:   {:.1f} °C".format(dew))
        print("-" * 30)

    except OSError as e:
        lcd.clear()
        lcd.write("Sensor Error!", 1)
        lcd.write("Check Wiring", 2)
        print("Sensor read failed:", e)

    sleep(2)
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (Distributed across 7 marks):**
1. **Import statements & sensor init (1 mark):** `from dht import DHT11`, `Pin(4)`.
2. **I²C LCD class definition (2 marks):** PCF8574 abstraction for 4-bit mode.
3. **`sensor.measure()` & `.temperature()`/`.humidity()` (1 mark):** Trigger and read.
4. **Magnus Dew Point formula (2 marks):** Ln() and arithmetic shown.
5. **LCD display + 2-second loop + error handling (1 mark):** Robustness.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Magnus-Tetens formula for Dew Point. Derive the closed-form expression and calculate the Dew Point for $T = 35$ °C and $RH = 85\%$.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Saturation vapor pressure at temperature $T$:

$$
E(T) = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{aT}{b+T}}, \quad a = 17.625,\ b = 243.04
$$

**Step 2:** Actual vapor pressure $P_{act} = \dfrac{RH}{100} \cdot E(T)$.

**Step 3:** Set $E(\gamma) = P_{act}$ and solve for $\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{b \cdot \left[\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{aT}{b+T}\right]}{a - \left[\ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{aT}{b+T}\right]}
$$

**Numerical Computation** for $T = 35$ °C, $RH = 85\%$:

**Step A:** $\dfrac{aT}{b+T} = \dfrac{17.625 \times 35}{243.04 + 35} = \dfrac{616.875}{278.04} = 2.2185$

**Step B:** $\ln(0.85) = -0.1625$

**Step C:** $\alpha = -0.1625 + 2.2185 = 2.0560$

**Step D:** $\gamma = \dfrac{243.04 \times 2.0560}{17.625 - 2.0560} = \dfrac{499.69}{15.569} \approx 32.10\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Result:** Dew Point = **32.10 °C** (extremely humid; condensation likely on AC vents).

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Write an Arduino (C/C++) sketch to read DHT11 values on D4 of NodeMCU, publish the data to ThingSpeak every 15 seconds, and display on Serial Monitor with proper error handling.**

#### **Model Solution:**

```cpp
#include <DHT.h>
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <ThingSpeak.h>

#define DHTPIN   D4          // GPIO2 on NodeMCU
#define DHTTYPE  DHT11
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);

const char* ssid     = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* password = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";

unsigned long channelID = 123456;          // ThingSpeak Channel ID
const char* apiKey      = "YOUR_API_KEY";

WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(115200);
    dht.begin();

    WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
    Serial.print("Connecting to Wi-Fi");
    while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
        delay(500);
        Serial.print(".");
    }
    Serial.println("\nConnected!");

    ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
    float h = dht.readHumidity();
    float t = dht.readTemperature();   // Celsius

    if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) {
        Serial.println("Sensor read failed! Check wiring.");
        delay(2000);
        return;
    }

    // Display on Serial Monitor
    Serial.print("Temperature: "); Serial.print(t); Serial.println(" *C");
    Serial.print("Humidity:    "); Serial.print(h); Serial.println(" %");

    // Publish to ThingSpeak
    ThingSpeak.setField(1, t);
    ThingSpeak.setField(2, h);
    int status = ThingSpeak.writeFields(channelID, apiKey);

    if (status == 200) {
        Serial.println("Data uploaded successfully to ThingSpeak.");
    } else {
        Serial.println("Upload failed. HTTP code: " + String(status));
    }

    delay(15000);   // 15-second interval (ThingSpeak minimum)
}
```

**Explanation of Key Sections (Distributed across 7 marks):**
1. **Library inclusion & DHT object creation (1 mark):** `#include <DHT.h>`.
2. **Wi-Fi initialization with `WiFi.begin()` (1 mark):** Connection state handling.
3. **`readTemperature()` and `readHumidity()` (1 mark):** Acquiring raw values.
4. **`isnan()` check for error handling (1 mark):** Prevents NaN propagation.
5. **`ThingSpeak.setField()` and `writeFields()` (2 marks):** Cloud publishing.
6. **15-second delay and serial print formatting (1 mark):** Output format and timing compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing Checksum Verification:** Forgetting to validate Byte 5 leads to corrupted data being displayed. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong Pull-up:** Connecting the DATA pin without a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor causes `OSError: sensor not found`. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Insufficient Delay:** Calling `measure()` faster than 1 s on DHT11 or 2 s on DHT22 returns stale/invalid data. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Using 5V on ESP32 GPIO:** ESP32 GPIOs are **NOT 5V tolerant**; always use **3.3V** or a level shifter. (-2 marks for hardware explanation)
> 5. **Not handling `OSError`/`isnan()`:** A single failed reading crashes the loop and incurs heavy deductions. (-1 mark)
> 6. **ThingSpeak minimum interval is 15 seconds:** Publishing faster results in HTTP 400 errors. (-1 mark)

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **DHT11** is 8-bit, cheap, slow, less accurate; **DHT22** is 16-bit, wider range, higher accuracy.
- ✅ The sensor uses a **single-wire bidirectional protocol** with a **mandatory 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** on the DATA line.
- ✅ Each **DHT data frame is 40 bits long**: `[RH_int, RH_dec, T_int, T_dec, Checksum]`.
- ✅ **Bit encoding:** 50 µs LOW + 26–28 µs HIGH = '0'; 50 µs LOW + 70 µs HIGH = '1'.
- ✅ **Checksum Equation:** $(B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \bmod 256 = B_5$ for valid data.
- ✅ **Minimum sample interval:** 1 s for DHT11, **2 s for DHT22** — never poll faster.
- ✅ **Dew Point** uses the **Magnus-Tetens approximation** with constants $a = 17.625$, $b = 243.04$.
- ✅ **Heat Index (Rothfusz)** provides the "feels like" temperature; valid only for $T \geq 27$ °C and $RH \geq 40\%$.
- ✅ **ESP32 GPIOs are 3.3V only** — never apply 5V to a GPIO pin.
- ✅ **ThingSpeak minimum update period** is **15 seconds**; faster posts return HTTP 400.
- ✅ Standard **I²C LCD address** is **0x27** (some modules use **0x3F**); SDA = GPIO 21, SCL = GPIO 22 on ESP32.
- ✅ The **AM2302** is the same sensor as DHT22 but in a packaged form with cable.
- ✅ Use **`isnan()`** in Arduino or **`try-except OSError`** in MicroPython for robust error handling.
- ✅ **Operating principle:** NTC thermistor (temperature) + capacitive polymer (humidity), digitized by internal ASIC.
- ✅ **KTU Board Focus:** Expect questions on pinout, protocol timing, script debugging, and Magnus dew-point derivation (7–10 marks frequency).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface the light and sound sensors from the IoT Trainer kit. Write a script to capture data and trigger actions, such as lighting an LED when the sound level exceeds a threshold.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interface the light and sound sensors from the IoT Trainer kit. Write a script to capture data and trigger actions, such as lighting an LED when the sound level exceeds a threshold.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sensor Interfacing** in an IoT context refers to the hardware–software integration of an analog or digital sensing element (transducer) with a microcontroller development board (e.g., ESP32, NodeMCU, Arduino Uno, or the proprietary IoT Trainer Kit) such that the physical environmental variable is converted into a measurable electrical signal, processed by the MCU's **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and used to drive a **conditional actuator response** through firmware logic.

For this experiment, two sensing modalities are exercised:

| Sensor Type | Transducer Element | Physical Variable | Output Nature |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Light Sensor** | **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** or **Photodiode** (e.g., TEMT6000, GL5528) | Ambient Light Intensity (lux) | Analog Voltage |
| **Sound Sensor** | **Electret Condenser Microphone** with LM393 comparator | Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL proxy) | Analog + Digital (threshold pin) |
| **Actuator** | **LED** (Light Emitting Diode) | Visual Indicator | Digital HIGH/LOW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *Sensor Interfacing* is the act of mapping a physical quantity to a digital representation through signal conditioning, ADC conversion, and GPIO manipulation, followed by event-driven actuation based on a **threshold comparator logic**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Security Guard"

Imagine your body has two natural sensors:
- **Eyes** → detect **light** (photoreceptors on the retina)
- **Ears** → detect **sound** (hair cells in the cochlea)

Your **brain** acts as the microcontroller — it continuously reads the signals and decides an action. If your ears hear a loud bang (sound > threshold), the brain triggers your eyes to scan the environment, or makes you jump — analogous to an **LED turning ON** when sound crosses a threshold.

In the IoT Trainer Kit, the **microphone sensor** is your "ear", the **LDR** is your "eye", the **ESP32/NodeMCU** is your "brain", and the **LED** is your "physical reaction" — a digital twin of the human reflex arc.

> [!NOTE]
> The IoT Trainer Kit typically bundles an **on-board LDR** (often wired to **GPIO34** or **A0**) and a **KY-038 / KY-037 sound sensor module** with both analog (A0) and digital (D0) outputs, plus a **dedicated LED on a fixed GPIO** (commonly **GPIO2** on NodeMCU / **GPIO5** on ESP32 DevKit).

## 1.3 Key Physical / Electrical Constants

- **ADC Reference Voltage (Vref)** on ESP32 = **3.3 V**; on Arduino Uno = **5.0 V**.
- **ESP32 ADC Resolution:** 12-bit → **0 to 4095** digital steps.
- **Arduino Uno ADC Resolution:** 10-bit → **0 to 1023** digital steps.
- **LM393 Sound Sensor Threshold:** Adjustable via on-board potentiometer; default digital output logic is **LOW when sound detected, HIGH when silent** (active-low on most kits).
- **LDR Resistance Range:** ~**1 kΩ (bright light)** to ~**1 MΩ (darkness)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why thresholding matters in KTU exams:** Thresholding converts continuous analog data into discrete **event-driven** logic — this is the conceptual heart of **edge computing** and **actuator triggering** in IoT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — The 5-Stage Pipeline

The end-to-end signal flow of a sensor-interfaced IoT node follows a strict pipeline. Each stage is mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivations:

1. **Physical Transduction** — The environmental variable (light photon flux or acoustic pressure wave) is converted into a small analog voltage by the sensor's transducer element.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — The raw signal is amplified (microphone pre-amp) or passed through a voltage divider (LDR + fixed resistor) to bring it into the **0–Vref** operating window of the MCU's ADC.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — The MCU samples the conditioned voltage and quantizes it into a discrete integer using the resolution bits ($N$).
4. **Firmware Threshold Comparison** — The digital value is compared against a user-defined threshold in the script. This is the **decision logic** layer.
5. **Actuator Actuation** — A GPIO pin is driven HIGH or LOW, energizing the LED (or relay, buzzer, motor) to produce a physical action.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **canonical reference** for all numerical and threshold-related problems in this experiment.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{2^N - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | ADC to Voltage conversion | Volts (V) | $N$ = ADC bit resolution (10 or 12) |
| 2 | $R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \times \dfrac{V_{ref} - V_{out}}{V_{out}}$ | LDR resistance from voltage divider | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $R_{fixed}$ = series resistor (typically **10 kΩ**) |
| 3 | $Lux \approx \dfrac{(R_{LDR} / 10)^{(-1.45)}}{10}$ | Empirical LDR-to-Lux approximation | lux | Approximate; non-linear in dark range |
| 4 | $dB_{SPL} \propto 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{ADC_{value}}{ADC_{calib}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level proxy | dB SPL | $ADC_{calib}$ = quiet-room baseline |
| 5 | $Threshold_{logic} = \begin{cases} 1 \;(LED\_ON) & \text{if } ADC_{sound} \geq T_{sound} \\ 0 \;(LED\_OFF) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Event-driven actuation | Boolean | $T_{sound}$ = user-defined threshold (e.g., **600** of 4095) |
| 6 | $f_{sampling} = \dfrac{1}{T_{delay}}$ | Sampling rate | Hz | Common: $T_{delay} = 200$ ms → **5 Hz** |
| 7 | $\text{Debounce Time} = N \times T_{sample}$ | Software debounce | seconds | Prevents flickering on noisy threshold |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state the ADC reference voltage** explicitly. A missing $V_{ref}$ declaration costs **1 mark** in the derivation sub-part.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Stage 1 (Transduction):** Real sensors are non-ideal — they have noise, drift, and non-linearity. Engineers choose LDRs over photodiodes when cost is the priority and bandwidth is low (≤1 kHz).
- **Stage 2 (Conditioning):** The **LDR voltage divider** is the single most asked KTU numerical. Students must derive $R_{LDR}$ from observed $V_{out}$.
- **Stage 3 (ADC):** The **12-bit ESP32 ADC** has a known non-linearity error — KTU may ask why we use `analogRead()` carefully.
- **Stage 4 (Threshold):** This is the **edge-computing** primitive. Instead of streaming raw data to the cloud, the node decides locally — saving bandwidth, latency, and power.
- **Stage 5 (Actuation):** The LED response time (~**nanoseconds**) is orders of magnitude faster than the sensor sampling — making the LED effectively instantaneous relative to the human eye.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: ADC Reading to Voltage Conversion (12-bit ESP32 Case)

**Given:** ESP32 ADC returns a digital value of **2500** when the LDR voltage divider outputs a mid-range value. ESP32 uses a **12-bit ADC** and **3.3 V reference**.

**Step 1 — Identify ADC parameters.**
Resolution $N = 12$, so maximum digital count = $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$.
Reference voltage $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V.

**Step 2 — Apply the canonical ADC conversion formula.**

$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC_{value}}{2^N - 1} \times V_{ref}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
V_{in} = \frac{2500}{4095} \times 3.3
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator-dominant ratio.**

$$
\frac{2500}{4095} = 0.6105
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $V_{ref}$.**

$$
V_{in} = 0.6105 \times 3.3 = 2.0147 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**
Since $2500$ is roughly **61%** of $4095$, the voltage should be roughly **61% of 3.3 V** ≈ 2.01 V. ✔ Verified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $V_{in} \approx 2.01$ V. This corresponds to a **moderately lit room** on a typical LDR curve.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LDR Resistance from Voltage Divider

**Given:** The LDR is connected in series with a fixed resistor $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ across a 3.3 V rail. The voltage at the divider midpoint is measured as $V_{out} = 1.65$ V.

**Step 1 — Write the voltage divider equation.**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply.**

$$
V_{out} \times (R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}) = V_{ref} \times R_{fixed}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $R_{LDR}$.**

$$
R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \times \left(\frac{V_{ref}}{V_{out}} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
R_{LDR} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times \left(\frac{3.3}{1.65} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{3.3}{1.65} - 1 = 2.0 - 1 = 1.0
$$

**Step 6 — Finalize.**

$$
R_{LDR} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 1.0 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $V_{out} = V_{ref}/2$, the LDR resistance **equals** the fixed resistor — a classic midpoint used to define the **calibration reference** in IoT light sensors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Threshold Logic — LED Trigger Condition

**Given:** The microphone sensor's analog output is read by the ESP32's ADC. The threshold $T_{sound} = 600$ is chosen empirically after observing a quiet room (baseline ≈ **200–300**) and a loud clap (peak ≈ **800–1200**).

**Step 1 — Define the decision function $f(ADC_{sound})$.**

$$
f(ADC_{sound}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } ADC_{sound} \geq T_{sound} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Map boolean to GPIO voltage.**

$$
V_{LED} = f(ADC_{sound}) \times V_{ref}
$$

**Step 3 — Logical flow in pseudocode.**

```
IF ADC_sound >= 600 THEN
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH)   // LED ON
ELSE
    digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW)    // LED OFF
END IF
```

**Step 4 — Add software debounce (industry best practice).**

$$
T_{debounce} = 5 \times T_{sample} = 5 \times 0.2 = 1.0 \text{ second}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 1-second debounce prevents the LED from flickering on transient spikes (e.g., a single key-drop on a desk). This is the KTU-favored **hysteresis-like** technique for analog thresholds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reference Script (ESP32 + MicroPython)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical lab reference script**. The KTU record must include a **circuit diagram**, the **code**, the **observation table**, and the **result** (LED response curve).

```python
# ============================================================
# PCAML507 - IoT LAB | Module Experiment 2
# Light & Sound Sensor Interfacing with Threshold LED Trigger
# Target Board: ESP32 DevKit V1 | Language: MicroPython
# ============================================================

from machine import Pin, ADC, time_pulse_us
import time

# -------- Pin Configuration --------
LIGHT_SENSOR_PIN = 34      # ESP32 GPIO34 (ADC1_CH6) -> LDR
SOUND_SENSOR_PIN = 35      # ESP32 GPIO35 (ADC1_CH7) -> Microphone
LED_PIN          = 2       # On-board LED (active HIGH)

# -------- Object Initialization --------
light_sensor = ADC(Pin(LIGHT_SENSOR_PIN))
light_sensor.atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)        # Full 0–3.3V range

sound_sensor = ADC(Pin(SOUND_SENSOR_PIN))
sound_sensor.atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)

led = Pin(LED_PIN, Pin.OUT)

# -------- Calibration / Threshold --------
SOUND_THRESHOLD   = 600     # Empirically tuned for room noise
LIGHT_THRESHOLD   = 2000    # Below this = dark room
DEBOUNCE_SAMPLES  = 3       # Consecutive hits to confirm event

# -------- Helper Functions --------
def read_average(adc, n=10):
    """Read ADC 'n' times and return integer mean to filter noise."""
    total = 0
    for _ in range(n):
        total += adc.read()
    return total // n

# -------- Main Event Loop --------
print("System Booted. Monitoring light & sound...")

try:
    while True:
        # 1. Read sensor values
        light_value = read_average(light_sensor, 10)
        sound_value = read_average(sound_sensor, 10)

        # 2. Convert ADC to voltage (optional, for logging)
        v_light = (light_value / 4095.0) * 3.3
        v_sound = (sound_value / 4095.0) * 3.3

        # 3. Threshold logic: LED on ONLY if sound is loud AND room is dark
        if sound_value >= SOUND_THRESHOLD and light_value < LIGHT_THRESHOLD:
            led.value(1)
            led_state = "ON"
        else:
            led.value(0)
            led_state = "OFF"

        # 4. Log to console
        print(f"Light ADC: {light_value:4d} | Sound ADC: {sound_value:4d} | "
              f"V_light: {v_light:.2f} V | V_sound: {v_sound:.2f} V | LED: {led_state}")

        # 5. Sampling delay
        time.sleep_ms(200)         # 5 Hz sampling rate

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    led.value(0)
    print("Experiment terminated by user.")
```

---

## 3.5 Reference Observation Table Format (for KTU Record)

| S.No | Ambient Condition | Light ADC | Sound ADC (Idle) | Sound ADC (Clap) | V_light (V) | V_sound (V) | LED State |
|------|------------------|-----------|------------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | Bright daylight | 3500 | 250 | 950 | 2.82 | 0.20 → 0.77 | OFF |
| 2 | Tube light ON | 2100 | 230 | 880 | 1.69 | 0.19 → 0.71 | OFF |
| 3 | Dark room | 800 | 210 | 820 | 0.64 | 0.17 → 0.66 | **ON** (on clap) |
| 4 | Dark room, silent | 750 | 200 | 220 | 0.60 | 0.16 | OFF |
| 5 | Window open, noisy | 3000 | 500 | 1100 | 2.42 | 0.40 → 0.89 | OFF (light too high) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between analog and digital sensor output with one example each from the IoT Trainer Kit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Output | Continuous voltage range (0–Vref) | Discrete HIGH/LOW (0 or Vref) |
| ADC Required? | **Yes** — must be quantized | **No** — read directly via GPIO |
| Example from kit | **LDR** on GPIO34 (light) | **D0 pin of KY-038** (sound threshold) |
| Resolution | Up to 12-bit on ESP32 | 1-bit (binary) |
| Use case | Precise measurement | Event detection |

**(1 mark** for the basic distinction, **1 mark** for the example, **1 mark** for the tabular clarity.) ✔

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State the role of the LM393 comparator in the KY-038 sound sensor module.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **LM393** is a dual low-power **voltage comparator IC** present on the KY-038 sound module. Its role is to:

1. **Compare the amplified microphone signal** (analog audio envelope) against a **user-adjustable reference voltage** set by the on-board potentiometer.
2. **Generate a clean digital HIGH/LOW** output on the **D0 pin** whenever the audio envelope crosses the threshold — providing a **hardware-level event trigger** that does not require the MCU to perform continuous analog sampling for binary "sound detected / not detected" decisions.
3. **Offload processing** from the MCU, enabling ultra-low-power wake-up architectures.

**(1 mark** per point.) ✔

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of an **LDR** with the ESP32 IoT Trainer Kit. Derive the expression for $R_{LDR}$ in terms of the observed voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete MicroPython script to read the LDR value every 200 ms and print both the **ADC count** and the **equivalent voltage** on the serial monitor. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The LDR is connected in a **voltage divider** configuration with a fixed resistor $R_{fixed} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ between the **3.3 V rail** and **GND**. The **midpoint** of the divider is tapped to the **GPIO34 (ADC1_CH6)** pin of the ESP32. As light intensity increases, $R_{LDR}$ decreases, causing the midpoint voltage to rise.

**Step 2 — Voltage Divider Equation (2 marks):**

$$
V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $R_{LDR}$ (2 marks):**

$$
R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \times \left(\frac{V_{ref}}{V_{out}} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the practical reading (1 mark):** If $V_{out} = 1.65$ V, then $R_{LDR} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times (3.3/1.65 - 1) = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```python
from machine import Pin, ADC
import time

LDR_PIN = 34
VREF    = 3.3
ADC_MAX = 4095

ldr = ADC(Pin(LDR_PIN))
ldr.atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)

while True:
    raw = ldr.read()
    volt = (raw / ADC_MAX) * VREF
    print(f"LDR ADC: {raw:4d}  |  Voltage: {volt:.3f} V")
    time.sleep_ms(200)
```

**(1 mark** for the pin configuration, **2 marks** for the ADC setup, **2 marks** for the voltage conversion using the formula, **1 mark** for the formatted serial print, **1 mark** for the 200 ms delay / loop structure.) ✔

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **threshold-based LED triggering mechanism** when the sound sensor's analog output exceeds a user-defined level. Justify why **software debouncing** is essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design and write a **MicroPython script** for the IoT Trainer Kit that turns ON an LED (on GPIO2) **only when** the sound ADC value exceeds **600** AND the ambient light ADC value is below **2000** (i.e., dark room). Include comments and use a 200 ms sampling interval. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Threshold Mechanism (3 marks):**
The MCU reads the sound sensor's analog output (0–4095) and compares it against a calibrated threshold $T_{sound}$. When $ADC_{sound} \geq T_{sound}$, the firmware drives the LED GPIO **HIGH**, sourcing current through a current-limiting resistor (typically **220 Ω**). This implements an **event-driven actuator** that converts a continuous analog input into a discrete digital action.

**Step 2 — Debouncing Justification (2 marks):**
Acoustic signals are **highly noisy** and exhibit rapid transients (echoes, reflections, electrical noise). Without debouncing, a single loud sound could trigger the LED multiple times in rapid succession, causing visible flickering. **Software debouncing** via a counter ($N = 3$ consecutive threshold-exceeding samples) ensures the LED responds only to **sustained events**, mimicking the **hysteresis** property of a Schmitt trigger.

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance (2 marks):**
This is the foundational concept behind **smart home security systems**, **clap-activated switches**, and **noise pollution monitors** — all of which require robust threshold + debounce logic to function reliably in real-world environments.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```python
from machine import Pin, ADC
import time

LIGHT_PIN  = 34   # LDR
SOUND_PIN  = 35   # Microphone
LED_PIN    = 2    # On-board LED

T_SOUND = 600
T_LIGHT = 2000
SAMPLE_DELAY_MS = 200

light  = ADC(Pin(LIGHT_PIN));  light.atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)
sound  = ADC(Pin(SOUND_PIN));  sound.atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)
led    = Pin(LED_PIN, Pin.OUT)

print("System Online — Monitoring Light & Sound...")

while True:
    light_val = light.read()
    sound_val = sound.read()

    # Threshold logic: dark room AND loud sound
    if sound_val >= T_SOUND and light_val < T_LIGHT:
        led.value(1)     # LED ON
    else:
        led.value(0)     # LED OFF

    print(f"Light: {light_val} | Sound: {sound_val} | LED: {'ON' if led.value() else 'OFF'}")
    time.sleep_ms(SAMPLE_DELAY_MS)
```

**(1 mark** for pin definitions, **1 mark** for thresholds, **2 marks** for correct combined condition (`AND` operator), **1 mark** for GPIO control, **1 mark** for serial logging, **1 mark** for the delay & loop.) ✔

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCAML507 Lab Exam Records:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare $V_{ref}$ and $N$** explicitly in the derivation — costs **1 mark** per sub-question.
> 2. **Using `digitalRead()` on an analog sensor pin** — the LDR and microphone **A0 pin** require `analogRead()` / `ADC.read()`. Reading a digital value from a purely analog pin yields **0 or 1 randomly**.
> 3. **Missing `atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)`** on ESP32 — the default attenuation clips the reading at ~1.0 V, severely limiting the dynamic range. KTU evaluators check this line specifically.
> 4. **No debounce / hysteresis** in the threshold loop — for a 14-mark question, the absence of a debounce mechanism costs **up to 2 marks**.
> 5. **No observation table** in the record — even a 3-row table is mandatory. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly mandates tabular observation.
> 6. **Forgetting a current-limiting resistor (220 Ω)** with the LED in the circuit diagram — direct connection to GPIO will damage the LED and the MCU.
> 7. **Confusing LM393's active-LOW digital output** — the D0 pin is **LOW when sound is detected** on most kits. Wiring it as active-HIGH is a common logic error.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- ☐ **LDR** = Light Dependent Resistor; resistance **decreases** with light intensity.
- ☐ **LDR interfacing** uses a **voltage divider** with a fixed resistor (10 kΩ typical).
- ☐ **KY-038 sound sensor** has **two outputs:** A0 (analog envelope) and D0 (digital threshold).
- ☐ **LM393 comparator** on the sound module enables hardware-level event detection.
- ☐ **ESP32 ADC** is **12-bit** (range **0–4095**); Arduino Uno is **10-bit** (0–1023).
- ☐ **ADC formula:** $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{value}}{2^N - 1} \times V_{ref}$.
- ☐ **ESP32 $V_{ref}$ = 3.3 V**; always set `atten(ADC.ATTN_11DB)` for full-scale reading.
- ☐ **Threshold logic** converts continuous analog to discrete **event-driven** actuation — core of edge computing.
- ☐ **Software debounce** (counter-based, $N=3$) prevents false triggering on noise.
- ☐ **LED requires 220 Ω current-limiting resistor** in series with the GPIO.
- ☐ **Sampling rate** $f_s = 1/T_{delay}$; typical $T_{delay} = 200$ ms → $f_s = 5$ Hz.
- ☐ **Combined condition** uses `and` (Python) / `&&` (Arduino) for multi-sensor logic.
- ☐ **Real-world use cases:** smart streetlights, security alarms, baby cry monitors, industrial noise alarms.
- ☐ **Record must contain:** circuit diagram + code + observation table + result + viva questions.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCAML507 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Use the IoT Trainer kit to set up an MQTT communication system. Publish sensor data to a topic and subscribe to another topic to receive control signals.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Use the IoT Trainer kit to set up an MQTT communication system. Publish sensor data to a topic and a subscribe to another topic to receive control signals. # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish/subscribe (pub/sub) based messaging protocol standardized under **ISO/IEC 20922**, designed specifically for resource-constrained IoT devices and low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks. It operates over TCP/IP (typically port **1883** for unencrypted and **8883** for TLS-encrypted communication) and follows the OASIS standard specification. In the context of this experiment, the IoT Trainer kit is configured to: - **Publish** sensor data (temperature, humidity, light, etc.) to a designated MQTT **topic** hosted on a **Broker** (server). - **Subscribe** to a separate control topic to receive command signals (e.g., LED ON/OFF, relay trigger) from another client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Architectural Concept — Pub/Sub vs. Traditional Client-Server:** > Unlike HTTP where a client directly requests data from a specific server, MQTT decouples the **Publisher** (data sender) from the **Subscriber** (data receiver). Communication is mediated by a central **MQTT Broker**, which is responsible for all message filtering, distribution, and session management. This decoupling is the foundational design principle of MQTT and is a direct KTU syllabus focus. ### Essential Terminology — Definition Block | Term | Formal Definition | |---|---| | **Broker** | Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that receives all published messages and routes them to interested subscribers. | | **Client** | Any device or application that connects to the broker to publish or subscribe. | | **Topic** | A UTF-8 string label (hierarchical, e.g., `home/livingroom/temp`) used by the broker to filter messages for subscribers. | | **Publish** | The act of a client sending a message to a topic on the broker. | | **Subscribe** | The act of a client registering interest in one or more topics with the broker. | | **QoS (Quality of Service)** | The level of delivery guarantee — 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), or 2 (exactly once). | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radio Station" Model To make the abstract pub/sub model instantly intuitive, imagine a **community radio station**: 1. **The Radio Station Tower** is the **MQTT Broker** — it is always on, and it manages everything in the middle. 2. **A DJ broadcasting on FM 101.5 MHz** is a **Publisher** sending data to a specific **Topic** (think of it as a "channel" or "frequency"). 3. **A listener in a car who has tuned their radio to FM 101.5 MHz** is a **Subscriber** that has registered interest in that specific topic. 4. **Listeners tuned to FM 99.0 MHz** will never hear the DJ on 101.5 — this is exactly how topic-based filtering works in MQTT. 5. **The DJ does not know who is listening, and the listeners do not know who is broadcasting** — this complete decoupling is the defining feature of pub/sub. > [!NOTE] > **Why MQTT for IoT?** In a typical IoT scenario involving thousands of low-power sensors transmitting over Wi-Fi, MQTT reduces bandwidth consumption by up to **~50x** compared to HTTP due to its fixed 2-byte minimum header (vs. HTTP's 8+ byte headers plus JSON/XML payload wrapping). This is critical for battery-powered devices running on constrained networks. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants for the Experiment The following default values and constants are expected to be memorized for KTU lab viva and exam: - **Default MQTT Port (Unencrypted):** **1883** - **Default MQTT Port (TLS/SSL Encrypted):** **8883** - **Maximum Topic Size:** **65,536 bytes** (UTF-8 encoded) - **Maximum Client ID Length:** **23 bytes** (for older MQTT 3.1.1; extended in v5) - **Keep Alive Interval Default:** **60 seconds** - **Wildcard Characters:** `+` (single-level), `#` (multi-level) - **Standard QoS Levels:** 0, 1, 2 # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 MQTT Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture MQTT is intentionally thin and sits on top of the **TCP/IP stack**, providing guaranteed ordered delivery. The architecture is logically divided into four functional layers: ### Layer 1: Application Layer (User/Sensor Data) - The actual payload — sensor readings, control commands, JSON-formatted telemetry. - Example payload: `{"temp": 28.4, "hum": 65, "ts": 1700000000}` (raw bytes sent to broker). ### Layer 2: MQTT Protocol Engine - Handles **CONNECT**, **PUBLISH**, **SUBSCRIBE**, **UNSUBSCRIBE**, **PINGREQ**, **DISCONNECT** control packets. - Manages session state, **Keep-Alive** PINGREQ/PINGRESP heartbeat mechanism. - Enforces topic filtering and wildcard matching. ### Layer 3: Transport Layer (TCP) - Provides reliable, ordered, connection-oriented byte streams. - Default port: **1883** (plain TCP) or **8883** (over TLS). ### Layer 4: Network/Link Layer (IP/Ethernet/Wi-Fi) - Standard IPv4/IPv6 over Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), or cellular (NB-IoT, LTE-M). - In the IoT Trainer kit context, typically Wi-Fi is used. ## 2.2 The MQTT Control Packet Format — Bit-Level Breakdown Every MQTT control packet follows the structure defined by the OASIS standard. A thorough understanding of this is a frequent viva question. A standard MQTT packet has a minimum overhead of just **2 bytes** (the Fixed Header) when QoS = 0, making it ideal for constrained devices. | Field | Size | Description | |---|---|---| | **Fixed Header** | 2 to 5 bytes | Present in ALL packets. Contains Packet Type, Flags, and Remaining Length. | | **Variable Header** | 0 to N bytes | Depends on packet type. E.g., PUBLISH uses it for Topic Name + Packet Identifier. | | **Payload** | 0 to N bytes | Application data. Present only in CONNECT, PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE. | ### Fixed Header Sub-Fields - **Byte 1 (Control Packet Type + Flags):** - Bits 7-4: Packet Type (e.g., `0001` = CONNECT, `0011` = PUBLISH, `1000` = SUBSCRIBE) - Bits 3-0: Flags (e.g., for PUBLISH: DUP, QoS level, RETAIN) - **Bytes 2-5 (Remaining Length):** - Variable-length encoding of the number of bytes remaining in the packet (Variable Header + Payload). - Encoded using a 1 to 4 byte scheme where each byte uses the lower 7 bits for data and the MSB as a continuation flag. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PUBLISH Packet — The Most Critical Packet for This Experiment** > For a PUBLISH packet: > - **Byte 1:** `0011 xxxx` (where `xxxx` encodes DUP/QoS/RETAIN flags) > - **Byte 2-N:** Remaining Length > - **Variable Header:** Topic Name (UTF-8) + Packet Identifier (only if QoS > 0) > - **Payload:** The actual sensor data (binary or text) ## 2.3 The Three Quality of Service (QoS) Levels — High-Yield Table | QoS Level | Name | Delivery Guarantee | Use Case in IoT Trainer | |---|---|---|---| | **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget; no acknowledgment | Non-critical telemetry (e.g., periodic temperature reading) | | **QoS 1** | At least once | Acknowledged; possible duplicates | Standard sensor data publish | | **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Four-step handshake; no duplicates | Critical control signals (e.g., relay toggle, door unlock) | > [!NOTE] > For the experiment, **QoS 0 is typically used for periodic sensor publishing** (efficiency), while **QoS 1 or 2 is recommended for control signal subscription** to guarantee the command reaches the device. ## 2.4 Wildcard Topic Subscriptions — Engineering Utility Two wildcard characters allow subscribing to multiple topics simultaneously: - **`+` (Plus Sign — Single Level):** Matches exactly one topic level. - Example: Subscribing to `home/+/temperature` receives `home/livingroom/temperature` and `home/bedroom/temperature`, but NOT `home/livingroom/floor1/temperature`. - **`#` (Hash — Multi-Level):** Matches all remaining levels. **Must be the last character in the topic.** - Example: Subscribing to `home/#` receives every message under `home/`. This is exceptionally useful when the subscriber does not know the exact topic name in advance, or when aggregating data from many sensors. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Value / Formula | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Default MQTT Port | **1883** (plain) / **8883** (TLS) | Network firewall configuration | | 2 | Minimum Header Size | **2 bytes** (QoS 0 PUBLISH) | Bandwidth optimization vs. HTTP | | 3 | Keep-Alive Heartbeat | Default **60 s** (range: 1–65535 s) | Detects broken connections | | 4 | QoS Levels | 0, 1, 2 (numeric codes) | Maps directly to flag bits in PUBLISH | | 5 | PUBLISH Packet Type Code | `0x30` (hex) = `0011 0000` (binary) | Wire-level packet inspection | | 6 | SUBSCRIBE Packet Type Code | `0x82` (hex) | Wire-level packet inspection | | 7 | CONNACK Packet Type Code | `0x20` (hex) | Connection acknowledgment | | 8 | Max Topic Length | 65,536 bytes | Memory planning on constrained devices | | 9 | Last Will & Testament (LWT) | A preset message sent by broker upon unexpected client disconnect | Fault detection in IoT networks | | 10 | Retain Flag | When set to 1, broker stores the last message for new subscribers | New subscribers immediately get latest state | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industrial IoT (IIoT) deployments, MQTT is used by Siemens, AWS IoT Core, Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, and Google Cloud IoT as the primary southbound protocol. Understanding the protocol mechanics is essential for designing low-latency SCADA and telemetry systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Procedure & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The following procedure is written in **exhaustive detail** as a lab manual would, ensuring that every transition from hardware setup to logical execution is explicitly shown. No step is skipped. ## 3.1 Hardware and Software Prerequisites ### Hardware Required (IoT Trainer Kit) 1. **Main Controller Board:** ESP32 / NodeMCU (ESP8266) — acts as the MQTT client. 2. **Onboard Sensors:** DHT11/DHT22 (temperature/humidity) and/or LDR (light). 3. **Onboard Actuators:** LED, Buzzer, and/or Relay module — used to receive control signals. 4. **USB Cable:** For flashing firmware and serial monitoring. 5. **Wi-Fi Router/Access Point:** Provides IP connectivity to the MQTT broker. ### Software Required 1. **Arduino IDE** (or PlatformIO) — for writing and flashing the ESP firmware. 2. **MQTT Broker:** **Mosquitto** (lightweight, open-source, runs on PC/Raspberry Pi/localhost). 3. **MQTT Client Test Tool:** **MQTTBox** or **MQTTX** (GUI) for verifying publish/subscribe from PC. 4. **PubSubClient Library** (by Nick O'Leary) — for Arduino. 5. **Wi-Fi Library** (built-in for ESP32/ESP8266). ## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure ### Step 1: Install and Start the MQTT Broker (Mosquitto) **Step 1.1:** Download Mosquitto from the official Eclipse Foundation website and install it on the host PC. **Step 1.2:** Open a terminal/command prompt and start the broker on the default port. ```bash mosquitto -p 1883 -v ``` The `-v` flag enables **verbose logging** so we can see every packet arriving at the broker. This is critical for debugging the experiment. **Step 1.3:** Verify the broker is listening. Open another terminal and subscribe to a wildcard topic. ```bash mosquitto_sub -h localhost -t "test/#" -v ``` If this command executes without error, the broker is operational. The `-h` flag specifies the broker host (localhost means the broker is on the same machine as the test client). ### Step 2: Identify Network Configuration **Step 2.1:** Find the IP address of the host PC running the broker. On Windows: ```bash ipconfig ``` On Linux/Mac: ```bash ifconfig ``` Assume the host IP is `192.168.1.100`. The ESP32 client will connect to this IP. **Step 2.2:** Ensure that the ESP32 and the host PC are on the **same Wi-Fi network** and that the **firewall allows inbound TCP traffic on port 1883**. ### Step 3: Write the Arduino Sketch for the IoT Trainer Kit The firmware performs two primary functions: 1. **Publishes** sensor readings (DHT11 temperature) to the topic `iot/sensor/data`. 2. **Subscribes** to the topic `iot/control/led` to listen for commands (e.g., "ON" / "OFF"). **Step 3.1:** Open Arduino IDE. Go to **Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries** and install: - `PubSubClient` by Nick O'Leary - `DHT sensor library` by Adafruit **Step 3.2:** Verify the **pin mapping** of the IoT Trainer kit. Typical defaults: | Component | Pin on ESP32 | |---|---| | DHT11 DATA pin | GPIO 4 | | Built-in LED | GPIO 2 | | Buzzer | GPIO 5 | | LDR (Analog) | GPIO 34 (ADC) | **Step 3.3:** Write the complete firmware. The logical flow is shown in Section 4. ### Step 4: Flash the Firmware to the IoT Trainer Kit **Step 4.1:** In Arduino IDE, select the correct board (**Tools → Board → ESP32 Dev Module** or **NodeMCU 1.0**). **Step 4.2:** Select the correct COM port (**Tools → Port → COMx**). **Step 4.3:** Click the **Upload** button. Wait for the "Done uploading" message. **Step 4.4:** Open the **Serial Monitor** at **115200 baud** to observe boot logs. ### Step 5: Verify the Connection in Serial Monitor Upon successful boot, the Serial Monitor will display: ``` Connecting to WiFi... WiFi connected. IP: 192.168.1.105 Connecting to MQTT Broker... Subscribed to: iot/control/led ``` If any line fails, debug using the Mosquitto verbose log on the host PC. ### Step 6: Test the Publish Path (Sensor Data) **Step 6.1:** On the host PC, open a new terminal and subscribe to the data topic: ```bash mosquitto_sub -h 192.168.1.100 -t "iot/sensor/data" -v ``` **Step 6.2:** Observe the output. Every few seconds, the ESP32 will publish a message of the form: ``` iot/sensor/data {"temperature":28.5,"humidity":62,"ts":1700000000} ``` **Step 6.3:** **Analytical Observation:** The timestamp `ts` increments every publish cycle, proving the publish loop is alive. The JSON payload is the **application data** carried in the MQTT PUBLISH packet's payload field. ### Step 7: Test the Subscribe Path (Control Signal) **Step 7.1:** On the host PC, open another terminal and publish a control message: ```bash mosquitto_pub -h 192.168.1.100 -t "iot/control/led" -m "ON" ``` **Step 7.2:** Observe the IoT Trainer kit. The onboard LED should turn ON. This proves the subscription path is working. **Step 7.3:** Send another command: ```bash mosquitto_pub -h 192.168.1.100 -t "iot/control/led" -m "OFF" ``` The LED should turn OFF.
