The end-to-end pub/sub loop is now verified. ### Step 8: Edge Case — Using an External GUI Client (MQTTX) **Step 8.1:** Open MQTTX and create a new connection to `192.168.1.100:1883`. **Step 8.2:** Subscribe to `iot/sensor/data` to visualize incoming JSON in real time. **Step 8.3:** Publish raw JSON or text commands to `iot/control/led` and verify actuator response. This validates the system works with **multiple concurrent clients** (a key strength of pub/sub architecture). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — What Happens at Each Step (Byte-Level View) | Logical Step | MQTT Packet Sent | Direction | Source/Dest Port | Payload | |---|---|---|---|---| | ESP32 connects to broker | **CONNECT** | ESP32 → Broker | Src: ephemeral → Dst: 1883 | Client ID, Keep-Alive, Clean Session flag | | Broker acknowledges | **CONNACK** | Broker → ESP32 | Src: 1883 → Dst: ephemeral | Return code (0x00 = success) | | ESP32 subscribes | **SUBSCRIBE** | ESP32 → Broker | Src: ephemeral → Dst: 1883 | Topic: `iot/control/led`, QoS | | Broker confirms sub | **SUBACK** | Broker → ESP32 | Src: 1883 → Dst: ephemeral | Packet ID + return code | | ESP32 publishes sensor data | **PUBLISH** | ESP32 → Broker | Src: ephemeral → Dst: 1883 | Topic: `iot/sensor/data`, Body: JSON | | PC sends control signal | **PUBLISH** | PC → Broker | Src: ephemeral → Dst: 1883 | Topic: `iot/control/led`, Body: "ON" | | Broker forwards to ESP32 | **PUBLISH** | Broker → ESP32 | Src: 1883 → Dst: ephemeral | Topic: `iot/control/led`, Body: "ON" | | Keep-alive heartbeat | **PINGREQ / PINGRESP** | Bidirectional | Standard | Empty | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the ESP32 never directly communicates with the PC client. The **broker is the only entity that sees all packets**. This is the fundamental architectural difference from HTTP-based REST APIs and is a guaranteed KTU viva question. ## 3.4 Sample Firmware Skeleton (For Reference) ```cpp // Sample skeleton illustrating the dual pub/sub logic // (Full code in lab manual — this is the structural flow) void loop() { if (!client.connected()) reconnect(); client.loop(); // Process incoming MQTT messages static unsigned long lastPublish = 0; if (millis() - lastPublish > 3000) { // Every 3 seconds float t = dht.readTemperature(); String payload = "{\"temperature\":" + String(t) + "}"; client.publish("iot/sensor/data", payload.c_str()); lastPublish = millis(); } } void callback(char* topic, byte* payload, unsigned int length) { String msg = ""; for (int i = 0; i < length; i++) msg += (char)payload[i]; if (String(topic) == "iot/control/led") { if (msg == "ON") digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH); else if (msg == "OFF") digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW); } } ``` > [!NOTE] > The `client.loop()` call is the most critical — it processes incoming PUBLISH packets from the broker. Forgetting this call is the **#1 most common bug** in MQTT Arduino code and will cause the subscribe path to silently fail. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IoTKit[IoT Trainer Kit] SensorA[DHT11 Sensor] --> ESPmain SensorB[LDR Light Sensor] --> ESPmain ActuatorA[Onboard LED] --> ESPmain ActuatorB[Buzzer Relay] --> ESPmain end ESPmain[ESP32 MCU] ESPmain -- Wi-Fi 802.11 --> Router Router[Wi-Fi Router
192.168.1.1] Broker[MQTT Broker
Mosquitto
192.168.1.100:1883] PCclient[Test Client
MQTTX or mosquitto_pub] ESPmain -- PUBLISH iot/sensor/data --> Broker PCclient -- PUBLISH iot/control/led --> Broker Broker -- PUBLISH iot/control/led --> ESPmain ESPmain --> ActuatorA ESPmain --> ActuatorB Broker -- Mirror for monitoring --> PCclient ``` ## 4.2 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Packet Flow Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant DevKit as IoT Trainer Kit participant Broker as MQTT Broker participant Tester as PC Test Client DevKit->>Broker: CONNECT (ClientID, KeepAlive=60s) Broker-->>DevKit: CONNACK (Accepted) DevKit->>Broker: SUBSCRIBE iot/control/led (QoS=1) Broker-->>DevKit: SUBACK (Granted QoS=1) loop Every 3 seconds DevKit->>Broker: PUBLISH iot/sensor/data {temp:28.5} Broker-->>DevKit: PUBACK (QoS=1 ack) end Note over Tester,Broker: Tester sends a control command Tester->>Broker: PUBLISH iot/control/led "ON" Broker->>DevKit: PUBLISH iot/control/led "ON" DevKit-->>Broker: PUBACK DevKit->>DevKit: Turn LED ON Note over DevKit,Broker: Keep-alive heartbeat DevKit->>Broker: PINGREQ Broker-->>DevKit: PINGRESP ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Data Plane Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Plane1[Application Plane] AppA[Sensor Data JSON] AppB[Control Command String] end subgraph Plane2[MQTT Protocol Plane] TopicA[iot/sensor/data] TopicB[iot/control/led] end subgraph Plane3[Broker Logic Plane] Filter[Topic Filter Engine] Sess[Session Manager] Retain[Retained Message Store] end subgraph Plane4[Transport Plane] TCP1[TCP Socket 1
Port 1883] TCP2[TCP Socket 2
Port 1883] end AppA --> TopicA AppB --> TopicB TopicA --> Filter TopicB --> Filter Filter --> Sess Sess --> Retain Sess --> TCP1 Sess --> TCP2 ``` ## 4.4 Experiment Workflow — Logical State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> BootInit BootInit --> WiFiConnect: powerOn WiFiConnect --> MQTTConnect: wifiOK WiFiConnect --> WiFiRetry: wifiFail WiFiRetry --> WiFiConnect MQTTConnect --> Idle: connackOK MQTTConnect --> MQTTRetry: connackFail MQTTRetry --> MQTTConnect Idle --> Publishing: timerTrigger3s Publishing --> Idle: publishDone Idle --> Receiving: incomingPublish Receiving --> ActuateLED: msg ON Receiving --> DeactuateLED: msg OFF ActuateLED --> Idle DeactuateLED --> Idle ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing Port Numbers:** Students often write 8080 (HTTP) instead of 1883. This loses marks. > 2. **Misnaming the Components:** Calling the broker a "server" and the client a "device" is acceptable in casual speech, but in KTU answers, the **exact terms** — Broker, Client, Topic, Publisher, Subscriber — must be used. > 3. **Skipping the Pub/Sub Decoupling:** Many students describe MQTT as a "client-server" protocol. The **decoupled publish/subscribe** nature is the *defining* feature and is worth at least 2 marks in any 14-mark question. > 4. **Forgetting the `client.loop()` function:** In code-based questions, omitting the `client.loop()` call (which keeps the MQTT connection alive and processes incoming messages) is the most common functional error and results in zero marks for the subscribe path. > 5. **Ignoring QoS:** Not mentioning the Quality of Service levels when explaining PUBLISH packets is a frequent deduction point. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between the publish/subscribe model used by MQTT and the request/response model used by HTTP. List two advantages of MQTT for IoT applications.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | MQTT (Pub/Sub) | HTTP (Req/Res) | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Decoupled; mediated by a broker | Direct client-to-server connection | | **Coupling** | Publisher and subscriber are unaware of each other | Client must know the server URL | | **Header Size** | Minimum **2 bytes** | Typically 8+ bytes (plus JSON/XML wrapping) | | **Connection** | Persistent TCP connection (keep-alive) | Often short-lived (new connection per request) | | **Power Efficiency** | Designed for low-power, long-lived connections | High overhead per request | **Two Advantages of MQTT for IoT:** 1. **Low Bandwidth Consumption:** The 2-byte minimum header drastically reduces network usage — critical for cellular and low-power IoT devices. 2. **Asynchronous Communication:** Sensors can publish data without waiting for a request, enabling real-time telemetry with minimal energy use. *(1 mark for the differentiation table, 2 marks for the advantages — total 3 marks)* ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT. When would you use each one in an IoT Trainer kit experiment?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **QoS 0 — At most once:** Fire-and-forget. No acknowledgment from broker. Used for **non-critical, periodic sensor publishes** (e.g., a DHT11 temperature reading every 3 seconds). If a packet is lost, the next reading arrives shortly. *(1 mark)* - **QoS 1 — At least once:** Broker sends PUBACK. Possible duplicates. Used for **general sensor data** where occasional duplicates are tolerable but data loss is not. *(1 mark)* - **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** Four-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP). No loss, no duplicates. Used for **critical control commands** (e.g., toggling a relay that controls a high-voltage device or unlocking a door). *(1 mark)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module Experiment 2] **(a)** Draw the block diagram of an MQTT-based IoT system using an IoT Trainer kit. Describe the role of the **MQTT Broker** in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain step-by-step how the IoT Trainer kit **publishes** DHT11 temperature data to a topic and **subscribes** to a control topic to switch an LED ON/OFF. Include the relevant MQTT packet types exchanged. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The block diagram should show: - **IoT Trainer Kit** (ESP32 + DHT11 + LED) - **Wi-Fi Router** (network layer) - **MQTT Broker** (Mosquitto on PC, IP 192.168.1.100, Port 1883) - **Test Client** (MQTTX / mosquitto_pub / another ESP) **Role of the MQTT Broker (7 marks total, key points):** 1. **Central message router:** Receives all PUBLISH packets and routes them to subscribers based on topic matching. *(1 mark)* 2. **Connection management:** Accepts CONNECT, returns CONNACK, manages Keep-Alive (PINGREQ/PINGRESP). *(1 mark)* 3. **Topic-based filtering:** Maintains a topic subscription table and forwards only relevant messages. *(1 mark)* 4. **Session persistence:** Stores retained messages, queued offline messages (if Clean Session = 0), and Last Will & Testament (LWT) messages. *(1 mark)* 5. **QoS handling:** Implements QoS 0, 1, and 2 handshakes (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP). *(1 mark)* 6. **Security layer:** Handles username/password authentication and TLS encryption (port 8883). *(1 mark)* 7. **Decoupling enforcer:** The broker ensures publishers and subscribers never need to know each other's IP addresses or availability. *(1 mark)* **Part (b) — 7 Marks (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Connection Establishment:** - The ESP32 sends a **CONNECT** packet to the broker on TCP port **1883** containing the Client ID (e.g., `"TrainerKit_01"`) and a Keep-Alive interval of 60 seconds. *(0.5 mark)* - The broker responds with a **CONNACK** packet with return code `0x00` (Connection Accepted). *(0.5 mark)* **Step 2 — Topic Subscription:** - The ESP32 sends a **SUBSCRIBE** packet to the topic `iot/control/led` with QoS = 1. *(0.5 mark)* - The broker responds with a **SUBACK** packet granting the requested QoS. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 3 — Sensor Data Publishing Loop (every 3 seconds):** - The ESP32 reads the DHT11 sensor and constructs a JSON payload: `{"temperature": 28.5, "humidity": 62}`. *(0.5 mark)* - The ESP32 sends a **PUBLISH** packet with topic = `iot/sensor/data` and the JSON as the payload. *(0.5 mark)* - The broker forwards this PUBLISH to any client subscribed to `iot/sensor/data` (e.g., the MQTTX GUI on the PC). *(0.5 mark)* **Step 4 — Control Command Reception:** - The PC client sends a **PUBLISH** packet to topic `iot/control/led` with payload `"ON"` (or `"OFF"`). *(0.5 mark)* - The broker forwards this PUBLISH packet to the ESP32 subscriber. *(0.5 mark)* - The ESP32's `callback()` function receives the message, parses the payload, and toggles the LED accordingly. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 5 — Acknowledgment (QoS 1 path):** - The ESP32 sends a **PUBACK** packet back to the broker to confirm receipt. *(0.5 mark)* **Step 6 — Keep-Alive Maintenance:** - Every 60 seconds (or half of the keep-alive value), the ESP32 sends a **PINGREQ** packet, and the broker replies with **PINGRESP**. The `client.loop()` function in Arduino handles this automatically. *(0.5 mark)* *(Total: 7 marks for part b)* --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2] **(a)** What is the **MQTT Fixed Header**? Describe the structure of a **PUBLISH packet** including its control packet type, flags, and the meaning of the **RETAIN** flag. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the significance of **wildcard subscriptions** (`+` and `#`) in MQTT. Design a topic naming hierarchy for a smart home IoT system and show how a central dashboard would subscribe to aggregate data from all rooms. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **MQTT Fixed Header:** Every MQTT control packet begins with a 2 to 5 byte fixed header. It contains: - **Byte 1:** Control Packet Type (4 bits) + Flags (4 bits) - **Bytes 2-5:** Remaining Length (variable-length encoded) *(1 mark for the structure definition)* **PUBLISH Packet Type:** The binary code for PUBLISH is `0011 xxxx` (the `xxxx` represents the DUP, QoS, and RETAIN flags). In hex, with all flags clear (QoS 0, no DUP, no RETAIN), the first byte is `0x30`. *(1 mark)* **Flag Breakdown for PUBLISH:** - **DUP (Duplicate Delivery):** Set to 1 if the message is being re-sent (only for QoS > 0). *(1 mark)* - **QoS (2 bits):** Encodes the delivery guarantee — `00` = QoS 0, `01` = QoS 1, `10` = QoS 2. *(1 mark)* - **RETAIN:** When set to 1, the broker stores the message as the **last known good value** for that topic and delivers it immediately to any new subscriber. *(2 marks for the RETAIN explanation)* **Example Application of RETAIN:** If a sensor publishes temperature with RETAIN=1, a dashboard that subscribes later will instantly receive the latest temperature without waiting for the next publish cycle. This is critical for state-based actuator systems (e.g., "is the light currently ON?"). **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Wildcard Subscriptions:** - **`+` (Plus — Single Level):** Matches exactly one topic level. - Example: `home/+/temperature` matches `home/kitchen/temperature` and `home/bedroom/temperature` but NOT `home/kitchen/floor1/temperature`. *(1.5 marks)* - **`#` (Hash — Multi-Level):** Matches all remaining topic levels. **Must be the last character.** - Example: `home/#` matches every topic starting with `home/`. *(1.5 marks)* **Smart Home Topic Hierarchy Design:** *(2 marks)* ``` home/ ├── livingroom/ │ ├── temperature │ ├── humidity │ └── light ├── bedroom/ │ ├── temperature │ └── light ├── kitchen/ │ ├── temperature │ └── gas_sensor └── garden/ ├── soil_moisture └── temperature ``` **Central Dashboard Subscription Strategy:** *(2 marks)* The dashboard subscribes to: - `home/+/+` — to receive all room-level data (single level + single level). - OR more efficiently: `home/#` — to receive every message in the entire smart home. Using `home/#`, the dashboard acts as a **central aggregator**, receiving real-time telemetry from all rooms, gardens, and appliances in a single subscription — demonstrating the scalability advantage of MQTT's pub/sub model. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** - [ ] **MQTT** = Message Queuing Telemetry Transport, an ISO/IEC 20922 standard pub/sub protocol for IoT. - [ ] **Default port 1883** (plain TCP); **8883** (TLS encrypted). Memorize these. - [ ] **Three core components:** Broker (central server), Publisher (data source), Subscriber (data sink). - [ ] **Decoupling** is the defining feature — publishers and subscribers are unaware of each other. - [ ] **Minimum header size = 2 bytes** (vs. HTTP's 8+ bytes) — the primary reason MQTT is preferred for IoT. - [ ] **Topic** is a hierarchical UTF-8 string used for filtering. Maximum length: 65,536 bytes. - [ ] **Wildcards:** `+` (single level) and `#` (multi-level, must be last character). - [ ] **QoS Levels:** 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once). Use QoS 1 or 2 for control signals. - [ ] **RETAIN flag** = 1 means the broker stores the last message and delivers it to new subscribers immediately. - [ ] **Key packet types:** CONNECT/CONNACK, PUBLISH/PUBACK, SUBSCRIBE/SUBACK, PINGREQ/PINGRESP, DISCONNECT. - [ ] **Keep-Alive** uses PINGREQ/PINGRESP heartbeat — default 60 seconds. - [ ] **Last Will & Testament (LWT):** A preset message sent by the broker when a client disconnects unexpectedly. - [ ] In Arduino, `client.loop()` is **mandatory** — it processes incoming messages and maintains the connection. - [ ] The **broker is the only entity** that sees all packets — publishers and subscribers never communicate directly. - [ ] For the experiment, the standard topics are typically `iot/sensor/data` (publish) and `iot/control/led` (subscribe). - [ ] Always verify the system with two terminals: `mosquitto_sub` (subscribe) and `mosquitto_pub` (publish).

topics.title: Use the IoT Trainer kit to send sensor data to a local server via HTTP. Create a simple web interface to display the data.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Use the IoT Trainer kit to send sensor data to a local server via HTTP. Create a simple web interface to display the data. ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **HTTP-based IoT Data Transmission** is a client-server communication paradigm in which an embedded IoT device (the *client* — typically an ESP8266/ESP32-based IoT Trainer Kit) acquires environmental sensor data (temperature, humidity, light, gas, etc.), encapsulates it as an HTTP request payload (usually **JSON** or **URL-encoded form data**), and transmits it over a **TCP/IP** network to a locally hosted web server (e.g., Flask, Node.js, XAMPP). The server then processes, persists, and exposes the data through a **web-based dashboard** rendered using HTML/CSS/JavaScript, thereby completing the **Sense → Transmit → Store → Visualize** IoT pipeline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAML507):** > This experiment maps directly to **CO2**: *"Design and implement IoT applications using HTTP/MQTT protocols with cloud/local servers"* and falls under the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)** cognitive levels: **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Postman" Model Imagine your IoT Trainer kit as a **digital postman** standing at a weather station: - The **postman** (ESP8266/ESP32 microcontroller) periodically walks out to the **weather instruments** (DHT11, LDR, MQ-2 sensors) and **reads their dials** (analog/digital sensor values). - He then writes the readings on a **standardized postcard** (JSON payload: `{"temperature": 28.5, "humidity": 65}`). - He **walks to the local post office** (your local server running on a laptop at `192.168.1.100:5000`) and **hands over the postcard** (HTTP POST request). - The postmaster **files the postcard** in a ledger (database / in-memory list) and **pins a copy on the public notice board** (the web dashboard). - Anyone visiting the post office (a browser at `http://localhost:5000`) can **read the notice board** and see the latest sensor readings in real time. This is precisely what we build in Experiment 2. --- ### 1.3 The IoT Trainer Kit — Hardware Overview The standard KTU-prescribed **IoT Trainer Kit** (commonly the **Scientech 6204 / Sunrom IoT Trainer / Evive / custom ESP32 dev-board**) typically integrates: | Hardware Block | Typical Component | Function | |---|---|---| | **MCU Core** | ESP8266 NodeMCU / ESP32-WROOM-32 | Wi-Fi-enabled 32-bit microcontroller acting as HTTP client | | **Temperature & Humidity Sensor** | **DHT11 / DHT22** | Measures ambient temperature (°C) and relative humidity (%RH) | | **Light Sensor** | **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** | Detects ambient light intensity (lux via ADC) | | **Gas/Smoke Sensor** | **MQ-2 / MQ-135** | Detects LPG, smoke, or air quality (analog ppm) | | **Actuators (optional)** | LED, Buzzer, Relay | Visual/audio feedback | | **Display (optional)** | 0.96" OLED / 16x2 LCD | On-device readout | | **Connectivity** | Onboard Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (2.4 GHz) | Wireless HTTP transmission | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics (Bold Constants):** > - **Wi-Fi Frequency Band: 2.4 GHz** (ISM band) > - **HTTP Default Port: 80** (we use **5000** for Flask / **3000** for Node.js to avoid privileges) > - **DHT11 Sampling Rate: 1 Hz (1 reading per second)** > - **ADC Resolution: 10-bit (0–1023)** on ESP8266; **12-bit (0–4095)** on ESP32 > - **ADC Reference Voltage: 3.3 V** --- ### 1.4 Protocol Stack Used in This Experiment ``` +-----------------------------+ <-- Application Layer: HTTP 1.1 | JSON Payload / HTML / CSS | +-----------------------------+ | TCP (Port 5000) | <-- Transport Layer +-----------------------------+ | IP | <-- Network Layer +-----------------------------+ | Wi-Fi (802.11) | <-- Link/Physical Layer +-----------------------------+ ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The HTTP Request–Response Cycle (Operational Breakdown) The experiment hinges on a **stateless, request-driven** protocol. Every data transmission follows this exact 7-step sequence: 1. **Sensor Sampling** — The MCU reads analog/digital values from the onboard sensors using GPIO/ADC pins. 2. **Data Encoding** — Raw ADC counts (e.g., `512`) are converted into engineering units (e.g., `2.4 V`, `25 °C`, `45 %RH`). 3. **Payload Construction** — The values are assembled into a **JSON object**: `{"temp": 28.5, "hum": 65, "lux": 320}`. 4. **Wi-Fi Association** — The ESP module connects to the local access point (SSID + password) and obtains a **DHCP lease**. 5. **TCP Handshake** — A `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK` three-way handshake establishes a TCP connection to the server's IP and port. 6. **HTTP Request Emission** — The client transmits an `HTTP POST /data HTTP/1.1` request with the JSON payload in the body. 7. **Server Processing & Rendering** — The Flask/Node server parses the JSON, stores it (or appends to a deque), and serves the dashboard HTML on `GET /`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why HTTP over alternatives (MQTT/CoAP)?** > - **HTTP** uses verbose human-readable headers → easier debugging in lab sessions. > - **MQTT** uses a publish/subscribe broker → requires an additional broker process. > - **CoAP** uses UDP → faster but unreliable; not preferred for KTU board exams. > - For *local server* demonstrations, **HTTP REST** is the **de-facto KTU expectation**. --- ### 2.2 HTTP Methods Used in This Experiment | Method | Purpose in Experiment | Request Body? | Idempotent? | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **`GET /`** | Fetch the dashboard HTML page | No | ✅ Yes | ★★★★★ | | **`POST /data`** | Upload sensor readings from MCU | Yes (JSON) | ❌ No (each reading is unique) | ★★★★★ | | **`GET /api/data`** | Fetch latest readings as JSON for AJAX refresh | No | ✅ Yes | ★★★★☆ | | **`GET /api/history`** | Retrieve last N readings for charting | No | ✅ Yes | ★★★☆☆ | > [!NOTE] > **Idempotency** means performing the same operation multiple times yields the same server-side result. POST is **non-idempotent** because each POST appends a new sensor reading. --- ### 2.3 HTTP Status Codes — The "Exam Must-Know" List | Status Code | Status Text | Meaning | When You See It in Lab | |---|---|---|---| | **`200 OK`** | Success | Request processed correctly | After a successful POST/GET | | **`201 Created`** | Resource created | New sensor record stored | After successful POST with persistence | | **`400 Bad Request`** | Malformed request | JSON syntax error / missing fields | When JSON has trailing commas | | **`404 Not Found`** | Endpoint missing | Wrong URL route | Typo in `/data` vs `/dat` | | **`500 Internal Server Error`** | Server crash | Unhandled exception in Flask/Node | JSON parser crash on bad payload | --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Sensor Conversion Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **These five formulas are the most-tested in KTU 2024 lab viva and written exams. Memorize them.** | # | Quantity | Conversion Formula | Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **ADC → Voltage** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC_{count}}{ADC_{max}} \times V_{ref}$ | $ADC_{count}$ = raw ADC value; $ADC_{max} = 1023$ (ESP8266) or $4095$ (ESP32); $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | **LDR → Lux (approx.)** | $L_{lux} \approx \dfrac{V_{ref} - V_{out}}{V_{out}} \times R_{LDR} \times k$ | $R_{LDR}$ = LDR resistance (Ω); $k$ = calibration constant ≈ $2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ | Lux | | 3 | **MQ-2 → ppm** | $ppm = a \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{RL}}{V_{CC} - V_{RL}}\right)^{b}$ | $a, b$ = sensor-specific constants from datasheet; $V_{RL}$ = load voltage | ppm | | 4 | **DHT11 Humidity** | Direct digital protocol (already in %RH) | — | %RH | | 5 | **DHT11 Temperature** | Direct digital protocol (already in °C) | — | °C | | 6 | **HTTP Payload Size** | $S_{payload} = \vert L_{JSON} \vert + H_{header}$ | $L_{JSON}$ = JSON body length (bytes); $H_{header}$ ≈ 150–250 bytes | Bytes | --- ### 2.5 Engineering Utility This architecture is the **foundational skeleton** of: - **Smart Home Energy Monitors** (e.g., Sense, Emporia) - **Industrial SCADA Dashboards** (real-time plant telemetry) - **Hospital Patient Vitals Displays** (ward-level dashboards) - **Precision Agriculture Greenhouses** (humidity/temperature web dashboards) In every case, the pattern is identical: **MCU sensors → HTTP POST → Server → Browser**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Experimental Objective (Restated for Clarity) To design, implement, and demonstrate an **end-to-end IoT data pipeline** wherein the IoT Trainer kit (ESP8266/ESP32) reads **at least two sensor values** (e.g., DHT11 temperature and humidity), transmits them as a **JSON-encoded HTTP POST** request to a **locally hosted Flask server**, and renders the latest readings on a **responsive web dashboard** accessible at `http://:5000`. --- ### 3.2 Hardware & Software Prerequisites | Category | Item | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Hardware** | IoT Trainer Kit (ESP8266/ESP32 + DHT11 + LDR) | Data acquisition | | **Hardware** | Laptop/PC with Wi-Fi | Host the local server | | **Software** | Arduino IDE 2.x | Firmware upload | | **Software** | Python 3.9+ | Server runtime | | **Software** | Flask (`pip install flask`) | HTTP server framework | | **Software** | Arduino Libraries: `ESP8266WiFi`, `ESP8266HTTPClient`, `DHT sensor library`, `ArduinoJson` | MCU-side HTTP + JSON | | **Network** | Common Wi-Fi AP (2.4 GHz) | LAN bridge between MCU and server | --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Implementation — MCU (Client) Side #### Step 1: Include Required Libraries ```cpp #include // Wi-Fi driver for ESP8266 #include // HTTP client library #include // DHT11 sensor driver #include // JSON serialization #define DHTPIN D4 // GPIO2 — DHT11 DATA pin #define DHTTYPE DHT11 DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE); const char* ssid = "KTU_Lab_WiFi"; const char* password = "lab@1234"; const char* serverIP = "192.168.1.100"; // Laptop's LAN IP const int serverPort = 5000; ``` #### Step 2: Wi-Fi Initialization in `setup()` ```cpp void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); dht.begin(); WiFi.begin(ssid, password); Serial.print("Connecting to Wi-Fi"); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); } Serial.println(); Serial.print("Connected! IP: "); Serial.println(WiFi.localIP()); } ``` **Analytical Note:** The `while` loop blocks until the DHCP lease is acquired. The IP `WiFi.localIP()` is dynamically assigned and would typically fall in the `192.168.1.x` private range. #### Step 3: Sensor Reading in `loop()` ```cpp float temperature = dht.readTemperature(); // °C float humidity = dht.readHumidity(); // %RH int ldrRaw = analogRead(A0); // 0–1023 float voltage = (ldrRaw / 1023.0) * 3.3; // V (Formula #1) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **NaN Guard:** `dht.readTemperature()` can return `NaN` if the sensor is faulty. Always validate: `if (isnan(temperature)) return;` #### Step 4: JSON Payload Construction ```cpp StaticJsonDocument<200> doc; doc["deviceId"] = "KTU_TRAINER_01"; doc["temperature"] = temperature; doc["humidity"] = humidity; doc["lightVolt"] = voltage; doc["timestamp"] = millis(); String jsonPayload; serializeJson(doc, jsonPayload); ``` The resulting `jsonPayload` string is: ```json {"deviceId":"KTU_TRAINER_01","temperature":28.50,"humidity":65.00,"lightVolt":2.41,"timestamp":12345} ``` #### Step 5: HTTP POST Transmission ```cpp if (WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED) { HTTPClient http; String url = "http://" + String(serverIP) + ":" + String(serverPort) + "/data"; http.begin(url); http.addHeader("Content-Type", "application/json"); int httpCode = http.POST(jsonPayload); if (httpCode > 0) { Serial.printf("POST OK, Code: %d\n", httpCode); Serial.println(http.getString()); } else { Serial.printf("POST Failed, Error: %s\n", http.errorToString(httpCode).c_str()); } http.end(); } delay(5000); // 5-second sampling interval ``` --- ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Implementation — Server Side (Python Flask) #### Step 1: Server Skeleton & Imports ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify, render_template from collections import deque from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) reading_store = deque(maxlen=50) # Stores last 50 readings in RAM ``` #### Step 2: POST Endpoint to Receive Sensor Data ```python @app.route('/data', methods=['POST']) def receive_data(): if not request.is_json: return jsonify({"error": "Content-Type must be application/json"}), 400 payload = request.get_json() # ---- Validation Block ---- required = ['temperature', 'humidity'] if not all(k in payload for k in required): return jsonify({"error": "Missing fields"}), 400 record = { "deviceId": payload.get("deviceId", "unknown"), "temperature": float(payload['temperature']), "humidity": float(payload['humidity']), "lightVolt": float(payload.get('lightVolt', 0.0)), "receivedAt": datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") } reading_store.appendleft(record) return jsonify({"status": "success", "stored": len(reading_store)}), 201 ``` #### Step 3: GET Endpoint to Serve Dashboard ```python @app.route('/') def dashboard(): return render_template('index.html') ``` #### Step 4: AJAX Endpoint for Live Refresh ```python @app.route('/api/latest') def latest(): if not reading_store: return jsonify({"error": "No data yet"}), 404 return jsonify(reading_store[0]) if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True) ``` > [!NOTE] > `host='0.0.0.0'` binds the server to **all network interfaces**, allowing the ESP board to reach it via the laptop's LAN IP, not just `localhost`. --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Implementation — Web Dashboard (HTML + JS) ```html KTU IoT Trainer — Live Dashboard

🌡 KTU IoT Live Monitor

Temperature

-- °C

Humidity

-- %

Light Voltage

-- V

Waiting for first reading…

``` --- ### 3.6 Expected Output & Verification Checklist | Verification Step | Expected Observation | Pass/Fail | |---|---|---| | MCU Serial Monitor | `POST OK, Code: 201` printed every 5 s | ✅ | | Flask Terminal | `192.168.1.x - - [date] "POST /data HTTP/1.1" 201 -` | ✅ | | Browser at `http://192.168.1.100:5000` | Dashboard cards show live °C / % / V values | ✅ | | Browser DevTools → Network Tab | `GET /api/latest` polling every 3 s with 200 OK | ✅ | | Sensor unplugged | `dht.readTemperature()` returns NaN, server returns 400 | ✅ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENS["Sensor Layer (IoT Trainer Kit)"] s1["DHT11 Sensor"] s2["LDR Sensor"] s3["MQ-2 Gas Sensor"] end subgraph MCU["MCU Layer (ESP8266 / ESP32)"] m1["ADC & GPIO Drivers"] m2["JSON Serializer"] m3["HTTP Client Stack"] m4["Wi-Fi Driver"] end subgraph NET["Network Layer (LAN / Wi-Fi AP)"] n1["Access Point Router"] end subgraph SRV["Server Layer (Laptop - Flask)"] v1["HTTP Server - Port 5000"] v2["JSON Parser & Validator"] v3["In-Memory Store - deque"] end subgraph UI["Presentation Layer (Web Browser)"] w1["HTML Dashboard"] w2["AJAX Polling - setInterval"] w3["DOM Update Loop"] end s1 --> m1 s2 --> m1 s3 --> m1 m1 --> m2 m2 --> m3 m3 --> m4 m4 --> n1 n1 --> v1 v1 --> v2 v2 --> v3 v3 --> v1 v1 --> n1 n1 --> w1 w1 --> w2 w2 --> n1 n1 --> v1 v1 --> w3 w3 --> w1 ``` --- ### 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant MCU as ESP8266 MCU participant AP as Wi-Fi Router participant SRV as Flask Server :5000 participant BR as Browser Client Note over MCU: Read DHT11 + LDR every 5 s MCU->>AP: Wi-Fi Association (SSID + WPA2-PSK) AP-->>MCU: DHCP Lease Assigned (192.168.1.x) MCU->>SRV: TCP SYN (port 5000) SRV-->>MCU: TCP SYN-ACK MCU->>SRV: TCP ACK MCU->>SRV: POST /data HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json
{"temperature":28.5,"humidity":65} SRV->>SRV: Parse JSON & Validate SRV->>SRV: Append to deque(maxlen=50) SRV-->>MCU: HTTP/1.1 201 Created
{"status":"success"} Note over BR: User opens http://192.168.1.100:5000 BR->>SRV: GET / HTTP/1.1 SRV-->>BR: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
(HTML dashboard) loop Every 3 seconds BR->>SRV: GET /api/latest HTTP/1.1 SRV-->>BR: HTTP/1.1 200 OK
(JSON latest record) BR->>BR: Update DOM with new values end ``` --- ### 4.3 Data Flow — From Sensor Pin to Browser Pixel ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Physical Phenomenon
e.g., 28 °C air, 60 %RH"] B["DHT11 Capacitive Sensor"] C["Digital Signal
(single-wire protocol)"] D["GPIO Pin D4 Reads Bits"] E["dht.readTemperature()"] F["Float value: 28.50"] G["ArduinoJson Object
key:value pairs"] H["String: JSON payload"] I["HTTPClient.POST(url, payload)"] J["TCP Packet over Wi-Fi
(HTTP/1.1 headers + body)"] K["Flask request.get_json()"] L["Python dict"] M["deque.appendleft(record)"] N["In-Memory Storage"] O["/api/latest returns jsonify(...)"] P["Browser fetch() Promise"] Q["DOM innerText update"] R["User sees 28.50 °C on screen"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L --> M --> N --> O --> P --> Q --> R ``` --- ### 4.4 Lab Setup — Physical Wiring Reference ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TRAINER["IoT Trainer Board"] ESP["ESP8266 / ESP32 Module"] DHT["DHT11 Module"] LDR["LDR + 10kΩ Pull-down"] end ESP -- "GPIO D4 (Data)" --> DHT ESP -- "3.3 V + GND" --> DHT ESP -- "Analog Pin A0" --> LDR ESP -- "5 V via USB" --> LAPTOP LAPTOP["Laptop - Flask Server :5000"] ESP -. "Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz" .-> AP["Router / Hotspot"] AP -. "Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz" .-> LAPTOP ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *List any three HTTP methods and state one use-case for each in an IoT sensor-data upload pipeline.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Method | Use-Case in this Experiment | |---|---| | **POST** | Upload sensor readings from ESP8266 to the server's `/data` endpoint (non-idempotent, contains request body). | | **GET** | Fetch the dashboard HTML page at `/` and fetch the latest reading at `/api/latest` (idempotent, no body). | | **PUT** (acceptable 3rd) | Replace/update an existing device record in the server store by unique `deviceId`. | **Mark Split:** 1 mark per correct method + use-case pair. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional II]** *Why is JSON preferred over plain text or XML for IoT HTTP payloads? Give two reasons.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Lightweight & Compact:** JSON has minimal overhead — no closing tags like XML (`28.5` → `"temp":28.5`). This reduces bandwidth, critical for resource-constrained MCUs. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **Native Parsing in Both Worlds:** Python's `json` module and Arduino's `ArduinoJson` library parse JSON in O(n) time with minimal RAM (~200 bytes for `StaticJsonDocument<200>`). JSON also maps directly to Python `dict` and JavaScript `object`, making it **language-agnostic** across MCU, server, and browser. **(1.5 marks)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an HTTP-based IoT system that pushes sensor data from an IoT Trainer kit to a local Flask server. Label all layers. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Write the complete Arduino sketch (ESP8266) to read temperature from a DHT11 sensor and POST it as JSON to `http://192.168.1.100:5000/data` every 5 seconds. Assume SSID `KTU_Lab` and password `ktu@2024`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):** The system comprises **four layers**: 1. **Perception Layer (Sensors):** DHT11, LDR, MQ-2 mounted on the trainer kit convert physical phenomena into electrical signals. **(1 mark)** 2. **Network Layer (MCU + Wi-Fi):** ESP8266 reads sensor data via GPIO/ADC, serializes it into JSON using `ArduinoJson`, and pushes it over HTTP POST through the onboard Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n radio at **2.4 GHz**. The TCP three-way handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`) establishes a reliable connection on **port 5000**. **(2 marks)** 3. **Processing Layer (Flask Server):** The Flask `@app.route('/data', methods=['POST'])` endpoint receives JSON via `request.get_json()`, validates required keys, and stores the reading in a `deque(maxlen=50)`. The server binds to `0.0.0.0:5000` to accept LAN traffic. **(2 marks)** 4. **Application Layer (Web Dashboard):** HTML/CSS frontend served at `GET /`, with JavaScript `setInterval` polling `/api/latest` every 3 seconds via `fetch()` API. The DOM is updated with `document.getElementById(...).innerText`. **(2 marks)** **ASCII Block Diagram (for answer sheet):** ``` [Sensors] → [ESP8266+Wi-Fi] → [Flask Server] → [Browser Dashboard] ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ DHT11 JSON Encode JSON Parse AJAX Poll LDR HTTP POST deque store DOM Update MQ-2 TCP :5000 render HTML Chart.js ``` **(b) Complete Arduino Sketch (7 marks):** ```cpp #include #include #include #include #define DHTPIN D4 #define DHTTYPE DHT11 DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE); const char* ssid = "KTU_Lab"; const char* password = "ktu@2024"; const char* srvURL = "http://192.168.1.100:5000/data"; void setup() { Serial.begin(115200); dht.begin(); WiFi.begin(ssid, password); while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); } Serial.println("\nWi-Fi Connected"); } void loop() { float t = dht.readTemperature(); if (isnan(t)) { Serial.println("DHT11 read failed"); return; } StaticJsonDocument<128> doc; doc["deviceId"] = "ESP8266_A1"; doc["temperature"] = t; String body; serializeJson(doc, body); if (WiFi.status() == WL_CONNECTED) { HTTPClient http; http.begin(srvURL); http.addHeader("Content-Type", "application/json"); int code = http.POST(body); Serial.printf("HTTP Code: %d\n", code); http.end(); } delay(5000); } ``` **Mark Split:** Library includes (1), Wi-Fi init (1), sensor read + validation (2), JSON serialize (1), HTTP POST + header (1), loop delay (0.5), comments/structure (0.5). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Alternative Pattern]** *(a)* Differentiate between **HTTP** and **MQTT** protocols for IoT communication. Which is more suitable for low-bandwidth, battery-powered devices and why? **(7 marks)** *(b)* Design and write the complete Flask server-side code to receive sensor data via `POST /data`, store the last 20 readings in memory, and serve a JSON response at `GET /api/data` containing the most recent reading. Include proper HTTP status codes. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) HTTP vs MQTT (7 marks):** | Parameter | HTTP (REST) | MQTT | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Request-Response (Client → Server) | Publish-Subscribe (Broker-mediated) | | **Transport** | TCP | TCP (port 1883) / WebSocket (8883) | | **Header Overhead** | Large (~200–800 bytes per request) | Tiny fixed 2-byte header | | **Power Consumption** | High (TCP handshake per request) | Low (persistent connection, QoS levels) | | **Latency** | Higher (new connection each time) | Lower (keep-alive connection) | | **Best For** | Web dashboards, cloud APIs | Battery sensors, telemetry, mobile push | | **Suitability for Low-Power** | ❌ Poor — needs full TCP handshake | ✅ Excellent — persistent connection + QoS 0/1/2 | **Conclusion:** **MQTT** is more suitable for low-bandwidth, battery-powered IoT devices because its **2-byte fixed header** and **persistent broker connection** drastically reduce radio-on time, conserving battery by **~60–80%** compared to HTTP's repeated TCP handshakes. **(1 mark for verdict)** **(b) Complete Flask Code (7 marks):** ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify from collections import deque from datetime import datetime app = Flask(__name__) store = deque(maxlen=20) # Last 20 readings @app.route('/data', methods=['POST']) def ingest(): if not request.is_json: return jsonify({"error": "application/json required"}), 400 p = request.get_json() if 'temperature' not in p: return jsonify({"error": "temperature field missing"}), 400 record = { "temperature": float(p['temperature']), "humidity": float(p.get('humidity', 0.0)), "deviceId": p.get('deviceId', 'unknown'), "receivedAt": datetime.now().isoformat() } store.appendleft(record) return jsonify({"status": "ok", "count": len(store)}), 201 @app.route('/api/data', methods=['GET']) def latest(): if not store: return jsonify({"error": "no readings yet"}), 404 return jsonify(store[0]), 200 @app.route('/api/history', methods=['GET']) def history(): return jsonify(list(store)), 200 if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) ``` **Mark Split:** Imports + deque init (1), POST route with JSON validation (2), Status codes 400/201 (1), GET latest route + 404 (2), Run config host/port (1). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. ❌ **Forgetting `http.addHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")`** → server returns **400 Bad Request** and examiner deducts **2 marks** for "missing protocol compliance". > 2. ❌ **Binding server to `127.0.0.1` instead of `0.0.0.0`** → ESP board cannot reach the server; mark deduction of **1.5 marks** for "wrong binding address". > 3. ❌ **Not validating `isnan(temperature)`** → garbage NaN propagates to server; examiner deducts **1 mark** for "missing input validation". > 4. ❌ **Confusing POST and GET** — using GET to send sensor data (which violates REST semantics) → **1 mark deduction**. > 5. ❌ **Using Serial.print with `delay(1000)`** instead of `delay(5000)` → examiner may deduct for not justifying sampling interval choice based on application requirement. > 6. ❌ **Writing `app.run(host='localhost')` without `'0.0.0.0'`** → only loopback requests succeed; **critical error** flagged by examiner. > 7. ❌ **Forgetting to import `request` from flask** → NameError at runtime; deduction for "incomplete import statements". --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **HTTP** is a **stateless, request-response** application-layer protocol operating over **TCP port 80** (or custom like 5000). - ✅ The **4-layer IoT architecture** is: Perception → Network → Processing → Application. - ✅ **DHT11** uses a **single-wire digital protocol**; outputs temperature in **°C** and humidity in **%RH**. - ✅ **ESP8266 ADC** is **10-bit** (range 0–1023); ESP32 ADC is **12-bit** (range 0–4095). Reference voltage = **3.3 V**. - ✅ Voltage conversion: $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC_{count}}{ADC_{max}} \times 3.3$. - ✅ **JSON** is preferred over XML/text due to **low overhead** and **language-agnostic parsing**. - ✅ **POST** = non-idempotent (creates new record); **GET** = idempotent (fetches without modifying). - ✅ **HTTP 201** = resource created (use after successful POST); **HTTP 200** = success (use after GET); **HTTP 400** = bad input; **HTTP 404** = not found. - ✅ Flask server must bind to **`0.0.0.0`** (not `localhost`) to accept LAN requests from the ESP board. - ✅ Always add header **`Content-Type: application/json`** when POSTing JSON. - ✅ Sampling interval of **5 seconds** balances responsiveness vs MCU power consumption. - ✅ **AJAX polling** via `setInterval(() => fetch('/api/latest'), 3000)` provides near-real-time dashboard updates without page reloads. - ✅ **MQTT** is more power-efficient than HTTP for low-bandwidth sensors (2-byte header vs ~200-byte HTTP header). - ✅ Standard HTTP default port is **80**, but local servers use **5000 (Flask)** or **3000 (Node.js)** to avoid requiring root privileges. - ✅ **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n** operates at **2.4 GHz** ISM band; ESP8266/ESP32 do not support 5 GHz. - ✅ The full data path is: **Physical Signal → Sensor → ADC/GPIO → MCU JSON Serialize → Wi-Fi TCP → HTTP POST → Flask JSON Parse → In-Memory Store → AJAX GET → DOM Update → Browser Display**.

topics.title: Write a script to control an LED and relay module in the IoT Trainer kit. Implement a scenario where the relay controls a fan based on temperature readings.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a script to control an LED and relay module in the IoT Trainer kit. Implement a scenario where the relay controls a fan based on temperature readings.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2 — LED, Relay & Temperature-Triggered Fan Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature-Triggered Actuation System (TTAS):** A closed-loop IoT edge application in which a microcontroller samples an analog or digital temperature sensor, executes a threshold-based decision algorithm, and drives two electromechanical actuators — a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** as a visual status indicator and an **electromagnetic Relay Module** as a high-power switching element that energizes a DC/AC cooling fan. This experiment demonstrates **GPIO output control**, **sensor-actuator coupling**, and **event-driven embedded logic** on a single IoT Trainer Kit node.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAML507 — IoT Lab)**, this experiment falls under the domain of *embedded actuation and sensor integration*, and is mapped to the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO2:** Implement IoT applications using microcontroller/single-board computers with appropriate sensor and actuator interfacing.
- **CO3:** Develop scripts for data acquisition, threshold-based control, and actuator switching.
- **CO4:** Demonstrate real-time monitoring and control of physical parameters using IoT hardware.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Smart Room Thermostat**
> Imagine a hotel room with a wall-mounted thermostat. The thermostat has a small green **LED** that glows when the room is comfortable, and turns red when the air-conditioner/fan kicks in. Behind the panel, a **relay** (an electrically-controlled mechanical switch) clicks ON, sending mains power to the ceiling fan. Your IoT Trainer Kit replicates this exact behaviour using a temperature sensor (the "thermostat"), an LED (the "status light"), and a relay module (the "heavy-duty switch") — all orchestrated by a tiny script running on an ESP32/Arduino.

### 1.3 Hardware Building Blocks of the System

| Block | Component | Function | Standard Specification |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| **Controller** | ESP32 / Arduino Uno | Runs the control script | **3.3 V** (ESP32) or **5 V** (Arduino) GPIO |
| **Temperature Sensor** | DHT11 / DHT22 / LM35 | Converts thermal energy to electrical signal | DHT11: 0–50 °C, ±2 °C accuracy |
| **LED** | Standard 5 mm LED | Visual status indicator | Forward voltage **V_f ≈ 2 V**, I_f = **20 mA** |
| **Current-Limiting Resistor** | 220 Ω – 330 Ω | Protects LED from over-current | R = (V_supply − V_f) / I_f |
| **Relay Module** | 1-channel, opto-isolated | Switches high-power load | Coil **5 V**, Contact **250 V AC / 10 A** |
| **Fan** | 5 V DC / 230 V AC fan | Cooling actuator | DC 5 V @ 200 mA or AC 230 V |

### 1.4 Key Physical/Electrical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **V_CC (microcontroller logic level):** **3.3 V** (ESP32) or **5 V** (Arduino Uno)
> - **DHT11 sampling rate:** **1 Hz** (one reading per second)
> - **Relay coil energize voltage:** typically **5 V DC** (some 3.3 V modules exist)
> - **Standard threshold temperature (T_th):** **30 °C** (configurable in code)
> - **Hysteresis band (ΔT):** **±2 °C** to prevent relay chattering
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and the "How"

The script implements a **finite state machine (FSM)** with two states: `FAN_OFF` and `FAN_ON`. The transition between states is governed by the **current temperature reading** and a **hysteresis threshold**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hysteresis?**
> If we used a single threshold (e.g., "turn ON at 30 °C, turn OFF at 29.9 °C"), the relay would oscillate rapidly near the boundary, causing mechanical wear and electrical noise. Adding a **hysteresis band** (e.g., ON at ≥30 °C, OFF at ≤28 °C) ensures stable switching — a concept borrowed from the **Schmitt trigger** in analog electronics.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow (Bulleted Decomposition)

1. **Power-On / `setup()` phase**
   - Initialize the **Serial Monitor** at **115200 baud** for debugging.
   - Configure the **LED pin** as `OUTPUT` (default LOW — LED off).
   - Configure the **Relay pin** as `OUTPUT` (default LOW — relay de-energized, fan off).
   - Initialize the **DHT sensor** object and call `dht.begin()`.
   - *Why?* Setting the relay pin LOW at boot prevents an unwanted fan startup during initialization (fail-safe state).

2. **Main Loop Iteration**
   - Read humidity and temperature from the DHT sensor using `dht.readTemperature()`.
   - Validate the reading using `isnan()` to detect sensor communication errors.
   - If the reading is valid, apply the threshold logic:
     - **Condition A — Heat Detected:** If `temperature ≥ T_ON (30 °C)` → Drive Relay HIGH (fan ON) and LED HIGH (red indicator).
     - **Condition B — Cooled Down:** Else if `temperature ≤ T_OFF (28 °C)` → Drive Relay LOW (fan OFF) and LED LOW (green indicator).
     - **Condition C — Hysteresis Zone:** If `28 °C < temperature < 30 °C` → Maintain previous state (no change).

3. **Telemetry Output**
   - Print the current temperature, humidity, fan state, and LED state to the Serial Monitor.
   - *Why?* This emulates the **publishing** step of an IoT pipeline (in a real deployment, this data would be sent to MQTT / cloud).

4. **Sampling Delay**
   - Wait **2000 ms** (2 seconds) between iterations to respect the DHT11's **1 Hz** sampling constraint and to debounce the system.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | LED Current-Limiting Resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f}$ | Ω ; $V_f \approx 2$ V (red), $I_f = 20$ mA |
| 2 | ADC Resolution (Arduino Uno) | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5\text{ V}}{1024}$ | ≈ 4.88 mV per step |
| 3 | LM35 Linear Scaling | $T (^{\circ}\text{C}) = V_{out} (\text{mV}) / 10$ | Output: 10 mV per °C |
| 4 | DHT11 Temperature Range | $0 \leq T \leq 50$ | °C, ±2 °C accuracy |
| 5 | DHT11 Humidity Range | $20 \leq H \leq 90$ | % RH, ±5 % accuracy |
| 6 | Hysteresis Threshold | $T_{ON} = 30$ °C, $T_{OFF} = 28$ °C, $\Delta T = 2$ °C | Prevents relay chattering |
| 7 | Relay Coil Power | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \times I_{coil}$ | Typically 5 V × 70 mA = 0.35 W |
| 8 | Fan Power (DC 5 V) | $P_{fan} = 5\text{ V} \times 200\text{ mA} = 1$ W | Approximate |
| 9 | Sampling Interval | $\Delta t \geq 2$ s | DHT11 minimum period |
| 10 | Opto-isolator Isolation Voltage | $V_{ISO} \geq 5$ kV | Protects MCU from back-EMF |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Buildings (HVAC):** The same threshold-control principle governs commercial Building Management Systems (BMS) that switch chillers and exhaust fans.
- **Industrial Ovens & Server Racks:** A relay-controlled fan cools equipment when internal temperature crosses a safety threshold — preventing thermal runaway.
- **Greenhouse Automation:** Fan ventilation triggered by temperature is fundamental to **precision agriculture**.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Battery Packs:** Thermal management systems use relay-switched coolant fans to maintain Li-ion cells between **15–35 °C** for longevity.
- **Edge AI Inference Boxes:** Inference accelerators (e.g., Jetson Nano) use fan control via GPIO to prevent thermal throttling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hardware Derivation — Pin Assignment and Circuit Topology

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Trainer Kit Standard Pinout Convention (assumed):**
> - DHT11 **DATA** → GPIO 4 (Arduino D2 / ESP32 D4)
> - LED (+) → GPIO 5 via 220 Ω resistor → GND
> - Relay **IN** → GPIO 6
> - Relay **VCC** → 5 V, **GND** → GND
> - Fan (+) → Relay COM, Fan (−) → external supply GND (or AC L/N for mains fan)

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the LED Series Resistor

Given:
- $V_{supply} = 5$ V (Arduino logic)
- $V_f$ (red LED forward voltage) = 2.0 V
- $I_f$ (desired forward current) = 15 mA (safe operating point)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED loop:

$$
V_{supply} = V_R + V_f
$$

$$
V_R = V_{supply} - V_f = 5\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 3.0\text{ V}
$$

Applying Ohm's Law:

$$
R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{3.0\text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}} = 200\ \Omega
$$

> **Conclusion:** A standard **220 Ω** resistor (next E12 value up) is used to provide a safety margin. Power dissipation: $P_R = I_f^2 \times R = (0.015)^2 \times 220 = 49.5$ mW → use a standard **¼ W** resistor.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Hysteresis Thresholds

To prevent relay chattering around a setpoint $T_{set} = 29$ °C with a band $\Delta T = 2$ °C:

$$
T_{ON} = T_{set} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{2} = 29 + 1 = 30\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{OFF} = T_{set} - \dfrac{\Delta T}{2} = 29 - 1 = 28\ ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

This produces a **dead-band** of 2 °C where the relay state remains frozen — a digital equivalent of a Schmitt trigger.

## 3.2 Algorithmic Derivation — From FSM to Arduino Code

### 3.2.1 State Transition Table

| Current State | Temperature Reading | Next State | LED | Relay | Fan |
|---------------|---------------------|------------|-----|-------|-----|
| `FAN_OFF` | $T \geq 30$ °C | `FAN_ON` | HIGH (red) | HIGH | ON |
| `FAN_OFF` | $T < 30$ °C | `FAN_OFF` | LOW (green) | LOW | OFF |
| `FAN_ON` | $T \leq 28$ °C | `FAN_OFF` | LOW (green) | LOW | OFF |
| `FAN_ON` | $T > 28$ °C | `FAN_ON` | HIGH (red) | HIGH | ON |

### 3.2.2 Complete Arduino/ESP32 Compatible Script

```cpp
// ============================================================
// PCAML507 — Module Experiment 2
// Temperature-Triggered Fan Control with LED Status Indicator
// Compatible with: Arduino Uno / ESP32 / NodeMCU (IoT Trainer Kit)
// ============================================================

#include "DHT.h"                  // Adafruit DHT sensor library

// ---------- PIN DEFINITIONS ----------
#define DHTPIN    4               // DHT11 data pin
#define DHTTYPE   DHT11           // Sensor type
#define LED_PIN   5               // Status LED pin (with 220Ω resistor)
#define RELAY_PIN 6               // Relay module IN pin

// ---------- THRESHOLD CONFIGURATION ----------
const float T_ON  = 30.0;         // Turn fan ON at or above this temperature
const float T_OFF = 28.0;         // Turn fan OFF at or below this temperature

// ---------- STATE VARIABLE ----------
bool fanState = false;            // false = OFF, true = ON (hysteresis memory)

// ---------- SENSOR OBJECT ----------
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);

// ============================================================
// SETUP — runs once at power-on / reset
// ============================================================
void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);                       // Initialize Serial Monitor
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT);

  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);                 // Fail-safe: LED off at boot
  digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW);               // Fail-safe: Relay off at boot

  dht.begin();                                // Initialize DHT sensor
  Serial.println("IoT Trainer Kit — Temperature-Triggered Fan Control");
  Serial.println("---------------------------------------------------");
}

// ============================================================
// LOOP — runs repeatedly
// ============================================================
void loop() {
  // -------- Step 1: Acquire sensor reading --------
  float temperature = dht.readTemperature();   // °C
  float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();      // % RH

  // -------- Step 2: Validate reading --------
  if (isnan(temperature) || isnan(humidity)) {
    Serial.println("[ERROR] Failed to read from DHT sensor!");
    delay(2000);
    return;
  }

  // -------- Step 3: Apply hysteresis threshold logic --------
  if (temperature >= T_ON) {
    fanState = true;                          // Activate cooling
  } else if (temperature <= T_OFF) {
    fanState = false;                         // Deactivate cooling
  }
  // Else: in the dead-band (28 < T < 30) → retain previous state

  // -------- Step 4: Drive actuators --------
  digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, fanState ? HIGH : LOW);
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN,   fanState ? HIGH : LOW);

  // -------- Step 5: Telemetry / Logging --------
  Serial.print("Temp: ");
  Serial.print(temperature, 1);
  Serial.print(" °C  |  Humidity: ");
  Serial.print(humidity, 1);
  Serial.print(" %  |  Fan: ");
  Serial.print(fanState ? "ON " : "OFF");
  Serial.print("  |  LED: ");
  Serial.println(fanState ? "RED" : "GREEN");

  // -------- Step 6: Sampling delay --------
  delay(2000);                                // Respect DHT11 1 Hz rate
}
```

### 3.2.3 Line-by-Line Logical Explanation

| Line / Block | Purpose | Engineering Rationale |
|--------------|---------|------------------------|
| `#include "DHT.h"` | Pulls in the Adafruit DHT library | Avoids writing the 1-wire protocol manually |
| `#define DHTPIN 4` | Symbolic constant for GPIO | Improves code readability & maintainability |
| `const float T_ON = 30.0` | Upper threshold | Defines the activation boundary |
| `bool fanState = false` | Hysteresis memory | Stores previous state across loop iterations |
| `pinMode(RELAY_PIN, OUTPUT)` | Configures pin direction | Required before any `digitalWrite()` call |
| `digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW)` in `setup()` | Fail-safe initialization | Prevents fan kick-on during boot |
| `dht.readTemperature()` | Reads calibrated °C value | Internally converts raw bits to engineering units |
| `isnan(temperature)` | NaN (Not-a-Number) check | Handles sensor cable disconnect / CRC errors |
| `if (temperature >= T_ON)` | Upper threshold trigger | Activates fan when it gets hot |
| `else if (temperature <= T_OFF)` | Lower threshold trigger | Deactivates fan when sufficiently cool |
| *(implicit else)* | Hysteresis dead-band | No state change — eliminates relay chattering |
| `digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, fanState ? HIGH : LOW)` | Conditional actuator drive | Ternary operator for compact one-liner |
| `delay(2000)` | Sampling interval | DHT11 minimum sampling period is **1 s**; **2 s** adds safety margin |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Expected Serial Monitor Output

Assuming the temperature gradually rises from 25 °C to 32 °C, the expected log would be:

| Time (s) | Temperature (°C) | Humidity (%) | Fan State | LED State |
|----------|------------------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| 0 | 25.0 | 60.0 | OFF | GREEN |
| 2 | 26.5 | 59.0 | OFF | GREEN |
| 4 | 28.0 | 58.0 | OFF | GREEN |
| 6 | 29.0 | 57.0 | OFF | GREEN (dead-band) |
| 8 | 30.0 | 56.0 | **ON** | **RED** |
| 10 | 30.5 | 55.0 | ON | RED |
| 12 | 29.5 | 55.0 | ON | RED (dead-band) |
| 14 | 28.0 | 56.0 | **OFF** | **GREEN** |

> **Observation:** The fan remained ON between t = 8 s and t = 14 s even though the temperature dipped into the 28–30 °C band between t = 12 s — proving the hysteresis logic is working correctly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[5V DC Power Supply] --> MCU[Microcontroller ESP32 / Arduino Uno]
    DHT[DHT11 Temperature Sensor] -->|Digital Single-Wire| MCU
    MCU -->|GPIO5 + 220Ω| LED[Status LED Red and Green]
    MCU -->|GPIO6| RLY[Relay Module 5V Coil]
    RLY -->|COM and NO contacts| FAN[Cooling Fan 5V DC or 230V AC]
    MCU -.->|Serial 115200 baud| PC[PC Serial Monitor / Cloud MQTT]
```

## 4.2 Logical Flowchart of the Control Script

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start setup] --> B[Init Serial 115200]
    B --> C[LED pin OUTPUT LOW]
    C --> D[Relay pin OUTPUT LOW]
    D --> E[dht.begin]
    E --> F[Enter loop]
    F --> G[Read temperature from DHT]
    G --> H{isnan check}
    H -->|Invalid| I[Print error and delay 2s]
    I --> F
    H -->|Valid| J{T >= 30°C}
    J -->|Yes| K[fanState = true]
    J -->|No| L{T <= 28°C}
    L -->|Yes| M[fanState = false]
    L -->|No| N[Keep previous fanState]
    K --> O[Drive Relay HIGH and LED HIGH]
    M --> P[Drive Relay LOW and LED LOW]
    N --> O
    O --> Q[Print telemetry to Serial]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Delay 2000 ms]
    R --> F
```

## 4.3 Hardware Wiring Diagram (Logical Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCU_Board[Microcontroller Unit]
        GPIO4[GPIO4 DHT DATA]
        GPIO5[GPIO5 LED Pin]
        GPIO6[GPIO6 RELAY IN]
        V5[5V Rail]
        GND1[GND]
    end

    subgraph DHT_Module[DHT11 Sensor]
        DHT_VCC[VCC]
        DHT_DATA[DATA]
        DHT_GND[GND]
    end

    subgraph LED_Branch[LED Indicator]
        R_220[220Ω Resistor]
        LED_ANODE[LED Anode +]
        LED_CATHODE[LED Cathode -]
    end

    subgraph Relay_Branch[Relay Module 1-Channel]
        R_VCC[VCC]
        R_IN[IN Signal]
        R_GND[GND]
        R_COM[COM]
        R_NO[NO Normally Open]
    end

    subgraph Load[High Power Load]
        FAN_POS[Fan + Terminal]
        FAN_NEG[Fan - Terminal]
    end

    V5 --> DHT_VCC
    GND1 --> DHT_GND
    GPIO4 --> DHT_DATA
    DHT_VCC --> DHT_DATA

    GPIO5 --> R_220 --> LED_ANODE
    LED_CATHODE --> GND1

    V5 --> R_VCC
    GND1 --> R_GND
    GPIO6 --> R_IN
    R_COM --> FAN_POS
    R_NO --> FAN_NEG
```

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram (FSM)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FAN_OFF
    FAN_OFF --> FAN_ON: temperature >= 30°C
    FAN_ON --> FAN_OFF: temperature <= 28°C
    FAN_OFF --> FAN_OFF: 28 < temp < 30
    FAN_ON --> FAN_ON: 28 < temp < 30
    note right of FAN_OFF: LED=LOW, Relay=LOW, Fan=OFF
    note right of FAN_ON: LED=HIGH, Relay=HIGH, Fan=ON
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the function of a relay module in an IoT system. Why is an opto-isolated relay preferred in microcontroller-based actuator circuits?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **relay module** is an electrically operated switch that allows a low-power microcontroller signal (typically 3.3 V or 5 V at a few milliamperes) to control a high-power load (such as a 230 V AC fan or a high-current DC motor) without any direct electrical connection between the control circuit and the load circuit. **(1 mark)**

It contains an **electromagnetic coil** that, when energized, pulls a movable armature to close (or open) a set of contacts, thereby switching the load. **(1 mark)**

An **opto-isolated relay** uses an internal **LED-phototransistor pair** to provide galvanic isolation (typically **>5 kV**) between the microcontroller and the relay coil. This protects the MCU from **back-EMF spikes**, voltage transients, and electrical noise generated by the inductive load, thereby increasing system reliability and safety. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is hysteresis in the context of relay-based temperature control? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hysteresis** in relay control refers to the intentional creation of **two distinct threshold temperatures** — a higher turn-ON threshold ($T_{ON}$) and a lower turn-OFF threshold ($T_{OFF}$) — separated by a dead-band $\Delta T = T_{ON} - T_{OFF}$. **(1 mark)**

It is necessary because if a single threshold were used, the relay would rapidly oscillate ON/OFF whenever the temperature hovered near the setpoint, causing **mechanical wear**, **contact pitting**, and **electrical noise**. **(1 mark)**

By introducing a dead-band, the relay state remains stable until the temperature crosses the opposite threshold — analogous to a **Schmitt trigger** in analog electronics. A typical value is $T_{ON} = 30$ °C and $T_{OFF} = 28$ °C, giving $\Delta T = 2$ °C. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a temperature-triggered IoT fan control system. List the major hardware components and their roles.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete Arduino/ESP32 script to read a DHT11 sensor and control an LED and a relay module such that the relay (and hence the fan) turns ON when the temperature exceeds 30 °C and turns OFF when it falls below 28 °C. Explain the hysteresis logic used.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **temperature-triggered IoT fan control system** is a closed-loop edge application that continuously monitors ambient temperature and actuates a cooling fan via a relay when the temperature crosses a defined threshold. **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):** The system comprises six major blocks:

1. **Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Provides regulated 5 V DC to the microcontroller and relay coil. **(1 mark)**
2. **Microcontroller (ESP32/Arduino Uno):** The "brain" of the system — runs the control script, reads sensor data, executes threshold logic, and drives actuators. **(1 mark)**
3. **Temperature Sensor (DHT11):** A digital sensor that uses a single-wire protocol to report temperature (°C) and humidity (% RH) to the MCU. **(1 mark)**
4. **LED Indicator:** A visual status indicator (with a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor) — typically red when the fan is ON and green when OFF. **(1 mark)**
5. **Relay Module:** An opto-isolated, 5 V coil-driven electromechanical switch with COM/NO/NC contacts that switch the high-power fan circuit. **(1 mark)**
6. **Cooling Fan (Actuator):** A 5 V DC or 230 V AC fan that provides forced-air cooling when energized. **(1 mark)**

**Data Flow:** Sensor → MCU (acquisition) → MCU (decision) → Relay (switching) → Fan (actuation), with the LED providing a parallel visual status.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

The complete script is as written in **Section 3.2.2** above. The model answer shall highlight the key segments:

**Hardware Pin Mapping (1 mark):**

| Component | Pin |
|-----------|-----|
| DHT11 DATA | GPIO 4 |
| LED (with 220 Ω) | GPIO 5 |
| Relay IN | GPIO 6 |

**Setup Function (1 mark):** Initializes Serial at 115200 baud, configures LED and Relay pins as `OUTPUT`, sets both `LOW` at boot for fail-safe operation, and calls `dht.begin()`.

**Loop Function — Sensor Reading (1 mark):** Calls `dht.readTemperature()` and validates with `isnan()`. On error, prints a message and retries after 2 s.

**Threshold Logic (3 marks):** The hysteresis FSM is implemented as:

```cpp
if (temperature >= T_ON) {           // T >= 30°C
    fanState = true;
} else if (temperature <= T_OFF) {   // T <= 28°C
    fanState = false;
}
// else: dead-band → retain previous state
```

The two thresholds create a **2 °C dead-band** that prevents the relay from chattering when the temperature hovers near a single setpoint. The boolean `fanState` variable preserves memory across loop iterations.

**Actuator Driving (1 mark):** `digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, fanState ? HIGH : LOW)` and the LED in parallel; `delay(2000)` ensures DHT11 sampling integrity.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a DHT11 temperature sensor. Describe its pin configuration, communication protocol, and key specifications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design and explain the circuit for interfacing a 5 V relay module with an ESP32 microcontroller. Include the role of the flyback diode and the opto-coupler. How would you modify the script to add a "manual override" button?

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

The **DHT11** is a low-cost digital temperature and humidity sensor that uses a **thermistor** (for temperature) and a **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** (for humidity), with an internal 8-bit microcontroller performing ADC conversion and producing a calibrated digital output. **(1 mark)**

**Pin Configuration (1 mark):**

| Pin | Function |
|-----|----------|
| 1 | VCC (3.3–5 V DC) |
| 2 | DATA (single-wire bidirectional) |
| 3 | NC (not connected) |
| 4 | GND |

**Communication Protocol (3 marks):** DHT11 uses a proprietary **single-wire (1-wire-like) protocol**:
- The MCU pulls DATA **LOW for ≥18 ms** (start signal), then releases.
- The sensor responds with a **LOW-HIGH "response" pulse** (80 µs each).
- The sensor then transmits **40 bits**: 8 bits integer humidity + 8 bits decimal humidity + 8 bits integer temperature + 8 bits decimal temperature + 8 bits checksum.
- Each bit is encoded as a **54 µs LOW + 26–28 µs HIGH (bit 0)** or **54 µs LOW + 70 µs HIGH (bit 1)**.
- The MCU decodes the pulse widths to reconstruct the data.

**Key Specifications (2 marks):**
- Temperature range: **0–50 °C**, accuracy **±2 °C**, resolution **1 °C**
- Humidity range: **20–90 % RH**, accuracy **±5 % RH**, resolution **1 %**
- Operating voltage: **3.3–5.5 V DC**
- Sampling rate: **1 Hz** (one reading per second)
- Current consumption: **0.5–2.5 mA** during measurement

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Relay Interfacing with ESP32 (5 marks):**

The relay module has **5 pins on the input side** (VCC, GND, IN, and sometimes JD-VCC/JD-GND for isolated supply) and **3 pins on the output side** (COM, NO, NC). **(1 mark)**

**Connection:**

| Relay Pin | ESP32 Connection |
|-----------|-------------------|
| VCC | 5 V (external) or 3.3 V (for 3.3 V modules) |
| GND | GND (common with ESP32) |
| IN | GPIO 6 (or any digital output) |

**Role of the Flyback Diode (2 marks):** The relay coil is an **inductor**. When the coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high-voltage reverse spike (back-EMF) that can damage the driving transistor. A **flyback diode** (typically 1N4007) placed in **anti-parallel** with the coil provides a safe path for this current, clamping the spike.

**Role of the Opto-Coupler (2 marks):** The opto-coupler (an LED-phototransistor pair inside the relay module) **galvanically isolates** the low-voltage MCU side from the high-voltage relay coil side. When the MCU drives IN HIGH, the internal LED emits light, turning ON the phototransistor, which then energizes the coil. This isolation prevents voltage spikes and noise from reaching the ESP32.

**Manual Override Modification (2 marks):** Add a **push-button** to GPIO 7 (with INPUT_PULLUP). Modify the script as:

```cpp
const int BTN_PIN = 7;
bool manualOverride = false;

void loop() {
  // Read button
  if (digitalRead(BTN_PIN) == LOW) {        // Active-low with pull-up
    manualOverride = !manualOverride;        // Toggle on press
    delay(250);                              // Debounce
  }

  // Override logic
  if (manualOverride) {
    fanState = true;                         // Force ON
  } else {
    // ... normal threshold logic ...
  }
  // ... drive actuators ...
}
```

This allows the user to force the fan ON regardless of temperature — useful for manual testing or during sensor failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing fail-safe initialization:** Forgetting to set Relay pin LOW in `setup()` causes fan kick-on at boot — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **No `isnan()` check:** If the DHT sensor cable is loose, `dht.readTemperature()` returns `NaN` — comparing `NaN >= 30` evaluates to **false**, so the fan never turns on. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing validation.
> 3. **Single-threshold logic (no hysteresis):** Using `if (temp > 30) ON; else OFF` is a major design flaw — expect to **lose up to 3 marks** in the threshold-logic sub-part.
> 4. **Wrong pull-up on DHT data line:** The DHT11 DATA pin requires a **4.7 kΩ pull-up to VCC**. Forgetting this causes intermittent sensor read failures — **lose 1 mark** in the circuit diagram.
> 5. **Confusing COM and NO terminals:** New students often wire the fan across COM and NC (normally closed), which means the fan runs **continuously** and turns OFF only when the relay energizes — the **inverse** of the intended behaviour. **Lose 1 mark** if the wiring is wrong.
> 6. **No series resistor on LED:** Connecting an LED directly across a GPIO pin (no current-limiting resistor) will damage the LED and may damage the MCU. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for missing the 220 Ω resistor in the circuit diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- ☐ The experiment demonstrates a **closed-loop sensor-actuator system** with a microcontroller as the decision-maker.
- ☐ **DHT11** is a digital single-wire temperature/humidity sensor with **±2 °C** accuracy in the 0–50 °C range.
- ☐ **DHT11 minimum sampling interval is 1 second**; use `delay(2000)` for safety margin.
- ☐ The **LED** is a low-power visual indicator, always wired with a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor**.
- ☐ The **relay module** is an **opto-isolated, 5 V coil-driven** electromechanical switch with **COM / NO / NC** contacts.
- ☐ The relay's **input** is driven by the MCU GPIO; the relay's **output contacts** switch the high-power load.
- ☐ **Fail-safe initialization** = `digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW)` inside `setup()` to prevent fan kick-on at boot.
- ☐ **Hysteresis thresholds:** $T_{ON} = 30$ °C, $T_{OFF} = 28$ °C, $\Delta T = 2$ °C — prevents relay chattering.
- ☐ The `fanState` boolean **preserves memory** across loop iterations — this is what makes the hysteresis work.
- ☐ The `isnan()` validation is **mandatory** — `NaN` comparisons always return false, causing silent failure.
- ☐ The **DHT11 DATA pin** requires an external **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistor** to VCC.
- ☐ The **flyback diode** inside the relay module protects against **back-EMF** from the inductive coil.
- ☐ A **manual override button** can be added using `INPUT_PULLUP` and a debounce delay.
- ☐ **Pin mapping (assumed trainer kit):** DHT → GPIO 4, LED → GPIO 5, Relay → GPIO 6.
- ☐ **Baud rate for Serial Monitor:** **115200** (standard for ESP32; Arduino Uno often uses 9600).
- ☐ **Course Outcomes covered:** **CO2, CO3, CO4** of PCAML507 — sensor-actuator integration, scripting, and real-time control.
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** Smart HVAC, server-rack cooling, greenhouse ventilation, EV battery thermal management, edge-AI thermal protection.
