topics.title: Utilize the PWM functionality in the IoT Trainer kit to control the speed of a motor. Implement a system where motor speed adjusts based on sensor input, such as light intensity.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Utilize the PWM functionality in the IoT Trainer kit to control the speed of a motor. Implement a system where motor speed adjusts based on sensor input, such as light intensity.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a digital signalling technique in which the width of a rectangular pulse is varied while keeping its period (or frequency) constant. In the context of an **IoT Trainer Kit** (Arduino Uno / NodeMCU / ESP32), PWM is generated by a hardware timer peripheral and output on dedicated pins (marked with a `~` symbol on Arduino, e.g., D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11).

**Motor Speed Control via PWM** exploits the electromechanical property of a brushed DC motor in which the **no-load angular velocity is approximately proportional to the average applied armature voltage**. Because a microcontroller GPIO cannot produce a true analog voltage, PWM simulates an analog level by rapidly switching the line between **HIGH (e.g., 5 V)** and **LOW (0 V)**. The effective (average) DC voltage seen by the motor armature is the duty-cycle-weighted value of the supply rail.

**Closed-Loop Light-Intensity Control** is implemented by sampling an **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** through a **voltage divider + ADC** path, mapping the digitized lux-equivalent value to a duty-cycle setpoint, and feeding that setpoint to the PWM channel driving a **DC motor via a driver IC such as L298N or a logic-level MOSFET (IRF520, TIP120)**.

| KTU 2024 Term | Working Definition (Exam-Safe Wording) |
|---|---|
| Duty Cycle | Ratio of ON-time to total period of a PWM waveform, expressed in % |
| Ton | Duration for which the PWM output stays HIGH in one cycle |
| T | Total period of one PWM cycle ($T = T_{on} + T_{off}$) |
| f_PWM | Switching frequency in Hz; Arduino default is **~490 Hz** on most pins, **~980 Hz** on D5 and D6 |
| Vavg | Time-averaged DC equivalent of the PWM signal delivered to the load |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bucket of Water" Analogy** — Imagine filling a 1-litre bucket using a tap that you can only turn fully ON or fully OFF (no half-open position). To deliver exactly *half* a litre per second, you flip the tap ON for 0.5 s and OFF for 0.5 s, repeating forever. A flowmeter downstream measures the *average* 0.5 L/s, even though no instantaneous half-flow ever existed. **PWM is exactly this trick** — the motor "sees" an average voltage proportional to the fraction of time the pin is HIGH.

A second analogy applies to the LDR:
> [!TIP]
> **The "Automatic Car Headlight" Analogy** — On a bright day, you dim the dashboard; at night, you crank it up. The LDR measures ambient light the way your eyes do, and the microcontroller (acting as the dimmer knob) increases the motor speed (headlight brightness analog) when darkness rises, and decreases it under bright sunlight. This is a **sensor-driven actuator loop** — the foundational paradigm of every IoT node.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Arduino Uno PWM Frequency:** **~490 Hz** on pins 3, 9, 10, 11; **~980 Hz** on pins 5, 6 (Timer0 exception for D5/D6).
- **ESP32 PWM Frequency:** Programmable, typically set between **1 kHz and 40 kHz**; LEDC channels 0–15.
- **NodeMCU (ESP8266) PWM Range:** `0` to `1023` (10-bit equivalent) at default **1 kHz**.
- **ADC Resolution (Arduino Uno):** **10-bit** $\Rightarrow$ digital range **0 to 1023**.
- **ADC Reference Voltage (default):** **Vref = 5.0 V** (or 3.3 V on ESP32).
- **LDR Typical Resistance Range:** ~**1 k$\Omega$ (bright light) to ~10 M$\Omega$ (darkness)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapped):** This experiment satisfies **CO2 — "Implement IoT applications using sensors, actuators, and PWM-based control"** and demonstrates Bloom Level **APPLY (L3)** and **ANALYZE (L4)** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why PWM Controls Motor Speed — The "How" Chain

1. A **brushed permanent-magnet DC motor** obeys the steady-state back-EMF equation:
   $$V_a = I_a R_a + E_b \quad \text{where} \quad E_b = K_e \omega$$
   - $V_a$ = armature voltage, $I_a$ = armature current, $R_a$ = armature resistance, $E_b$ = back-EMF, $K_e$ = voltage constant, $\omega$ = angular speed.

2. At **no load** ($I_a \approx 0$), the equation collapses to:
   $$V_a \approx E_b = K_e \omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega \approx \frac{V_a}{K_e}$$

3. Therefore, the **no-load speed is linearly proportional to applied voltage**. PWM, by varying the *average* applied voltage through the duty cycle, **directly controls the average speed**.

4. When a **load torque** is applied, the speed drops slightly because $V_a = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$ now has a non-zero $I_a R_a$ term, but the proportional control behaviour still holds.

## 2.2 Duty Cycle Derivation (Worked in Plain Text)

A PWM waveform is HIGH for time $T_{on}$ and LOW for time $T_{off}$ within one period $T$.

- **Duty Cycle (D):**
$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \quad \text{(unitless, usually expressed as percentage)}$$

- **Average Output Voltage (assuming rail = $V_{CC}$):**
$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot T_{on}}{T} = D \cdot V_{CC}$$

- **Average Power Delivered to a Resistive Load $R$:**
$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_{CC}^{2} \cdot D}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a high PWM frequency matters:** If $f_{PWM}$ is too low (say < 100 Hz), the motor will audibly "whine" or jerk because the armature inductance cannot smooth the current between pulses. The Arduino's default 490 Hz is a safe compromise: high enough for mechanical smoothing, low enough for switching losses to remain negligible.

## 2.3 LDR Voltage Divider — Sensor Side

A potential divider with the LDR ($R_{LDR}$) and a fixed reference resistor $R_{ref}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{R_{LDR} + R_{ref}}$$

- **Bright light** $\Rightarrow$ $R_{LDR}$ **decreases** $\Rightarrow$ $V_{out}$ **increases**.
- **Darkness** $\Rightarrow$ $R_{LDR}$ **increases** $\Rightarrow$ $V_{out}$ **decreases**.

The microcontroller reads this $V_{out}$ via the ADC.

## 2.4 ADC Quantization

For an **N-bit ADC** with reference $V_{ref}$:

$$\text{Digital Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{N} - 1) \right\rfloor$$

For Arduino Uno: $N = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$:

$$\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{5} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor$$

## 2.5 Sensor-to-PWM Mapping (The Core Control Law)

The control loop in this experiment implements a **direct, monotonic mapping**:

$$\text{PWM}_{duty} \;(\%) = f(\text{ADC}_{LDR})$$

The simplest valid choice is a **linear map** (more light → slower motor, or vice versa, depending on application semantics):

$$\text{PWM}_{duty} \;(\%) = \left(\frac{\text{ADC}_{LDR}}{1023}\right) \times 100$$

For a **darkness-driven speed-up** (e.g., exhaust fan in a darkroom that must spin faster the darker it gets), the mapping is **inverted**:

$$\text{PWM}_{duty} \;(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{\text{ADC}_{LDR}}{1023}\right) \times 100$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Value (Trainer Kit) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Duty Cycle $D$ | $D = T_{on} / T$ | % or unitless | 0 – 100 % |
| 2 | Average Voltage $V_{avg}$ | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$ | Volts (V) | 0 – 5 V |
| 3 | PWM Frequency $f_{PWM}$ | $f_{PWM} = 1 / T$ | Hertz (Hz) | **490 Hz** (Arduino default) |
| 4 | DC Motor Speed $\omega$ | $\omega \approx V_a / K_e$ | rad/s | 0 – ~300 rad/s |
| 5 | Back-EMF $E_b$ | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V | load-dependent |
| 6 | LDR Divider $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{ref} / (R_{LDR} + R_{ref})$ | V | 0.1 V – 4.9 V |
| 7 | ADC Code (10-bit) | $\text{ADC} = \lfloor (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \cdot 1023 \rfloor$ | integer | 0 – 1023 |
| 8 | Linear Duty Map | $D = (\text{ADC}/1023) \times 100$ | % | 0 – 100 % |
| 9 | Inverted Duty Map | $D = (1 - \text{ADC}/1023) \times 100$ | % | 0 – 100 % |
| 10 | ADC Resolution $\Delta V$ | $\Delta V = V_{ref} / (2^{N} - 1)$ | V | **4.89 mV** (5 V / 1023) |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of This Control Law

- **Smart street-lighting:** Bright ambient → dim LEDs; dark → full brightness.
- **Automatic exhaust fans in kitchens / darkrooms:** Inverse mapping.
- **Solar-panel sun-tracking motor drives:** Light differential across two LDRs steers a servo/PMDC motor toward the brightest direction.
- **IoT-based agricultural ventilation:** Greenhouses throttle fan RPM by canopy shading sensor.
- **Industrial process control:** Any continuous actuator (solenoid valve, heater, pump) modulated by an analog-equivalent PWM duty is a direct descendant of this experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Note:** In production systems, the LDR is often replaced by a calibrated **lux-meter IC (BH1750, TSL2561)** delivering I²C digital values, and the DC motor is replaced by a **BLDC with closed-loop PID on encoder feedback**. The KTU lab experiment uses the LDR + brushed DC motor pair because it is low-cost, deterministic, and visually demonstrable within a 2-hour lab slot.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Component List & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Component List (Standard IoT Trainer Kit Pinout)

| S.No. | Component | Trainer Kit Pin / Port | Electrical Rating | Purpose in Experiment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) | D0 – D13, A0 – A5 | 5 V logic, 40 mA per pin | Central controller |
| 2 | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) | A0 (via divider) | ~1 k$\Omega$ to 10 M$\Omega$ | Ambient light sensor |
| 3 | Resistor $R_{ref}$ = 10 k$\Omega$ | A0 node to GND | 1/4 W, ±5 % | Forms divider with LDR |
| 4 | DC Geared Motor (BO / TT type) | M1 / OUT1–OUT2 of L298N | 3 – 12 V DC, 100 – 300 mA | Actuator (load) |
| 5 | L298N Dual H-Bridge Motor Driver | IN1, IN2, ENA | Logic 5 V, motor side up to 35 V | PWM amplification + direction |
| 6 | External Battery Pack | L298N +12 V / GND | 7.4 V Li-ion or 9 V | Motor power rail |
| 7 | Jumper Wires | — | — | Interconnections |
| 8 | USB-B Cable | Arduino USB port | Data + 5 V supply | Programming + serial monitor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative:** ESP32 / NodeMCU users can substitute the Arduino; pin numbers change but the algorithm remains identical. ESP32's `analogWrite()` is replaced by `ledcWrite(channel, duty)`.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation — The Control Law

Let us concretely derive the PWM output for three representative LDR lighting scenarios.

### **Case A: Bright Sunlight (LDR = ~1 k$\Omega$)**

**Step 1.** Compute the divider voltage with $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$, $R_{ref} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000 + 10{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{11} \approx 4.545 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2.** Quantize via the 10-bit ADC:

$$\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{4.545}{5} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 930.0 \rfloor = 930$$

**Step 3.** Apply the **direct (light → speed)** mapping:

$$D = \left(\frac{930}{1023}\right) \times 100 \approx 90.9 \, \%$$

**Step 4.** Resulting average motor voltage:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC} = 0.909 \cdot 5 \approx 4.545 \text{ V} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{motor near full speed}$$

---

### **Case B: Indoor Ambient (LDR = ~10 k$\Omega$)**

**Step 1.** Divider voltage:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 10{,}000} = 2.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2.** ADC:

$$\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{2.5}{5} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = 511$$

**Step 3.** Duty:

$$D = \left(\frac{511}{1023}\right) \times 100 \approx 49.95 \, \% \approx 50 \, \%$$

**Step 4.** $V_{avg} \approx 2.5$ V — motor at half speed.

---

### **Case C: Total Darkness (LDR = ~1 M$\Omega$)**

**Step 1.** Divider voltage:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}000} \approx 0.0495 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2.** ADC:

$$\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.0495}{5} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 10.13 \rfloor = 10$$

**Step 3.** Duty:

$$D = \left(\frac{10}{1023}\right) \times 100 \approx 0.98 \, \% \approx 1 \, \%$$

**Step 4.** $V_{avg} \approx 0.05$ V — motor effectively still.

> [!TIP]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice the non-linear behaviour at the extremes. Because the LDR resistance spans three orders of magnitude, the divider output is roughly **logarithmic with respect to light intensity** (lux). A linear mapping in ADC space therefore *under-utilises* the low-light region. In a viva, mentioning this and proposing a `log` or piecewise-linear mapping is a guaranteed L4 (Analyse) credit.

## 3.3 Full Reference Program (Arduino-Compatible C/C++)

> **This is the standard KTU-expected lab code — it is mandatory for IoT LAB paper submissions.**

```cpp
/*============================================================
 * Experiment: PWM-based DC Motor Speed Control using LDR
 * Platform : Arduino Uno (or compatible)
 * Course   : PCAML507 - IoT Lab, KTU 2024 Scheme
 * CO       : CO2 - Interface sensors/actuators and implement
 *            control logic using PWM
 *============================================================*/

const int LDR_PIN   = A0;      // LDR + 10k divider midpoint
const int PWM_PIN   = 9;       // ~PWM capable pin (Timer1, 490 Hz)
const int IN1_PIN   = 8;       // L298N direction pin 1
const int IN2_PIN   = 7;       // L298N direction pin 2

const int PWM_MAX   = 255;     // 8-bit PWM resolution (Arduino)
const int ADC_MAX   = 1023;    // 10-bit ADC resolution

void setup() {
  pinMode(LDR_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(PWM_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN1_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN2_PIN, OUTPUT);

  // Set motor direction (single direction for this experiment)
  digitalWrite(IN1_PIN, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN2_PIN, LOW);

  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("LDR-Controlled PWM Motor Experiment Started...");
}

void loop() {
  // Step 1: Read LDR sensor
  int ldrValue = analogRead(LDR_PIN);   // 0 (dark) to 1023 (bright)

  // Step 2: Map sensor value to PWM duty (light -> speed)
  // Using the direct linear control law
  int pwmValue = map(ldrValue, 0, ADC_MAX, 0, PWM_MAX);

  // Step 3: Output PWM to motor driver enable pin
  analogWrite(PWM_PIN, pwmValue);

  // Step 4: Diagnostic output for serial monitor
  Serial.print("LDR ADC: ");
  Serial.print(ldrValue);
  Serial.print("  |  PWM Duty: ");
  Serial.print((pwmValue * 100.0) / PWM_MAX, 1);
  Serial.print(" %  |  Vavg = ");
  Serial.print((pwmValue / 255.0) * 5.0, 2);
  Serial.println(" V");

  // Step 5: 100 ms refresh for stable visual feedback
  delay(100);
}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
**Define PWM. How is the average output voltage related to the duty cycle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a technique for generating an analog-equivalent signal using a digital output by varying the *width* of HIGH pulses within a fixed period.
> The **average output voltage** is given by:
> $$\bar{V} = D \cdot V_{CC} \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$$
> Thus, by varying $D$ from 0 % to 100 %, $\bar{V}$ can be made to vary smoothly from 0 V to $V_{CC}$, enabling analog-like control of actuators such as DC motors. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
**Why is a motor driver IC (L298N) required between the Arduino and a DC motor? Mention two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Two primary reasons:
> 1. **Current Limitation:** The ATmega328P GPIO can source/sink only ~40 mA, while a DC motor typically draws 100 – 500 mA. Direct connection would damage the microcontroller. **(1.5 marks)**
> 2. **Back-EMF Protection:** A spinning motor generates a high-voltage reverse spike when switched off. The L298N contains internal **flyback diodes** that safely shunt this spike, protecting both the motor and the controller. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe an **LDR-based PWM control system** to vary the speed of a DC motor. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average voltage delivered to the motor** in terms of duty cycle and supply voltage. Using a numerical example, show that a **50 % duty cycle at 5 V** delivers an average of **2.5 V** to the armature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Block Roles (7 marks):**
| Block | Component | Function (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| Input (Light) | Ambient photons | Physical input stimulus |
| Sensor | LDR | Converts light intensity $\rightarrow$ resistance |
| Signal Conditioning | Voltage divider with 10 k$\Omega$ | Converts resistance $\rightarrow$ analog voltage 0 – 5 V |
| Digitiser | Arduino 10-bit ADC on A0 | Converts 0 – 5 V $\rightarrow$ 0 – 1023 digital code |
| Controller | ATmega328P firmware | Maps ADC code $\rightarrow$ PWM duty using `analogWrite()` |
| PWM Generator | Timer1 hardware | Produces 490 Hz square wave on pin D9 |
| Driver | L298N | Amplifies logic-level PWM to motor-current levels |
| Actuator | DC Geared Motor | Converts average electrical energy $\rightarrow$ mechanical rotation |
| Output (Speed) | $\omega$ rad/s | Final controlled variable |

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

The PWM waveform is HIGH ($V_{CC}$) for time $T_{on}$ and LOW (0 V) for time $T_{off}$ in period $T$.

$$\bar{V} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_{CC} \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off} \right]$$

$$\bar{V} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot T_{on}}{T} = V_{CC} \cdot D$$

**Numerical verification:** $D = 50\% = 0.5$, $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$:

$$\bar{V} = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ V} \;\;\checkmark$$

Since motor speed $\omega \propto V_a$ (no-load), the motor runs at approximately **half of its maximum speed**. **(3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for substitution + 2 marks for interpretation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice Question]**

**(a)** Design the **voltage divider circuit** for an LDR such that the output voltage varies from **0.2 V (dark) to 4.5 V (bright)** when $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$. Assume the LDR resistance varies from **10 k$\Omega$ (bright) to 1 M$\Omega$ (dark)**. Find the value of the reference resistor $R_{ref}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **complete Arduino program** to read this LDR value and output a corresponding PWM signal on pin D9 to drive a motor via the L298N driver. The mapping must be **linear and direct** (more light → faster motor). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Designing the Divider (7 marks):**

Place $R_{ref}$ **from A0 to GND** and the LDR **from VCC to A0**. Then:

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{R_{LDR} + R_{ref}}$$

**At bright condition** ($R_{LDR} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{out} = 4.5\text{ V}$):

$$4.5 = 5 \cdot \frac{R_{ref}}{10\text{k} + R_{ref}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4.5 \cdot (10\text{k} + R_{ref}) = 5 \cdot R_{ref}$$

$$45\text{k} + 4.5 R_{ref} = 5 R_{ref} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.5 R_{ref} = 45\text{k} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{ref} = 90\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Verify at dark condition** ($R_{LDR} = 1\text{ M}\Omega$):

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{90\text{k}}{1{,}000\text{k} + 90\text{k}} = 5 \cdot \frac{90}{1090} \approx 0.413 \text{ V}$$

This is close to the target 0.2 V (within design tolerance for a non-critical IoT demo). For a more aggressive design, choose $R_{ref} \approx 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ and place the LDR in the lower leg. **(7 marks: 3 for setup, 2 for solving, 2 for verification)**

**(b) Complete Arduino Program (7 marks):**

```cpp
const int LDR_PIN = A0;
const int PWM_PIN = 9;
const int IN1_PIN = 8;
const int IN2_PIN = 7;

void setup() {
  pinMode(LDR_PIN, INPUT);
  pinMode(PWM_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN1_PIN, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN2_PIN, OUTPUT);
  digitalWrite(IN1_PIN, HIGH);
  digitalWrite(IN2_PIN, LOW);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  int ldr = analogRead(LDR_PIN);              // 0–1023
  int pwm = map(ldr, 0, 1023, 0, 255);         // direct linear map
  analogWrite(PWM_PIN, pwm);
  Serial.print("LDR: "); Serial.print(ldr);
  Serial.print("  PWM: "); Serial.println(pwm);
  delay(100);
}
```

**Viva explanation (must mention during exam):**
- `analogRead()` returns 0–1023 because the ADC is 10-bit. **(1 mark)**
- `analogWrite()` accepts 0–255 because the PWM is 8-bit by default. **(1 mark)**
- The `map()` function performs the linear interpolation $pwm = \frac{ldr}{1023} \times 255$. **(1 mark)**
- Direction is set by `IN1=HIGH`, `IN2=LOW`; speed is set by ENA's PWM duty. **(1 mark)**
- `delay(100)` provides a 10 Hz control-loop refresh — adequate for visual feedback without flicker. **(1 mark)**
- Code is well-commented and uses symbolic constants. **(1 mark)**
- 1 mark reserved for compilation/output demonstration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 8-bit PWM (0–255) with 10-bit ADC (0–1023).** Always explicitly mention both ranges in the program comments. Many students write `analogWrite(ldr)` directly — a guaranteed 0 for that line.
> 2. **Forgetting to set motor direction pins** (IN1, IN2) — the L298N will output 0 V on both OUT pins if both INs are LOW, leading to "no rotation" confusion. Always set IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW for one direction.
> 3. **No common ground** between Arduino, L298N, and the external battery. A floating ground causes erratic, non-repeatable behaviour. *Always tie all GND rails together.*
> 4. **Writing `delay(3000)`** for debounce/refresh — this destroys real-time response. Use `delay(100)` maximum.
> 5. **Not mentioning the back-EMF / flyback diode** when asked "why L298N?" — a direct GPIO drive will eventually kill the MCU pin.
> 6. **Using `int` instead of `float`** in divisions (e.g., `5/2 = 2` in C++). Use `5.0/2` or explicit float casts.
> 7. **Forgetting `Serial.begin(9600);`** — the serial monitor will show garbage if the baud rate mismatches.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall before the lab exam):**

- ☐ **PWM** = varying pulse *width* at constant *period*; duty cycle $D = T_{on}/T$.
- ☐ **Average motor voltage:** $\bar{V} = D \cdot V_{CC}$. Motor speed $\omega \propto \bar{V}$ (no-load approximation).
- ☐ **Arduino Uno default PWM frequency:** **490 Hz** on pins 3, 9, 10, 11; **980 Hz** on pins 5, 6.
- ☐ **`analogWrite(pin, value)`** accepts **0 – 255** (8-bit). Never pass 0–1023 directly.
- ☐ **`analogRead(pin)`** returns **0 – 1023** (10-bit). Use `map()` for unit conversion.
- ☐ **LDR resistance decreases with light**; hence in a divider where LDR is on the $V_{CC}$ side, $V_{out}$ *increases* with brightness.
- ☐ **L298N ENA pin** = enable + speed (PWM input). **IN1, IN2** = direction control.
- ☐ **Always share a common GND** between Arduino, driver, and external power supply.
- ☐ **Flyback diodes** in L298N protect the circuit from motor back-EMF spikes.
- ☐ **ADC resolution** = $V_{ref} / 1023 = 5\text{ V} / 1023 \approx 4.89 \text{ mV}$ per LSB on Arduino Uno.
- ☐ **Linear control law:** $D = (\text{ADC} / 1023) \times 100\%$ (direct) or $D = (1 - \text{ADC}/1023) \times 100\%$ (inverted).
- ☐ **LDR response is non-linear** with lux — a logarithmic or piecewise mapping gives smoother control.
- ☐ **Two common mappings:** *Light → speed* (e.g., sun-tracking) and *Darkness → speed* (e.g., auto-exhaust).
- ☐ **Refresh rate:** 10–100 ms is the sweet spot for IoT demos; avoid < 10 ms (flicker) and > 500 ms (laggy).
- ☐ **Serial monitor baud rate** must match `Serial.begin()` — default **9600**.
- ☐ **Record book essentials:** 5+ reading table, Duty vs. ADC plot on graph, signed observation table, neat circuit diagram, and a 1-paragraph application note.
- ☐ **CO mapping:** This experiment satisfies **CO2** (sensor-actuator interfacing with PWM control) and demonstrates **Bloom L3 (Apply)** + **L4 (Analyse)**.
- ☐ **Sample viva questions to expect:** *(i) Why PWM instead of a DAC? (ii) What happens if frequency is too low? (iii) Why L298N, not direct GPIO? (iv) How to make motor run faster in darkness? (v) Difference between 8-bit PWM and 10-bit ADC?*

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme PCAML507 IoT Lab examination!** *Remember: hardware setup neatness + correct code + working demo + clear observation table = full marks.*

Write a script to analyze sensor data from the IoT Trainer kit, such as calculating averages or identifying trends. Visualize the results.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML507 - INTERNET OF THINGS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a script to analyze sensor data from the IoT Trainer kit, such as calculating averages or identifying trends. Visualize the results.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Sensor Data Analysis** in an Internet of Things (IoT) context is the systematic computational process of collecting, cleaning, transforming, and statistically modeling raw telemetry data generated by heterogeneous sensors (such as temperature, humidity, light, and gas sensors interfaced with a microcontroller) to extract meaningful patterns, compute descriptive statistics (mean, median, variance), detect temporal trends, and present the findings through human-readable graphical representations. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PCAML507 (Internet of Things Lab)**, this experiment falls under **Module Experiment 2**, which evaluates a student's ability to bridge embedded sensing with data analytics on the host machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAML507 - 2024 Scheme):** The student must demonstrate a working script (typically in **Python** using libraries like `pandas`, `numpy`, and `matplotlib`) that ingests sensor output from the IoT Trainer kit, performs at least one statistical aggregation (e.g., rolling average) and at least one visualization (e.g., line plot or scatter plot).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **24-hour bakery** that records the ambient room temperature and humidity every 10 minutes to maintain dough quality. After a week, the baker has 1,008 data points — far too many to eyeball. The baker asks: *"When does the room get hottest? Is the temperature creeping upward each day (a trend)?"* A **sensor data analysis script** acts as the baker's tireless accountant: it reads the logbook (CSV file), crunches the numbers (averages), spots the slowly rising curve (linear trend), and finally draws a clean chart so the baker can act on it. The IoT Trainer kit is the *bakery*; Python is the *accountant*; matplotlib is the *chart paper*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in IoT Analytics:**
> - **Sampling Rate (Hz):** Number of sensor readings per second — typical IoT kits operate at **1–10 Hz**.
> - **Resolution (bits):** ADC bit-depth — most trainer kits use **10-bit or 12-bit ADCs** (values 0–1023 or 0–4095).
> - **Engineering Units:** Raw ADC counts are converted to physical units (°C, %, lux) using the sensor's transfer function.

### Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Evaluation
The KTU examiner awards marks not just for *running* the script but for demonstrating the **pipeline**: **Sensor → Serial/CSV Log → Python Ingestion → Statistical Engine → Visualizer**. Missing any link in this chain typically costs **2 to 3 marks** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IoT Sensor Data Analysis Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The experiment must be understood as a **five-stage computational pipeline**. Each stage has a clear "Why" and "How" that KTU examiners expect in the viva voce.

- **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (How):** The IoT Trainer kit's microcontroller (Arduino Uno / ESP8266 / NodeMCU) reads analog/digital sensor values and prints them over **UART (Serial at 9600 baud)** in CSV format: `timestamp,temperature,humidity,light`.
- **Stage 2 — Data Logging (Why):** A host-side listener (PuTTY, `pyserial`, or Arduino Serial Monitor) captures the stream and persists it to a `.csv` file so analysis becomes **reproducible and offline**.
- **Stage 3 — Statistical Aggregation (How):** Python's `pandas` library loads the CSV; `numpy` computes aggregates. The arithmetic mean smooths sensor noise (thermal jitter, ADC quantization).
- **Stage 4 — Trend Identification (Why):** A **linear regression** (`numpy.polyfit`) or **moving average** reveals slow drifts (e.g., a room warming over hours) that are invisible in raw data.
- **Stage 5 — Visualization (How):** `matplotlib.pyplot` renders the raw series alongside the smoothed/trend curve, satisfying the KTU rubric's "Visualize the results" clause.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula a student must memorize for the **PCAML507** lab record and viva. The pipe symbols inside mathematical expressions are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$ | Baseline reference value for a sensor stream. |
| 2 | Moving Average (window $k$) | $$MA_t = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} x_i$$ | Noise filtering in real-time telemetry. |
| 3 | Variance | $$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$$ | Quantifies sensor noise / instability. |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$$ | Same units as the sensor; used in **±3σ outlier detection**. |
| 5 | Linear Trend Slope | $$m = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$ | Detects monotonic drift over time. |
| 6 | Linear Trend Intercept | $$b = \bar{y} - m\bar{x}$$ | Anchor point of the fitted trend line. |
| 7 | ADC to Voltage | $$V = \frac{ADC\_count \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$ | Converts raw 10/12-bit ADC to volts; $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ typical. |
| 8 | LM35 to °C | $$T(^{\circ}C) = \frac{V_{out}}{10 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}C}$$ | Standard linear transfer function for LM35 temperature sensor. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These same formulas power industrial IoT dashboards in **smart agriculture** (soil moisture trends), **cold-chain logistics** (temperature drift alarms), and **structural health monitoring** (vibration variance thresholds).

### 2.3 Trend Identification — The "Why" Behind the Math
A single average hides change. A **moving average** is a low-pass filter: it suppresses high-frequency noise while preserving slow trends. A **linear regression** fits a straight line $y = mx + b$ through all data points, summarizing the *direction* (sign of $m$) and *magnitude* (absolute value of $m$) of change. KTU examiners commonly ask: *"If $m = 0.12$ °C/hour, what does it imply?"* — the answer: the environment is **warming at 0.12 °C per hour**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — From Raw Sensor CSV to Trend

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):**
The IoT Trainer kit's DHT11 sensor logs temperature (°C) every 5 seconds. The following 10 readings are captured: `24, 25, 25, 26, 27, 28, 28, 29, 30, 31`. Compute the mean, moving average (window $k=3$), variance, standard deviation, and linear trend slope $m$.

---

#### Step 1 — Arithmetic Mean ($\bar{x}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \\
&= \frac{1}{10}\left(24 + 25 + 25 + 26 + 27 + 28 + 28 + 29 + 30 + 31\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{10}(273) \\
&= 27.3 \,^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Sum the 10 readings, then divide by $n=10$. Result: average temperature is **27.3 °C**.

---

#### Step 2 — Moving Average with Window $k = 3$

The first valid moving average uses readings 1, 2, 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA_3 &= \frac{24 + 25 + 25}{3} = \frac{74}{3} \approx 24.67 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_4 &= \frac{25 + 25 + 26}{3} = \frac{76}{3} \approx 25.33 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_5 &= \frac{25 + 26 + 27}{3} = \frac{78}{3} = 26.00 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_6 &= \frac{26 + 27 + 28}{3} = \frac{81}{3} = 27.00 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_7 &= \frac{27 + 28 + 28}{3} = \frac{83}{3} \approx 27.67 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_8 &= \frac{28 + 28 + 29}{3} = \frac{85}{3} \approx 28.33 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_9 &= \frac{28 + 29 + 30}{3} = \frac{87}{3} = 29.00 \,^{\circ}C \\
MA_{10} &= \frac{29 + 30 + 31}{3} = \frac{90}{3} = 30.00 \,^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A rolling window of 3 readings smooths the jagged raw stream. Notice $MA_3$ starts lower because the first three values are the lowest in the series.

---

#### Step 3 — Variance ($\sigma^2$)

For each $x_i$, compute $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{10}\left[(24-27.3)^2 + (25-27.3)^2 + \cdots + (31-27.3)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{10}\left[10.89 + 5.29 + 5.29 + 1.69 + 0.09 + 0.49 + 0.49 + 2.89 + 7.29 + 13.69\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{10}(48.10) \\
&= 4.81 \,(^{\circ}C)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Square each deviation, sum, divide by $n$. The unit is squared because we squared the deviations.

---

#### Step 4 — Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sqrt{\sigma^2} = \sqrt{4.81} \approx 2.19 \,^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Taking the square root returns the unit to °C. **±3σ** bounds: $27.3 \pm 6.57 = [20.73, 33.87]$ °C. Any reading outside this band would be a statistical outlier.

---

#### Step 5 — Linear Trend Slope ($m$)

Let $x_i = i$ (time index 1 to 10) and $y_i$ = temperature reading.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 10, \quad \sum x_i = 55, \quad \sum y_i = 273 \\
\sum x_i y_i &= (1)(24) + (2)(25) + (3)(25) + (4)(26) + (5)(27) \\
&\quad + (6)(28) + (7)(28) + (8)(29) + (9)(30) + (10)(31) \\
&= 24 + 50 + 75 + 104 + 135 + 168 + 196 + 232 + 270 + 310 \\
&= 1564 \\
\sum x_i^2 &= 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 64 + 81 + 100 = 385
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{10(1564) - (55)(273)}{10(385) - (55)^2} \\
&= \frac{15640 - 15015}{3850 - 3025} \\
&= \frac{625}{825} \\
&\approx 0.758 \,^{\circ}C \text{ per sample step}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Positive slope confirms a **monotonic warming trend** of approximately **0.76 °C per 5-second sample**, i.e., the environment is steadily heating — exactly the kind of insight the KTU examiner wants the script to expose.

---

### 3.2 Reference Python Script (IoT Lab — KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following script is provided as the **model answer** for the lab record. It assumes the trainer kit streams CSV to a file `sensor_log.csv` on the host PC. Students are expected to **type, execute, and screenshot** the output.

```python
# ============================================================
# PCAML507 - IoT LAB | Module Experiment 2
# Sensor Data Analysis & Visualization
# ============================================================
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ---------- Stage 1: Load sensor CSV ----------
df = pd.read_csv("sensor_log.csv")
print("First 5 rows of sensor data:\n", df.head())

# ---------- Stage 2: Compute statistics ----------
mean_temp   = df["temperature"].mean()
median_temp = df["temperature"].median()
std_temp    = df["temperature"].std()
var_temp    = df["temperature"].var()

print(f"Mean Temp    = {mean_temp:.2f} C")
print(f"Median Temp  = {median_temp:.2f} C")
print(f"Std Deviation= {std_temp:.2f} C")
print(f"Variance     = {var_temp:.2f}")

# ---------- Stage 3: Moving Average (window = 5) ----------
df["MA5"] = df["temperature"].rolling(window=5).mean()

# ---------- Stage 4: Linear Trend ----------
x = np.arange(len(df))
y = df["temperature"].values
coeffs = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)        # [slope, intercept]
trend  = np.poly1d(coeffs)
df["Trend"] = trend(x)

print(f"Trend Slope  = {coeffs[0]:.3f} C per sample")
print(f"Trend Intercept = {coeffs[1]:.3f} C")

# ---------- Stage 5: Visualization ----------
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot(df["temperature"], label="Raw Sensor", marker="o", alpha=0.6)
plt.plot(df["MA5"],        label="Moving Avg (k=5)", linewidth=2)
plt.plot(df["Trend"],      label="Linear Trend", linestyle="--", color="red")
plt.xlabel("Sample Index")
plt.ylabel("Temperature (C)")
plt.title("IoT Trainer Kit - Temperature Analysis")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.savefig("temperature_analysis.png", dpi=120)
plt.show()
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva Cue:** If the examiner asks *"Why `rolling(window=5)`?"*, answer: *"A 5-sample moving average balances noise suppression with responsiveness to genuine change, a common engineering heuristic."*

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define sensor data analysis in the context of an IoT Trainer kit. List the two main statistical operations a script must perform as per the PCAML507 syllabus.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sensor data analysis is the computational process of ingesting raw readings (temperature, humidity, light) from the trainer kit's sensors, transforming them into meaningful statistics, and visualizing the outcome. The **two main operations** mandated by the **PCAML507 (2024 Scheme)** syllabus are:
1. **Calculating averages** (mean, median, or moving average) to summarize the dataset.
2. **Identifying trends** (e.g., linear regression) to detect drifts or patterns over time.
*(Award full 3 marks for naming both operations with a one-line justification each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*What is the purpose of a moving average in IoT sensor analytics? Why is a window size of $k=5$ commonly chosen?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A moving average is a low-pass filter that smooths short-term sensor noise (ADC jitter, environmental flicker) by averaging the most recent $k$ readings. The formula is $MA_t = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} x_i$. A **window of $k=5$** is commonly chosen because it strikes a balance: small enough to remain responsive to genuine environmental changes, yet large enough to suppress random noise — a standard engineering rule of thumb.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose **3–4 marks** by (a) forgetting to convert ADC counts to engineering units, (b) hard-coding the file path instead of using a relative path, and (c) failing to label axes on the matplotlib plot. Read the rubric for axes labels — they are mandatory.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* With the help of a block diagram, describe the complete pipeline for analyzing sensor data from an IoT Trainer kit, from physical sensing to visualization. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A temperature sensor outputs 8 readings: `22, 23, 24, 25, 25, 26, 27, 28` °C. Compute the mean, standard deviation, and the linear trend slope. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram — 7 marks**
Draw a five-block flow: **Sensor → Microcontroller (ADC + UART) → Host PC (CSV Log) → Python Analysis Engine → Matplotlib Visualization.** Each block must be labeled with its function and the data format transferred (analog → digital → CSV → DataFrame → PNG). *(Full 7 marks for a labeled diagram with all five blocks and arrows showing data flow direction.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Mean (1.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{22+23+24+25+25+26+27+28}{8} = \frac{200}{8} = 25.0 \,^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Standard Deviation (2.5 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{8}\left[(22-25)^2 + (23-25)^2 + \cdots + (28-25)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{8}\left[9 + 4 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 4 + 9\right] = \frac{28}{8} = 3.5 \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.87 \,^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Linear Trend Slope (3 marks)**
With $x_i = 1,2,\ldots,8$ and $y_i$ = temperature:
- $\sum x_i = 36$, $\sum y_i = 200$
- $\sum x_i^2 = 204$
- $\sum x_i y_i = (1)(22)+(2)(23)+(3)(24)+(4)(25)+(5)(25)+(6)(26)+(7)(27)+(8)(28) = 22+46+72+100+125+156+189+224 = 934$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} \\
&= \frac{8(934) - 36(200)}{8(204) - 36^2} = \frac{7472 - 7200}{1632 - 1296} = \frac{272}{336} \approx 0.81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (award 0 mark if omitted):** The slope $m \approx 0.81$ °C per sample indicates a steady warming trend of roughly **0.8 °C every 5 seconds**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain the difference between an arithmetic mean and a moving average. Under what conditions is each preferred in IoT analytics? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Write a Python script using `pandas` and `matplotlib` that loads a CSV file named `sensor_log.csv` (columns: `timestamp`, `temperature`, `humidity`), computes a 5-point moving average for the `temperature` column, fits a linear trend, and plots all three series on a single graph with a legend and axis labels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mean vs Moving Average — 7 marks**

| Aspect | Arithmetic Mean | Moving Average |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Formula | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | $MA_t = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=t-k+1}^{t} x_i$ |
| Scope | Entire dataset | Local window of size $k$ |
| Use when | Reporting overall summary (daily average temp) | Monitoring real-time drift, filtering noise |
| Limitation | Hides temporal variation | Lag in detecting sudden changes |

**Preferred conditions:** Use the **arithmetic mean** for static, post-hoc reports (e.g., "average lab temperature today was 27 °C"). Use the **moving average** for **streaming, real-time dashboards** where local smoothing is needed without waiting for the full dataset. *(7 marks: 3 for correct definitions with formulas, 2 for the comparison table, 2 for the preferred-conditions justification.)*

**(b) Python Script — 7 marks**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# (1 mark) Load CSV
df = pd.read_csv("sensor_log.csv")

# (1.5 marks) Moving Average
df["MA5"] = df["temperature"].rolling(window=5).mean()

# (1.5 marks) Linear Trend
x = np.arange(len(df))
coeffs = np.polyfit(x, df["temperature"].values, 1)
df["Trend"] = np.poly1d(coeffs)(x)

# (2 marks) Plot with legend and labels
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(df["temperature"], label="Raw Temperature", alpha=0.6)
plt.plot(df["MA5"],        label="Moving Avg (k=5)", linewidth=2)
plt.plot(df["Trend"],      label="Linear Trend", linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("Time Index")
plt.ylabel("Temperature (C)")
plt.title("IoT Sensor Data Analysis")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

*Mark distribution: 1 for CSV load, 1.5 for moving average, 1.5 for trend, 2 for plot (with legend, axis labels, title), 1 for clean code structure.*

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Encryption** in the context of IoT is the mathematical process of converting plaintext (readable sensor data, actuator commands, or control messages) into ciphertext (unreadable, scrambled form) using a defined algorithm (cipher) and a secret key, such that only authorized entities possessing the correct key can reverse the process (decryption) and recover the original information.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAML507)** framework, encryption on an IoT Trainer Kit is implemented as an embedded firmware routine that intercepts the data payload *before* transmission (e.g., over UART to a gateway, or over MQTT/HTTP to a cloud broker) and applies a lightweight cryptographic transform. Common techniques demonstrated at the B.Tech level include the **Caesar Cipher**, **XOR Cipher**, **Base64 Encoding**, and introductory **AES-128 (Advanced Encryption Standard)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAML507):** IoT security is a 3-credit laboratory course. This experiment is the **first mandatory security experiment** of the module, establishing the foundation for secure communication, which is a core Course Outcome (CO) of the program.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Plaintext (P)** | The original, unencrypted message (e.g., `"Temp=25C"`). |
| **Ciphertext (C)** | The encrypted, unreadable output (e.g., `"XOR#$@1"`). |
| **Key (K)** | A secret value used by the algorithm to encrypt and decrypt. |
| **Algorithm (E/D)** | The mathematical function applied to convert P → C and C → P. |
| **Symmetric Key** | Same key is used for both encryption and decryption (used in this experiment). |
| **Asymmetric Key** | Uses a public/private key pair (out of scope for this lab). |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a sealed **locked diary** to your friend across a classroom. Instead of writing your secrets on an open postcard (plaintext), you:

1. Write your secrets on paper (the plaintext message).
2. Place the paper inside a **steel box with a combination lock** (the encryption algorithm).
3. Spin the lock to a secret 3-digit combination that only you and your friend know (the key).
4. Send the locked box across the room. Even if a bully (an attacker) intercepts the box, he cannot read the secret without smashing the box — and he doesn't know the combination.

Your friend receives the box, dials the secret combination, opens it, and reads the secret. This is **encryption (locking) and decryption (unlocking)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the IoT Trainer Kit, the "diary" is your **sensor data** (temperature, humidity, light), the "steel box" is the **firmware algorithm** running on the microcontroller (e.g., Arduino/ESP8266/NodeMCU), the "combination" is the **encryption key**, and the "classroom" is the **wireless/wired network** that may have eavesdroppers.

## 1.3 Why Encryption is Essential in IoT

IoT devices frequently transmit data over **insecure channels** (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, public MQTT brokers). Without encryption:

- An attacker can **eavesdrop** on sensor readings (privacy breach).
- An attacker can **inject false commands** to actuators (e.g., unlock a smart door).
- An attacker can **spoof device identity** (impersonation).

The CIA Triad — **Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication** — is preserved through encryption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Security Constants to Remember:**
> - AES-128 key length: **128 bits = 16 bytes**.
> - AES-256 key length: **256 bits = 32 bytes**.
> - Caesar Cipher shift range: typically **0–25** for English alphabets.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Basic Encryption Techniques

For the IoT Trainer Kit, we classify the demonstrable techniques into three families:

### A. Classical Substitution Cipher — Caesar Cipher
A simple monoalphabetic substitution where each letter in the plaintext is shifted by a fixed number of positions (the key, K) in the alphabet.

### B. Bitwise Stream Cipher — XOR Cipher
Each byte of plaintext is XOR-ed with a key byte. It is the **fastest** encryption method for resource-constrained microcontrollers and is a true building block of modern stream ciphers (RC4, ChaCha20).

### C. Modern Block Cipher — AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)
The industry standard, recommended by NIST. Implemented on IoT kits using hardware-accelerated libraries (e.g., `AES.h`, `Crypto.h`, or ESP32 hardware AES).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

### Technique 1: Caesar Cipher Logic
1. Convert each character of the plaintext to its numerical ASCII/alphabetic index.
2. Add the key (K) modulo 26 (for alphabets) or modulo 256 (for full ASCII).
3. Convert the new index back to a character → this is the ciphertext.
4. Decryption is the reverse: subtract the key modulo 26.

### Technique 2: XOR Cipher Logic
1. The key is a single byte (or a repeating byte string).
2. For every byte `P[i]` of the plaintext, compute `C[i] = P[i] XOR K`.
3. To decrypt, apply the **same operation**: `P[i] = C[i] XOR K`. (XOR is its own inverse!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why XOR is its own inverse:** From Boolean algebra, $A \oplus A = 0$ and $A \oplus 0 = A$. Therefore, if $C = P \oplus K$, then $C \oplus K = (P \oplus K) \oplus K = P$. This is the foundational property exploited in all symmetric stream ciphers.

### Technique 3: AES-128 Logic
1. The 128-bit plaintext block is arranged in a **4×4 state matrix** of bytes.
2. The cipher applies **10 rounds** (for AES-128) of four transformations: `SubBytes`, `ShiftRows`, `MixColumns`, `AddRoundKey`.
3. The final round omits `MixColumns`.
4. A unique round key is derived from the original 128-bit master key for each round using the **Key Expansion** algorithm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Encryption Formula | Decryption Formula | Key Length | Strength | IoT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Caesar Cipher | $C = (P + K) \mod 26$ | $P = (C - K) \mod 26$ | 1 byte (K) | **Very Weak** | Demonstration only |
| XOR Cipher | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ | N bytes (repeating) | Weak (if key reused) | **High** (speed) |
| Base64 Encoding | Map 3 bytes → 4 ASCII chars | Reverse mapping | None (not encryption!) | **None** (encoding) | High (transport) |
| AES-128 (ECB) | $C = \text{AES}(P, K)$ | $P = \text{AES}^{-1}(C, K)$ | **128 bits (16 bytes)** | **Strong (NIST)** | High (with library) |
| AES-128 (CBC) | $C_0 = E(P_0 \oplus IV, K)$ | $P_0 = D(C_0, K) \oplus IV$ | 128 bits + IV | **Very Strong** | High (recommended) |

Where:
- $P$ = Plaintext character/block
- $C$ = Ciphertext character/block
- $K$ = Secret Key
- $IV$ = Initialization Vector (random nonce, prevents pattern leakage)
- $\oplus$ = Bitwise XOR operator
- $\mod$ = Modulo operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Base64 is NOT encryption.** It is an *encoding* scheme. A KTU examiner may ask this as a trick question. Base64 simply maps binary data to printable ASCII and is trivially reversible without a key. It provides **zero confidentiality**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Home Gateways** use AES-128 in CBC/GCM mode to secure Zigbee/Wi-Fi traffic.
- **MQTT Brokers** (HiveMQ, Mosquitto) support TLS (Transport Layer Security), which uses AES-256-GCM under the hood.
- **Healthcare IoT** (pacemakers, insulin pumps) MUST use encryption to prevent life-threatening command injection attacks (FDA 2023 cybersecurity guidelines).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** uses encryption to protect SCADA telemetry from corporate espionage.
- **Smart Energy Meters** in India (as per IS 16444 / CEA guidelines) mandate encrypted data transmission to prevent tampering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Caesar Cipher on an IoT Sensor Reading

**Problem:** A temperature sensor reads the plaintext `"ESP"`. Encrypt it using the **Caesar Cipher** with shift key **K = 3**, then decrypt and verify the original message.

### Step 1: Map Plaintext to Alphabetical Indices

For the letters of the English alphabet (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25):

$$
P = \text{`E'} = 4, \quad P = \text{`S'} = 18, \quad P = \text{`P'} = 15
$$

### Step 2: Apply Encryption Formula $C = (P + K) \mod 26$

For `E` (4):

$$
C_1 = (4 + 3) \mod 26 = 7 \mod 26 = 7
$$

For `S` (18):

$$
C_2 = (18 + 3) \mod 26 = 21 \mod 26 = 21
$$

For `P` (15):

$$
C_3 = (15 + 3) \mod 26 = 18 \mod 26 = 18
$$

### Step 3: Map Indices Back to Characters

- 7 → `H`
- 21 → `V`
- 18 → `S`

$$
\boxed{C = \text{`HVS'}}
$$

### Step 4: Decryption Verification Using $P = (C - K) \mod 26$

For `H` (7):

$$
P_1 = (7 - 3) \mod 26 = 4 \mod 26 = 4
$$

For `V` (21):

$$
P_2 = (21 - 3) \mod 26 = 18 \mod 26 = 18
$$

For `S` (18):

$$
P_3 = (18 - 3) \mod 26 = 15 \mod 26 = 15
$$

Mapping back: 4 → `E`, 18 → `S`, 15 → `P`. We recover `ESP`. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Caesar Cipher has only **26 possible keys** and can be brute-forced in milliseconds. It is suitable for KTU lab demonstration of *principle* but is **never used in real IoT deployments**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — XOR Cipher on a Numeric Payload

**Problem:** A humidity sensor outputs the ASCII string `"42"`. The IoT device uses an XOR key **K = 0x5A** (one byte, repeated). Show the encrypted and decrypted bit patterns.

### Step 1: Get ASCII Codes of Plaintext

$$
P_1 = \text{`4'} = 0x34, \quad P_2 = \text{`2'} = 0x32
$$

### Step 2: Binary Representations

$$
P_1 = 0x34 = 0011\,0100_2, \quad P_2 = 0x32 = 0011\,0010_2
$$

$$
K = 0x5A = 0101\,1010_2
$$

### Step 3: Apply XOR $C_i = P_i \oplus K$

For `4` (0x34 XOR 0x5A):

$$
C_1 = 0011\,0100_2 \oplus 0101\,1010_2 = 0110\,1110_2 = 0x6E
$$

For `2` (0x32 XOR 0x5A):

$$
C_2 = 0011\,0010_2 \oplus 0101\,1010_2 = 0110\,1000_2 = 0x68
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \text{0x6E 0x68} = \text{`nh' (if rendered as ASCII)}}
$$

### Step 4: Decryption Verification $P_i = C_i \oplus K$

For 0x6E:

$$
P_1 = 0110\,1110_2 \oplus 0101\,1010_2 = 0011\,0100_2 = 0x34 = \text{`4'}
$$

For 0x68:

$$
P_2 = 0110\,1000_2 \oplus 0101\,1010_2 = 0011\,0010_2 = 0x32 = \text{`2'}
$$

We recover `"42"`. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decrypted output `0x34 0x32` is **bit-for-bit identical** to the plaintext. This proves XOR is a lossless, symmetric transformation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — AES-128 Block Encryption (Conceptual Walkthrough)

**Problem:** The IoT sensor outputs a 16-byte block (e.g., a 128-bit temperature/humidity timestamp packet). Encrypt with AES-128 using a 16-byte secret key.

### Step 1: Arrange Plaintext as a 4×4 State Matrix

Each cell is one byte. The bytes are filled **column-by-column** (top-to-bottom, left-to-right):

$$
\text{State} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_4 & b_8 & b_{12} \\ b_1 & b_5 & b_9 & b_{13} \\ b_2 & b_6 & b_{10} & b_{14} \\ b_3 & b_7 & b_{11} & b_{15} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2: AddRoundKey (Round 0)
XOR the entire state with the 16-byte master key. This is the initial whitening step.

### Step 3: Execute 9 Main Rounds (Rounds 1–9), each with 4 Steps:
- **SubBytes:** Replace each byte $S_{i,j}$ with its S-Box lookup $S_{i,j} = \text{S-Box}[S_{i,j}]$ (non-linear confusion).
- **ShiftRows:** Cyclically left-shift row $r$ by $r$ positions (row 0: no shift, row 1: 1, row 2: 2, row 3: 3).
- **MixColumns:** Multiply each column by a fixed GF($2^8$) matrix (diffusion).
- **AddRoundKey:** XOR state with the round-specific key $K_r$ derived from Key Expansion.

### Step 4: Final Round (Round 10)
SubBytes → ShiftRows → **AddRoundKey** (no MixColumns).

### Step 5: Output the 4×4 State Matrix as Ciphertext

The total round count for AES-128 is:

$$
N_r = 10 \text{ rounds (since } 128 \text{-bit key, } N_r = 6 + \lceil 128/32 \rceil = 10\text{)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> On the IoT Trainer Kit, the student does not implement AES from scratch. They call `aes.encrypt(plaintext, key)` from a library. The above walkthrough is for **theory and exam explanation**, satisfying the KTU outcome of "understanding the security algorithm conceptually."

---

## 3.4 Hardware Integration: How the IoT Kit Performs Encryption

The IoT Trainer Kit (typically Arduino Uno/Nano or ESP8266/ESP32) executes the following high-level data flow:

1. **Sensor Read:** Analog/Digital pin reads sensor value (e.g., DHT11 temperature = 25°C).
2. **Format:** Firmware converts the value to a string: `"T:25,H:60"`.
3. **Encrypt:** The string is passed to the encryption function (XOR, Caesar, or AES), producing ciphertext.
4. **Transmit:** Ciphertext is sent over UART (to PC/Serial Monitor) or Wi-Fi (to MQTT broker).
5. **Receive & Decrypt:** Receiver (PC script or another node) applies the inverse function with the same key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the lab record, the student must demonstrate the **same plaintext, encrypted, then decrypted, recovering the original**. The Serial Monitor should show three lines: `Original:`, `Encrypted:`, `Decrypted:`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End IoT Encryption Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions of the sensor, microcontroller, encryption module, communication channel, and decryption receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IoT_Sender
        A[Sensor Module: DHT11, LM35, LDR] --> B[ADC / I2C Read]
        B --> C[Plaintext Buffer: T 25 H 60]
        C --> D[Encryption Engine: XOR, Caesar, AES]
        K1[Secret Key: K = 0x5A] --> D
    end

    D -- Ciphertext 6E 68 ... --> E[Transport Channel: UART, Wi-Fi, MQTT, BLE]

    E --> F[Decryption Engine: Inverse Operation]
    K2[Same Secret Key: K = 0x5A] --> F
    F --> G[Plaintext Output: T 25 H 60]
    G --> H[Application: Cloud Dashboard, Mobile App, Actuator]

    A1[Attacker: Sniffing Wi-Fi Packets] -. Cannot read without K .-> E
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **plaintext** is created at the sensor node.
- The **secret key K** is stored securely in firmware (in real life, in a hardware security module or TPM).
- The ciphertext traverses an **insecure medium** (radio waves, public broker).
- An **attacker** can intercept the ciphertext but cannot recover the plaintext without K.
- The **legitimate receiver** applies the inverse decryption to recover the original sensor reading.

## 4.2 Flowchart — Firmware Decision Logic (Encryption Mode Selection)

This diagram models the firmware state machine a student would implement on the IoT Trainer Kit, allowing selection of the encryption method at runtime.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON / Reset]) --> Init[Initialize Serial 9600 baud]
    Init --> ReadSensor[Read Temperature and Humidity]
    ReadSensor --> Format[Format String: T val H val]
    Format --> Menu[Display Mode Menu on Serial Monitor]
    Menu --> Choice{User Selects Mode}
    Choice -- 1 --> C1[Caesar Encrypt: shift K = 3]
    Choice -- 2 --> C2[XOR Encrypt: key 0x5A]
    Choice -- 3 --> C3[AES-128 Encrypt: 16 byte key]
    Choice -- 4 --> C4[Base64 Encode: not encryption]
    C1 --> Transmit[Print Encrypted Bytes to Serial]
    C2 --> Transmit
    C3 --> Transmit
    C4 --> Transmit
    Transmit --> Decrypt[Receiver Decrypts and Prints Plaintext]
    Decrypt --> Loop[Repeat every 2 seconds]
    Loop --> ReadSensor
```

**Interpretation:**
- The firmware is **interactive**, allowing the examiner to see the output of all three techniques in one run.
- The **transmit** step uses the Serial Monitor at 9600 baud (configurable).
- The **loop** ensures the demonstration is continuous for the viva voce.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Sender–Receiver Handshake with Key Pre-Agreement

This sequence diagram shows the temporal order of operations when the IoT node and the gateway agree on a key (a simplified version of what TLS does in production).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor Node
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant KeyStore as Secure Key Storage
    participant Channel as Insecure Channel
    participant Gateway as Gateway / Cloud

    Note over Sensor,KeyStore: Pre-deployment: Key K is burned into both nodes
    Sensor->>MCU: Read raw value 25
    MCU->>KeyStore: Retrieve Key K
    MCU->>MCU: Compute Ciphertext C = E(P, K)
    MCU->>Channel: Transmit C
    Channel->>Gateway: Forward C
    Gateway->>Gateway: Compute Plaintext P = D(C, K)
    Gateway->>Gateway: Display T 25
```

**Interpretation:**
- In a real lab, the key is **shared out-of-band** (e.g., manually typed in the firmware before flashing).
- The **insecure channel** may be sniffed, but only ciphertext is visible.
- Decryption happens **only** on trusted endpoints.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Caesar vs XOR vs AES (Mermaid Quadrant)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Quadrant: Speed vs Security]
    Q1 --> TopLeft[Caesar: High Speed, Very Low Security]
    Q1 --> TopRight[AES: Medium Speed, Very High Security]
    Q1 --> BotLeft[Base64: High Speed, Zero Security]
    Q1 --> BotRight[XOR: High Speed, Low Medium Security]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Top-right (AES)** is the production target.
- **Top-left (Caesar)** is the pedagogical starting point.
- **Bottom-right (XOR)** is the educational bridge between classical and modern ciphers.
- **Bottom-left (Base64)** is a reminder that encoding is not encryption.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]
**Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric key encryption. Which type is typically preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| Keys Used | **One shared secret key** | **Public key + Private key pair** |
| Speed | **Fast** (suitable for bulk data) | Slow (heavy math) |
| Key Distribution | Difficult (must share secretly) | Easy (public key is public) |
| Example Algorithms | AES, DES, 3DES, XOR | RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman |
| IoT Suitability | **Preferred** | Not preferred for edge devices |

**Preferred for IoT: Symmetric Key (AES-128)** because IoT devices have limited CPU, RAM, and battery. Symmetric algorithms like AES are **100×–1000× faster** than RSA and consume far less power, making them ideal for battery-operated sensor nodes. *(3 marks: 1 for definition difference, 1 for preference, 1 for justification with example)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]
**Explain why Base64 encoding is NOT considered a form of encryption. Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Base64 is an **encoding scheme**, not an encryption algorithm. Encryption requires a **secret key** such that the output is mathematically infeasible to reverse without knowing the key. Base64, on the other hand, uses a **fixed, publicly known mapping** (a 64-character alphabet: A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /) and is **trivially reversible** by anyone who knows the scheme — no key is needed.

**Example:** The plaintext `"IoT"` in ASCII is `0x49 0x6F 0x74`. Base64 encodes this to `"SW90"`. Anyone can run a Base64 decoder online (e.g., `base64decode.org`) and instantly recover `"IoT"` without any secret. Since no key is involved, Base64 provides **zero confidentiality** and **zero security** — it only ensures binary data can traverse text-based protocols like HTTP and JSON. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for "no key" argument, 1 for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end architecture for securing IoT sensor data** during transmission. Identify the role of the secret key, plaintext, ciphertext, and the transmission medium. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Implement the **Caesar Cipher** technique on an IoT Trainer Kit (Arduino/ESP8266) to encrypt the sensor string `"IOTLAB"`. Use shift key **K = 7**. Show all intermediate numerical calculations. Write the equivalent Arduino `setup()` and `loop()` function outline. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Architecture Description:**

The end-to-end secure IoT data pipeline consists of **five logical blocks** working in sequence:

1. **Sensor Block:** Captures physical phenomena (temperature, humidity, motion) and outputs analog/digital signals.
2. **Plaintext Formation Block:** Microcontroller firmware formats raw readings into human-readable strings (e.g., `"T:28"`).
3. **Encryption Block:** Applies a cryptographic algorithm (AES/XOR) using a pre-shared secret key $K$, transforming plaintext $P$ to ciphertext $C = E(P, K)$.
4. **Transmission Channel:** Carries ciphertext over potentially insecure media (Wi-Fi, BLE, MQTT, LoRa).
5. **Decryption & Receiver Block:** Authorized receiver applies $P = D(C, K)$ to recover the original data and forwards it to cloud/actuator.

**Block Diagram (textual representation since figure is required in exam):**

```
Sensor → Plaintext → [E(P, K)] → Ciphertext → Channel → [D(C, K)] → Plaintext → Application
                       ↑                                   ↑
                   Secret Key K                        Same Secret Key K
```

**Role of Key:** The secret key $K$ is shared between sender and receiver *out-of-band* (e.g., hardcoded in firmware). Without $K$, the ciphertext is computationally infeasible to reverse. The same key enforces **confidentiality** (eavesdroppers cannot read) and **integrity** (tampering is detected). *(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for diagram, 2 for key role, 1 for CIA triad link)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Convert `"IOTLAB"` to uppercase indices (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25):**

$$
I = 8, \quad O = 14, \quad T = 19, \quad L = 11, \quad A = 0, \quad B = 1
$$

**Step 2: Apply $C = (P + 7) \mod 26$:**

- I: $(8 + 7) \mod 26 = 15$ → `P`
- O: $(14 + 7) \mod 26 = 21$ → `V`
- T: $(19 + 7) \mod 26 = 0$ → `A` (wrap-around at 26)
- L: $(11 + 7) \mod 26 = 18$ → `S`
- A: $(0 + 7) \mod 26 = 7$ → `H`
- B: $(1 + 7) \mod 26 = 8$ → `I`

**Ciphertext = `"PVASHI"`**

**Step 3: Arduino Firmware Outline (pseudocode):**

```cpp
int key = 7;

String encrypt(String plain) {
  String cipher = "";
  for (int i = 0; i < plain.length(); i++) {
    char c = plain.charAt(i);
    if (c >= 'A' && c <= 'Z') {
      cipher += char((c - 'A' + key) % 26 + 'A');
    } else {
      cipher += c;
    }
  }
  return cipher;
}

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  String original = "IOTLAB";
  String encrypted = encrypt(original);
  Serial.print("Original: ");   Serial.println(original);
  Serial.print("Encrypted: ");  Serial.println(encrypted);
}
void loop() {}
```

*(7 marks: 2 for index mapping, 2 for modulo calculation, 2 for Arduino code logic, 1 for correct ciphertext answer)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **XOR Cipher** algorithm. Prove mathematically that XOR encryption is its own inverse (i.e., decryption uses the same operation as encryption). Apply the XOR operation to encrypt the 8-bit ASCII value of the character `'K'` using key `K = 0x3C`. Show all binary steps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Caesar Cipher, XOR Cipher, and AES-128** across the following parameters: key length, computational complexity, security strength, and IoT resource consumption. Which one would you recommend for a battery-powered environmental monitoring node transmitting data every 10 seconds, and why? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**XOR Cipher Algorithm Description:**

The XOR (exclusive OR) cipher is a symmetric, bitwise stream cipher where each byte (or bit) of the plaintext is combined with a key byte using the bitwise XOR operator ($\oplus$). The general formula is:

$$
C_i = P_i \oplus K_i
$$

For decryption, the same operation is reapplied:

$$
P_i = C_i \oplus K_i
$$

**Mathematical Proof that XOR is its own inverse:**

From Boolean algebra, the XOR operator satisfies the following identities:

$$
A \oplus A = 0 \quad \text{(self-inverse)}
$$
$$
A \oplus 0 = A \quad \text{(identity element)}
$$
$$
(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C) \quad \text{(associativity)}
$$

Starting from the ciphertext equation $C = P \oplus K$, applying the decryption operation:

$$
D(C, K) = C \oplus K
$$

Substituting $C$:

$$
D(C, K) = (P \oplus K) \oplus K
$$

Using associativity:

$$
D(C, K) = P \oplus (K \oplus K)
$$

Using self-inverse:

$$
D(C, K) = P \oplus 0
$$

Using identity:

$$
\boxed{D(C, K) = P}
$$

Thus, decryption with the same key recovers the original plaintext. **Q.E.D.** *(3 marks: 1 for algorithm, 2 for proof)*

**XOR Encryption of ASCII `'K'` with K = 0x3C:**

- ASCII value of `'K'` = 0x4B = `0100 1011` in binary.
- Key `K = 0x3C` = `0011 1100` in binary.
- XOR operation, bit by bit:

| Bit Position | Plaintext Bit | Key Bit | XOR Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 (MSB) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 0 (LSB) | 1 | 0 | 1 |

- Result: `0111 0111` = **0x77** = ASCII `'w'`.
- **Ciphertext: `'w'`**
- To verify decryption: `'w'` = 0x77 = `0111 0111`. XOR with K = `0011 1100` gives `0100 1011` = 0x4B = `'K'`. ✓ *(4 marks: 1 for ASCII conversion, 2 for binary XOR table, 1 for verification)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Caesar Cipher | XOR Cipher | AES-128 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key Length** | 1 byte (shift 0–25) | 1 to N bytes (repeating) | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| **Computational Complexity** | O(n) — trivial | O(n) — bitwise, very fast | High (10 rounds, GF math) |
| **Security Strength** | **Trivial** (26 keys) | Weak (reused key) | **NIST standard, strong** |
| **IoT Resource Use** | Negligible | Negligible | Moderate (1–5 KB RAM) |
| **Brute-Force Resistance** | <1 millisecond | Minutes (key dependent) | **$2^{128}$ operations** (infeasible) |

**Recommendation: AES-128 (with hardware acceleration if available).**

For a **battery-powered environmental monitoring node** transmitting every 10 seconds, the trade-offs are:
1. **Power budget:** The node has limited mAh capacity. Caesar/XOR consume negligible power but are cryptographically broken.
2. **Data sensitivity:** Environmental data (temperature, humidity) may be commercially sensitive (e.g., pharma cold chain) — so encryption is mandated.
3. **Transmission frequency:** Every 10 seconds is low frequency, so even AES-128's few-millisecond execution is acceptable.
4. **Longevity:** A device deployed for 5+ years must use a cipher that withstands future attack improvements — only AES qualifies.

**Justification:** If the microcontroller is an **ESP32**, it has **hardware AES acceleration**, making AES-128 essentially free in terms of CPU cycles. For an **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)**, software AES is slower but still feasible for low-rate telemetry. Caesar/XOR should be reserved for **pedagogical demonstration** only. *(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for recommendation, 2 for engineering justification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Calling Base64 "encryption":** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. Base64 is an **encoding**, not encryption. Always state "encoding for transport" and clarify "no key involved."
> 2. **Confusing XOR with OR:** Students often write `P[i] OR K[i]` instead of `P[i] XOR K[i]`. These are different bitwise operations. OR is **not** self-inverse.
> 3. **Forgetting the modulo wrap-around in Caesar Cipher:** When the shift exceeds Z (25), students forget to wrap to A. Always apply `mod 26`.
> 4. **Not showing the decryption step in the lab record:** Both encryption AND decryption must be demonstrated. The Examiner's "Viva" question will be: *"How do you verify your encryption works?"* — answer: *by decrypting the ciphertext and comparing with the original plaintext.*
> 5. **Hardcoding the key in the Serial Monitor print statement:** A security-aware examiner will reject code that `Serial.println(key)`. The key must be stored privately in firmware, never broadcast.
> 6. **Confusing symmetric with public-key cryptography:** This experiment is **symmetric**. Mentioning RSA/ECC without addressing the actual algorithm is a red flag.
> 7. **Not justifying the choice of algorithm for IoT constraints:** A common viva question is: *"Why not use AES-256?"* — Answer: AES-128 is sufficient (no known practical attacks), and AES-256 doubles the round count (14 rounds) and key schedule overhead, wasting IoT battery.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Encryption** = converting plaintext to ciphertext using an algorithm and a key.
- ✅ **Decryption** = the inverse operation, recovering plaintext from ciphertext.
- ✅ **Three techniques demonstrated on IoT kits:** Caesar, XOR, AES-128.
- ✅ **Caesar Cipher** formula: $C = (P + K) \mod 26$; uses 1-byte key; demo only.
- ✅ **XOR Cipher** formula: $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$; same op for encryption and decryption; fast but weak.
- ✅ **AES-128** uses a 16-byte key, 10 rounds, and is the production standard.
- ✅ **Base64 is NOT encryption** — it is a binary-to-text encoding with no key.
- ✅ **Symmetric key** = same key for both ends (used in this lab).
- ✅ **IoT resource constraints** favor lightweight symmetric ciphers like AES-128, not RSA/ECC.
- ✅ **Lab record must show** Original → Encrypted → Decrypted → Original (round-trip).
- ✅ **Serial Monitor output** should be labeled with timestamps for the viva demonstration.
- ✅ **Key storage** must be secure (in real IoT, use a TPM or secure element, not plaintext firmware).
- ✅ **Initialization Vector (IV)** is required for AES-CBC to avoid pattern leakage across packets.
- ✅ **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)** mode is preferred over ECB mode for sensor streams.
- ✅ **For exam answers:** Always include the block diagram, the formula, a worked example, and a 1-line IoT-justification sentence.

---
