> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Project: Design and implement a project that uses multiple components of the IoT Trainer kit to create a functional IoT system, such as a smart home system.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Home IoT System** is an integrated cyber-physical system that employs pervasive sensing, embedded computation, and networked communication to monitor and control household appliances and environmental parameters autonomously. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme IoT Lab, the smart home system is realized as a **multi-node Embedded System** built upon the **ESP8266/ESP32** microcontroller, integrating heterogeneous sensors (DHT11, LDR, PIR, MQ-2), actuators (Relay, Buzzer, Servo), and a **Wi-Fi-enabled cloud middleware** (Blynk / ThingSpeak / MQTT) to perform closed-loop data acquisition, threshold-based decision making, and remote actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAML507):** Module Experiment 2 mandates that students must *integrate a minimum of three sensors, two actuators, and one communication protocol* into a single functional prototype. Partial credit is reserved for hardware assembly even if cloud integration fails.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Butler" Mental Model

Imagine hiring a **24x7 invisible butler** for your home. This butler:

| Human Butler Action | IoT System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Feels the room temperature and switches on the fan | DHT11 sensor + Relay-controlled Fan |
| Notices darkness and turns on the porch light | LDR + Relay-controlled Bulb |
| Detects a stranger entering the house | PIR Motion Sensor + Buzzer + Cloud Alert |
| Detects LPG gas leakage and raises alarm | MQ-2 Gas Sensor + Buzzer + Mobile Push |
| Reports the home status to the owner on phone | ESP8266 + Blynk/ThingSpeak Dashboard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Architectural Principle:** An IoT system is fundamentally a **Sense → Think → Actuate** loop. The "Sense" layer captures physical phenomena, the "Think" layer (microcontroller + firmware) processes data against thresholds, and the "Actuate" layer physically modifies the environment (turn on/off devices, alert user).

## 1.3 Components Inventory of a Standard KTU IoT Trainer Kit

A typical KTU-recommended IoT Trainer kit contains the following modules:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Trainer Kit Components:**
> 1. **ESP8266 NodeMCU / ESP32 DevKit** — Wi-Fi enabled microcontroller (the "brain")
> 2. **DHT11** — Digital Temperature & Humidity sensor
> 3. **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — Analog light intensity sensor
> 4. **PIR (HC-SR501)** — Passive Infrared motion sensor
> 5. **MQ-2 / MQ-135** — Gas/Smoke sensor (Analog + Digital out)
> 6. **HC-SR04** — Ultrasonic distance sensor
> 7. **Relay Module (1-channel or 4-channel, 5V/230V)** — High-voltage switching
> 8. **Buzzer (Active/Piezo)** — Audible alarm
> 9. **16x2 LCD with I2C backpack** — Local display
> 10. **OLED Display (0.96", SSD1306)** — Graphical local display
> 11. **LEDs, Resistors (220Ω, 10kΩ), Breadboard, Jumper wires**
> 12. **Servo Motor (SG90)** — Mechanical actuation
> 13. **Push Buttons & Switches**

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wi-Fi Frequency Band:** **2.4 GHz** (ISM Band)
- **ESP8266 Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (logic), **5 V** (USB power)
- **DHT11 Measurement Range:** Temperature **0–50 °C**, Humidity **20–90 %RH**
- **DHT11 Accuracy:** Temperature **±2 °C**, Humidity **±5 %RH**
- **Relay Coil Voltage:** **5 V DC**; Contact rating: **250V AC / 10A** or **30V DC / 10A**
- **PIR Detection Range:** **3–7 meters** with **120°** cone
- **ADC Resolution (ESP8266):** **10-bit** → **0 to 1023** discrete levels
- **Reference Voltage (Vref):** **3.3 V** on ESP8266, **3.3 V** on ESP32
- **Standard Baud Rate for Serial Monitor:** **115200 bps**
- **HTTP Port:** **80**; **MQTT Default Port:** **1883** (TCP) / **8883** (TLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Decomposition (Sense → Think → Actuate)

The smart home system operates as a deterministic finite state machine running inside an infinite `loop()`. Each pass through the loop executes the following structured pipeline:

### Step 1 — Sensor Acquisition (SENSE)
- **Digital sensors** (DHT11, PIR) communicate via a single-wire protocol or GPIO interrupt.
- **Analog sensors** (LDR, MQ-2) output a continuous voltage in the range **0 V to 3.3 V**, which is quantized by the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** of the ESP8266.

### Step 2 — Signal Conditioning (PRE-PROCESS)
- Raw ADC counts are converted to engineering units using the formula in the table below.
- DHT11 returns 8-bit integer and 8-bit decimal values for both temperature and humidity.
- LDR output is mapped to a **0–100 %** brightness index.

### Step 3 — Threshold Decision (THINK)
- Each sensor value is compared against a **configurable threshold**.
- Boolean flags (e.g., `motionDetected`, `gasLeak`, `isDark`, `isHot`) drive the actuator logic.
- This is equivalent to a **fuzzy-rule-based expert system** with hard boundaries.

### Step 4 — Actuation (ACT)
- Relays are driven through a **transistor-based optocoupler isolation** to protect the MCU from back-EMF.
- Buzzers and LEDs are driven through current-limiting resistors.

### Step 5 — Cloud Telemetry (COMMUNICATE)
- Sensor data is published to a cloud broker (Blynk/ThingSpeak/MQTT) at a **sampling interval of 2–5 seconds**.
- The cloud dashboard renders real-time graphs and triggers push notifications.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC to Voltage | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{reading}}{2^{n} - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) | $n = 10$ for ESP8266, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V |
| 2 | Voltage Divider (LDR) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$ | Volts (V) | $R_{fixed} = 10$ kΩ typical |
| 3 | LDR to % Brightness | $\%L = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Inverted for dark detection |
| 4 | DHT11 Temperature | $T = byte_{int} + 0.1 \times byte_{dec}$ | °C | Negative values use MSB sign bit |
| 5 | DHT11 Humidity | $RH = byte_{int} + 0.1 \times byte_{dec}$ | %RH | 0–100% theoretical range |
| 6 | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \dfrac{t_{echo} \times v_{sound}}{2}$ | meters (m) | $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s at **20 °C** |
| 7 | Speed of Sound vs Temp | $v = 331 + 0.6 \times T$ | m/s | Use °C for T |
| 8 | Power Consumption (DC) | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | Quiescent vs Active current |
| 9 | Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_{s} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing requirement |
| 10 | Energy per Day | $E = P \times t$ | Wh | For battery sizing |
| 11 | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{drop}}{R_{coil}}$ | Amperes (A) | Optocoupler saturation check |
| 12 | DHT11 Checksum | $Sum = T_{int} + T_{dec} + RH_{int} + RH_{dec}$ | — | Last 8 bits must equal lower 8 bits of Sum |
| 13 | MQTT QoS Levels | QoS 0 / 1 / 2 | — | 0=At most once, 1=At least once, 2=Exactly once |
| 14 | Blynk Virtual Pin | V0–V255 | — | Each pin = one sensor/actuator channel |

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **ADC to Voltage conversion** is foundational in **any embedded sensor node** — used in industrial process control, biomedical instrumentation (ECG/EEG acquisition), and automotive ECUs.
> - The **ultrasonic distance formula** underpins **parking sensors, robotic obstacle avoidance, liquid level measurement in tanks**, and **drone altimeters**.
> - The **Nyquist sampling theorem** is the bedrock of **digital audio (44.1 kHz for 20 kHz audio)**, **oscilloscopes**, and **software-defined radio**.
> - **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT protocol used in **AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT**, enabling **Tesla fleet telemetry** and **smart agriculture** deployments across millions of devices.

## 2.4 Cloud Communication Protocol Selection

| Protocol | Use Case | Bandwidth | Power |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/REST** | Simple dashboards, ThingSpeak | High | High |
| **MQTT** | Lightweight pub/sub, Blynk | Low | Low |
| **CoAP** | Constrained devices | Very Low | Very Low |
| **WebSocket** | Real-time bidirectional | Medium | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU lab record, **always justify your protocol choice**. For a smart home with 4–5 sensors updating every 2 seconds, **MQTT (QoS 0)** is the optimal choice due to its 2-byte fixed header and publish/subscribe decoupling.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Count to Engineering Unit Conversion

The ESP8266 uses a **10-bit Successive Approximation Register ADC**. The maximum count is therefore $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$. The conversion from raw ADC integer to input voltage is derived from the linear mapping principle.

**Step 1 — Establish the linear mapping endpoints.**
The ADC maps the input voltage range $[0, V_{ref}]$ linearly to the integer range $[0, 2^{n} - 1]$. For ESP8266: $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $n = 10$.

**Step 2 — Write the proportion.**
Since the relationship is linear, the input voltage is proportional to the ADC count:

$$\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} = \dfrac{ADC}{2^{n} - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $V_{in}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \dfrac{ADC}{2^{n} - 1} \times V_{ref} \\
V_{in} &= \dfrac{ADC}{1023} \times 3.3 \quad \text{(Volts)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example.** If the ADC reads `512` from an LDR voltage divider:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \dfrac{512}{1023} \times 3.3 \\
V_{out} &= 0.5005 \times 3.3 \\
V_{out} &= 1.6515 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

This voltage corresponds to $\dfrac{1.6515}{3.3} \times 100 = 50.05\%$ of full scale — i.e., a *medium-bright* ambient condition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Using Time-of-Flight

The HC-SR04 emits a 40 kHz burst and listens for the echo. The echo pin stays HIGH for a duration $t_{echo}$ (in microseconds) that is proportional to the round-trip distance.

**Step 1 — Relate distance, speed, and time.**

$$d_{round\text{-}trip} = v_{sound} \times t_{echo}$$

**Step 2 — Account for round-trip factor.** The sound wave travels **to the object AND back**, so the one-way distance is half:

$$d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \times t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion.** The HC-SR04 datasheet specifies $t_{echo}$ in microseconds ($\mu$s). To keep units consistent, divide $v_{sound}$ (in m/s) by $10^{6}$ to get m/$\mu$s, or convert $t_{echo}$ to seconds. The convenient engineering form used in Arduino/ESP firmware is:

$$d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{echo}}{58}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the constant 58.**
Speed of sound in air at **20 °C** is $v = 343$ m/s $= 0.0343$ cm/$\mu$s.

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{cm} &= \dfrac{v \times t_{\mu s}}{2} \\
d_{cm} &= \dfrac{0.0343 \times t_{\mu s}}{2} \\
d_{cm} &= \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58.31}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding $58.31$ down to $58$ gives the empirical constant widely used in firmware, with a small calibration error of $< 0.6\%$.

## 3.3 Derivation: LDR Voltage Divider & Threshold Inversion

The LDR is a **photoresistor** whose resistance decreases as light intensity increases. It is placed in a voltage divider with a fixed $R_{fixed} = 10$ kΩ resistor.

**Step 1 — Write the voltage divider equation.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

**Step 2 — Extreme case analysis.**
- **Bright light:** $R_{LDR} \to $ small (e.g., 1 kΩ) → $V_{out}$ is small.
- **Darkness:** $R_{LDR} \to $ large (e.g., 1 MΩ) → $V_{out}$ approaches $V_{in}$.

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage for threshold logic.**

$$Light\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}}\right) \times 100$$

The inversion is required because a *high* ADC count corresponds to *darkness*, which is more intuitive when displayed as "Light % = 100% − measured dark fraction".

| Layer | Component | Function | Pin (NodeMCU) | Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sense | DHT11 | Temperature + Humidity | D4 (GPIO2) | Single-wire |
| Sense | LDR | Ambient Light | A0 (ADC0) | Analog |
| Sense | PIR | Motion Detection | D5 (GPIO14) | Digital Interrupt |
| Sense | MQ-2 | Gas/Smoke | A0 (shared via MUX) | Analog |

| Think | ESP8266 | Process & Decide | — | Firmware (C++) |
| Actuate | Relay 1 (Fan) | AC switch | D6 (GPIO12) | GPIO |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Q: Define the three-layer architecture of an IoT system with a suitable example from a smart home deployment. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The three-layer architecture of an IoT system consists of:
1. **Perception Layer (Sense Layer):** Comprises physical sensors and actuators that interact with the environment. *Example:* DHT11 sensor, LDR, PIR sensor deployed in a smart home.
2. **Network Layer (Communication Layer):** Handles data transmission between perception and application layers using protocols like Wi-Fi, MQTT, HTTP. *Example:* ESP8266 publishes data to Blynk cloud over Wi-Fi.
3. **Application Layer (Service Layer):** Provides user-facing services like dashboards, mobile apps, and analytics. *Example:* Blynk mobile app displaying real-time temperature graph and toggle switch for relay.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP protocols. Which is preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HTTP | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe |
| Header Size | Large (variable) | Small (2-byte fixed) |
| Overhead | High | Low |
| Server Load | High (per-client connection) | Low (broker handles fanout) |
| Power Consumption | High | Low |
| Real-time Suitability | Poor | Excellent |

**Preferred Protocol:** **MQTT** is preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices because of its **2-byte fixed header**, **asynchronous pub/sub model**, and **low power footprint** — critical for battery-powered sensor nodes.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Design a smart home system using an ESP8266 NodeMCU that monitors temperature, humidity, ambient light, and gas concentration, and automatically controls a fan, light bulb, and buzzer. Provide the circuit connections, decision logic, and a complete firmware sketch.**

#### Part (a) — System Design, Circuit & Decision Logic (7 Marks)

**Hardware Connections:**

| Sensor/Actuator | NodeMCU Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| DHT11 DATA | D4 (GPIO2) | Temperature + Humidity |
| LDR (voltage divider) | A0 (ADC0) | Ambient light (0–3.3V) |
| MQ-2 Analog Out | A0 (shared MUX) | Gas concentration |
| PIR OUT | D5 (GPIO14) | Motion detection |
| Relay 1 IN | D6 (GPIO12) | Fan control |
| Relay 2 IN | D7 (GPIO13) | Bulb control |
| Buzzer + | D8 (GPIO15) | Audible alarm |

**Decision Logic (Threshold Engine):**

| Condition | Action | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| `temperature > 30 °C` | Turn ON | Relay 1 (Fan) |
| `temperature ≤ 30 °C` | Turn OFF | Relay 1 (Fan) |
| `lightPercent < 20 %` | Turn ON | Relay 2 (Bulb) |
| `lightPercent ≥ 20 %` | Turn OFF | Relay 2 (Bulb) |
| `gasValue > 400` (ADC) | Turn ON | Buzzer + Cloud Alert |
| `motionDetected == true` | Toggle | Bulb (welcome mode) |

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**
```
[Sensors] → [ESP8266 MCU] → [Threshold Engine] → [Relays + Buzzer]
                                  ↓
                          [Blynk Cloud Dashboard]
```

#### Part (b) — Firmware Sketch (7 Marks)

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <BlynkSimpleEsp8266.h>
#include <DHT.h>

// ---------- CONFIG ----------
char ssid[]     = "YourWiFiName";
char auth[]     = "YourBlynkAuthToken";
char mqttUser[] = "YourMqttUser";
char mqttPass[] = "YourMqttPass";

#define DHTPIN    D4
#define DHTTYPE   DHT11
#define PIR_PIN   D5
#define LDR_PIN   A0
#define MQ2_PIN   A0
#define RELAY_FAN D6
#define RELAY_BULB D7
#define BUZZER    D8

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
BlynkTimer timer;

float temperature, humidity;
int lightPercent, gasValue;
bool motionDetected;

void sendSensors() {
    temperature = dht.readTemperature();
    humidity    = dht.readHumidity();

    int ldrRaw  = analogRead(LDR_PIN);
    lightPercent = map(ldrRaw, 0, 1023, 0, 100);

    gasValue = analogRead(MQ2_PIN);

    motionDetected = (digitalRead(PIR_PIN) == HIGH);

    // --- Decision Logic ---
    if (temperature > 30.0) digitalWrite(RELAY_FAN, HIGH);
    else                    digitalWrite(RELAY_FAN, LOW);

    if (lightPercent < 20) digitalWrite(RELAY_BULB, HIGH);
    else                    digitalWrite(RELAY_BULB, LOW);

    if (gasValue > 400) {
        digitalWrite(BUZZER, HIGH);
        Blynk.notify("GAS LEAK DETECTED!");
    } else {
        digitalWrite(BUZZER, LOW);
    }

    // --- Cloud Telemetry ---
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, temperature);
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V1, humidity);
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V2, lightPercent);
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V3, gasValue);
    Blynk.virtualWrite(V4, motionDetected);
}

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(115200);
    pinMode(PIR_PIN, INPUT);
    pinMode(RELAY_FAN, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(RELAY_BULB, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(BUZZER, OUTPUT);

    dht.begin();
    Blynk.begin(auth, ssid, mqttUser, mqttPass);
    timer.setInterval(2000L, sendSensors);
}

void loop() {
    Blynk.run();
    timer.run();
}
```

**Code Walkthrough (model answer explanation):**
1. **Libraries:** `ESP8266WiFi.h` enables Wi-Fi; `BlynkSimpleEsp8266.h` provides the Blynk client; `DHT.h` abstracts the DHT11 protocol.
2. **Pin Definitions:** Use `#define` for clarity and to make pin changes a single-line edit.
3. **`sendSensors()` function:** Runs every 2 seconds via `BlynkTimer`. Reads all sensors, applies threshold logic, drives actuators, and pushes to Blynk virtual pins `V0–V4`.
4. **`setup()`:** Initializes serial, GPIO directions, DHT, and Blynk connection. Registers the periodic callback.
5. **`loop()`:** Two calls only — `Blynk.run()` (handles Blynk protocol) and `timer.run()` (executes scheduled callbacks). No `delay()` is used because Blynk requires a non-blocking main loop.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Explain the Sense-Think-Actuate loop of a smart home IoT system. How does MQTT enable asynchronous communication between the sensor node and the cloud dashboard? Illustrate with a publish-subscribe sequence diagram.**

#### Part (a) — Sense-Think-Actuate Loop and MQTT Architecture (7 Marks)

**Sense-Think-Actuate Loop — Detailed Explanation:**

The smart home IoT system is modelled as a **closed-loop control system** with three functional stages:

**1. Sense (Data Acquisition Stage):**
Physical phenomena are converted into electrical signals by sensors. The DHT11 produces a **single-wire digital stream** with 40 bits (16-bit humidity + 16-bit temperature + 8-bit checksum). The LDR changes resistance, producing an **analog voltage** in a voltage divider. The PIR produces a HIGH/LOW digital signal on motion. The MQ-2 sensor's SnO₂ layer changes resistance in the presence of gas, altering the voltage at the analog output.

**2. Think (Computation & Decision Stage):**
The ESP8266 firmware reads these raw signals, applies calibration (e.g., `map()` for LDR), compares against thresholds stored as constants, and updates boolean flags. This stage is the **"intelligence"** of the IoT system — without it, the sensors would be passive data loggers with no autonomous action.

**3. Actuate (Physical Output Stage):**
Based on the decisions, the firmware drives the relay coils through GPIO pins. The relays' mechanical contacts switch **230 V AC** loads (fans, bulbs). The buzzer produces a **2–5 kHz** audible tone when triggered. Concurrently, the same data is published to the cloud.

**Why this loop is iterative (not one-shot):**
The `loop()` function repeats indefinitely, ensuring the system **continuously monitors** changing conditions. If temperature drops below 30 °C, the fan turns off automatically in the next cycle — this is **closed-loop feedback**.

**MQTT Architecture — Why Pub/Sub is Superior to HTTP:**

HTTP uses a **synchronous request-response** model: the client must initiate every request, the server cannot push unsolicited data, and each connection carries a heavy TCP+HTTP header overhead. For a battery-powered sensor node polling the cloud every 2 seconds, HTTP drains the battery in hours.

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** solves this with a **broker-mediated publish/subscribe model**:
- The **ESP8266 (publisher)** sends a message to a topic (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).
- The **broker** (HiveMQ, CloudAMQ, EMQX) stores and forwards the message.
- Multiple **subscribers** (Blynk app, Node-RED dashboard, phone push service) receive the same message without the publisher knowing them.
- The **QoS (Quality of Service)** level guarantees delivery: QoS 0 (fire and forget), QoS 1 (at least once), QoS 2 (exactly once).

#### Part (b) — Publish-Subscribe Sequence Diagram (7 Marks)

**MQTT Publish-Subscribe Flow:**

```
Step 1:  ESP8266  ---> CONNECT (clientID, keepalive=60s)  --->  HiveMQ Broker
Step 2:  Broker   ---> CONNACK (session present, return code)  --->  ESP8266
Step 3:  Mobile App ---> CONNECT ---> Broker
Step 4:  Mobile App ---> SUBSCRIBE (topic="home/#", QoS=1) ---> Broker
Step 5:  Broker    ---> SUBACK ---> Mobile App
Step 6:  ESP8266   ---> PUBLISH (topic="home/livingroom/temp", payload="28.5", QoS=1) ---> Broker
Step 7:  Broker    ---> PUBLISH (same topic, payload) ---> Mobile App
Step 8:  Mobile App ---> PINGREQ (keep-alive) ---> Broker
Step 9:  Broker    ---> PINGRESP ---> Mobile App
Step 10: Mobile App ---> PUBLISH (topic="home/livingroom/bulb", payload="ON", QoS=1) ---> Broker
Step 11: Broker    ---> PUBLISH (relay command) ---> ESP8266
Step 12: ESP8266   ---> digitalWrite(RELAY_BULB, HIGH)  // Physical actuation
```

**Sequence Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
Publisher            Broker              Subscriber
   |                   |                     |
   |--CONNECT--------->|                     |
   |<--CONNACK---------|                     |
   |                   |<-----CONNECT--------|
   |                   |----CONNACK--------->|
   |--PUBLISH(temperature)-->                |
   |                   |--PUBLISH(temperature)-->|
   |                   |                     |
   |                   |<-----PUBLISH(bulb=ON)-|
   |<--PUBLISH(bulb=ON)-|                     |
   |                   |                     |
   |==digitalWrite(RELAY_BULB, HIGH)==>  [Physical World]
```

**Key Advantages in Smart Home Context:**
1. **Asynchronous:** Mobile app does not need to constantly poll the ESP8266; the broker pushes updates.
2. **Decoupled:** Adding a third subscriber (e.g., a smart speaker) requires zero changes to the publisher firmware.
3. **Low Bandwidth:** 2-byte fixed header makes MQTT ideal for low-rate sensor telemetry.
4. **Last-Will-and-Testament (LWT):** Broker can be configured to publish a "device offline" message if the ESP8266 disconnects unexpectedly — critical for home security monitoring.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Lab Viva & Records:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `Blynk.run()` call inside `loop()`:** Causes Blynk to disconnect after 10 seconds. Always include both `Blynk.run()` and `timer.run()` in the main loop. **−3 marks**
> 2. **Using `delay()` instead of `BlynkTimer`:** Blocks Wi-Fi reconnection and Blynk heartbeat, leading to "device offline" status. **−2 marks**
> 3. **No common ground between relay module and ESP8266:** Causes the relay to "chatter" or not switch at all. Always tie all GND pins together on the breadboard. **−2 marks**
> 4. **Reading MQ-2 without warm-up delay:** MQ-2 sensors need a **20–30 second preheat** for stable readings. Reading immediately gives erratic values. **−1 mark**
> 5. **Missing pull-up resistor on DHT11 DATA line:** The DHT11 requires a **10 kΩ pull-up** between DATA and VCC. Without it, the sensor returns `NaN` values. **−2 marks**
> 6. **Not declaring DHT object as global:** Causes "object not declared in scope" errors. Declare `DHT dht(PIN, TYPE);` at the top of the sketch. **−1 mark**
> 7. **Powering relay from ESP8266 3.3V pin:** Relay coil draws ~70 mA, which exceeds the 3.3V regulator's capacity. Use the **5V (Vin) pin** or an external supply. **−3 marks**
> 8. **No serial debug output:** Examiners love to see `Serial.print()` statements for sensor values — it proves the code is actually running. Add them liberally. **−1 mark**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart Home IoT Project:**

- [ ] **Architecture:** Three layers — **Sense (sensors) → Think (MCU firmware) → Actuate (relays/buzzer/servo)**
- [ ] **Microcontroller:** **ESP8266 NodeMCU** (Wi-Fi enabled, 10-bit ADC, 3.3 V logic, 5 V Vin)
- [ ] **Sensors:** DHT11 (temp+hum), LDR (light), PIR (motion), MQ-2 (gas), HC-SR04 (distance)
- [ ] **Actuators:** Relay module (5V coil, 230V/10A contacts), Buzzer (active type), Servo (SG90, PWM)
- [ ] **Cloud Platforms:** **Blynk** (easiest), **ThingSpeak** (graphing), **MQTT** (industry standard)
- [ ] **Protocols:** HTTP (heavy), MQTT (lightweight, pub/sub), CoAP (constrained)
- [ ] **Key Formulas:**
  - ADC: $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC}{1023} \times 3.3$
  - LDR: $V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + 10k}$
  - Ultrasonic: $d_{cm} = \dfrac{t_{\mu s}}{58}$
  - Nyquist: $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$
- [ ] **Critical Pin Allocations:** DHT→D4, PIR→D5, Relay Fan→D6, Relay Bulb→D7, Buzzer→D8, LDR→A0
- [ ] **Code Structure:** Use `BlynkTimer` instead of `delay()`; always call `Blynk.run()` in `loop()`
- [ ] **Safety:** Common GND everywhere; 5V supply for relay coil; opto-isolation recommended for 230V loads
- [ ] **Viva Questions to Prepare:**
  1. Why ESP8266 over Arduino Uno? *(Answer: built-in Wi-Fi, cheaper, smaller)*
  2. Why MQTT over HTTP? *(Answer: 2-byte header, pub/sub, low power)*
  3. What happens if Wi-Fi disconnects? *(Answer: device operates locally, Blynk reconnects automatically, LWT message published)*
  4. Difference between active and passive buzzer? *(Answer: active has internal oscillator, passive needs PWM frequency)*
  5. What is QoS in MQTT? *(Answer: Quality of Service — 0/1/2 delivery guarantees)*
